Furthermore, we classify studies according to methodology, datasets, and evaluation metrics and determine main trends, strengths, and weaknesses across the literature. Finally, a discussion highlights research gaps and directions for future research that can improve the reliability and interpretability of machine learning-based maze classification and segmentation.

title: Intelligent Watering System for Crop Fields Using IoT-Based Automation and Environmental Sensing
authors: Mohammad Maroof Siddiqui
publish: 2025 International Conference on Innovation and Intelligence for Informatics, Computing, and Technologies (3ICT)
abstract: Optimal use of water is the most critical factor in agricultural productivity, particularly in water-short and erratic-rainfall areas. Conventional irrigation frequently leads to water waste and low crop yield, translate into high operational costs. This paper presents the design and deployment of IWS (Intelligent Watering System) for crop fields using Internet of Things (IoT) technologies and Environmental Sensors. It can sense soil moisture, temperature and humidity and adds intelligence to irrigation process using real-time data analytics. In this system intended to make better use of water and get the highest crop yield out of it, with a goal toward reducing human input as well as time and money. In addition, wireless communication modules are incorporated for remote monitoring and control, and the decision-making algorithms can realize the adaptive response of field status based on changes. The proposed solution is scalable and inexpensive, which can be employed for both small and large farms. Using IoT based platforms and sensor networks, the proposed system has a potential applicability for sustainable farming practices, environmental stress reduction as well as precision agriculture in water stressed areas.

title: Automated Detection of Middle Ear Infections Using Squeeze-and-Excitation ConvNeXt
authors: M. Rethina Kumari, V. Anusuya
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Trends in Computing and Communication (ETCOM)
abstract: Children and adults are prone to ear infections, especially otitis media, which cause serious problems if left untreated. Traditional diagnostic approaches rely mainly on visual examinations, which is subjective and variable amongst practitioners. A computer-aided detection system that uses an effective image processing and Deep Learning (DL) architecture to automate the identification of ear infections has been proposed as a solution to this problem. Hence, this works deploys an enhanced Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) - ConvNeXt model proposed for automated detection of middle ear infections. The proposed approach begins with Bilateral Filter in preprocessing stage to eliminate noise while keeping edge structures that are critical for accurate diagnosis. In order to improve the separation of healthy and sick areas in otoscopic images, cluster pixel regions are then subjected to Gaussian Kernel Mean Shift Segmentation. Boundary Descriptors are retrieved to emphasize the infection zones' form and edge-based characteristics for effective depiction. It provides compact and discriminative shape-based characteristics, are then used to capture the boundary features of segmented regions. These characteristics are then classified using enhanced SE ConvNeXt model, which combines the capabilities of ConvNeXt architecture with SE attention mechanism to improve feature selectivity and diagnostic accuracy. Experimental assessments using Python software show that this framework achieves good accuracy of 99.97%, precision of 96.9%, and recall of 96.9% in detecting middle ear infections. With its ability to diagnose earrelated problems more quickly, accurately, and non-invasively, the proposed approach shows possibilities for inclusion into clinical decision support systems.

title: Quantum Machine Learning for Disease Prediction: A Comparative and Multimodal Study on Early-Stage and Chronic Conditions
authors: Nitika Bahl, Pranjal Singh, Kulvinder Singh
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Trends in Computing and Communication (ETCOM)
abstract: Quantum Machine Learning (QML) is fast becoming a powerful instrument in medical diagnostics because it can process high-dimensional data and small samples efficiently. This paper is a detailed analysis of QML models used for disease prediction, with a relative comparison of Variational Quantum Classifiers (VQC), Quantum Convolutional Neural Networks (QCNN), and Quantum Support Vector Machines (QSVM) in clinical environments. We suggest a new multimodal quantumclassical hybrid approach that analyzes electronic health records (EHR), medical imaging, and biosignal data by means of dedicated QML pipelines with late fusion for ultimate decisionmaking. Recent IEEE literature is integrated in experimental performance glimpses to demonstrate the merits, difficulties, and viability of QML in real-world healthcare applications. The envisioned architecture provides the foundation for future applications of quantum-enhanced diagnostics with existing quantum simulators as well as emerging hardware.

title: Lineage Diffusion Models
authors: Bahman Moraffah, Raha Moraffah
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: Latent variables often depend on directed graph structure. Many existing models impose simple trees or local independence, which limits their ability to represent uncertainty and long-range relationships under noise or missing data. These restrictions reduce the reliability of latent-state inference on general directed acyclic graphs. Lineage Diffusion Models (LDM) define a continuous diffusion process over latent variables placed on the nodes of a known DAG. A graph neural network approximates the reverse-time score so that the model supports structured, uncertainty-aware Bayesian inference on graph-conditioned latent spaces. The method handles multi-parent structure, incomplete observations, and variable graph density. Experiments show that LDM achieves higher inference accuracy and stronger uncertainty calibration than hierarchical Bayesian and graph-VAE baselines.

title: Unpaired Image Translation Using Cycle-Consistent GANs for the Development of a Digital Artwork Restoration System
authors: Adesh Kumar Mishra, S. Goshteeswaran, Aashim Dhawan, S Gowri, Naveen N M, Neha Jaswani
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: The paper suggests an effective strategy of digital art restoration grounded on unpaired image-to-image translation through CycleGANs (Cycle-Consistent Generative Adversarial Networks). The model uses a two-GAN based mapping between the damaged and restored artworks without using paired images dataset. To improve the semantic reconstruction, the generator is brought up with two attention mechanisms, namely, spatial attention and channel attention. Training: new loss functions: smooth L1-based perceptual cycle consistency loss, identity loss, and SSIM loss, using VGG-19 feature embeddings as optimisation target. Two datasets of 1349, 2041 artwork images were experimentally evaluated. The model presented in this paper has gained the best restoration results with MSE of 68.99, SSIM of 0.69 and PSNR of 30.88 on the 'Art images' dataset compared to CycleGAN (MSE: 77.11, SSIM: 0.60, PSNR: 29.54) and Pix2Pix. Also, the model achieved MSE: 104.91, SSIM: 0.54, PSNR: 27.94 on the 'Best artwork of all time information set. Visual and quantitative comparisons affirm that much better details preservation and textures restoration are achieved. The method proposed shall further automated restoration of artwork using context aware learning in GANs.

title: Brain-DGCNN: A Multi-Scale Dynamic Graph Neural Network for Parkinson's Disease Diagnosis
authors: Ruizhe Zhong, Huan Jiang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Big Data and Data Mining (BDDM)
abstract: Early diagnosis of Parkinson's disease (PD) is an important prerequisite so that effective clinical interventions can be provided timely. Although graph neural network (GNN)-based models have recently demonstrated their potential advantage in analyzing resting-state fMRI, existing GNN methods all take the fixed graphs built upon static threshold as input without considering the adaptive nature characteristics of functional connections. In view of this problem, here we present a new Brain fMRI-Based Dynamic Graph Convolutional Network (Brain-DGCNN) framework for predicting whether the subjects belong to PD and HC, respectively. Different from previous studies based on fixed graph structures, our proposed Brain-DGCNN does not need any predefined adjacency matrices. Instead, we use dynamic edge convolution to rebuild topology structures using k-nearest neighbors in feature spaces layer by layer, which could well discover intrinsic long-distance functional connections. Experiments show that our method outperforms the compared baselines with an accuracy of 95.16%, F1-score of 95.15%, and AUC-ROC of 99%. Our result shows that it is beneficial to learn the topology dynamically rather than fix it in advance when predicting PD from fMRI data, providing a new idea for the accurate diagnosis of PD.

title: A Hybrid Approach to Classify Sentences for Instructional Content Identification in Sinhala
authors: S.A.R. Nethmini, Lochandaka Ranathunga
publish: 2025 IEEE 19th International Conference on Industrial and Information Systems (ICIIS)
abstract: This paper presents a hybrid sentence classification framework for instructional content in the Sinhala language. It combines rule-based categorization, using domain-adapted bags of words and linguistic features, with a machine learning approach using sentence embeddings and logistic regression. The system efficiently distinguishes and filters out non-instructional sentences such as introductions, engagement phrases, and tips, while reliably identifying instructional steps. Experimental evaluation demonstrates the effectiveness of this strategy in accurately identifying and filtering instructional sentences in Sinhala text, forming a foundation for future applications in automated content organization and analysis. The approach was evaluated against instructional video-based content and achieved about 95.34% performance accuracy.

title: A Bayesian Multiobject Tracking Approach with Neural-Enhanced Motion and Measurement Models
authors: Shaoxiu Wei, Mingchao Liang, Florian Meyer
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: This paper introduces a hybrid Bayesian multiobject tracking (MOT) method that relies on a statistical model and makes use of neural networks to enhance parts of the model that have been identified as overly simplistic. Contrary to previous work, neural enhancement is performed for both the measurement and the motion model. To ensure tractable computation, our framework uses belief propagation (BP) to avoid high-dimensional operations combined with sequential Monte Carlo methods to perform low-dimensional operations efficiently. The resulting MOT method combines the flexibility and robustness of model-based approaches with the capability to learn from data of neural networks. We evaluate the performance of the proposed method based on the nuScenes autonomous driving dataset and demonstrate that it has state-of-the-art performance.

title: Gaze for Dyads & Blinks for Triads: Task Engagement Predictors in Free-Flow VR Interactions
authors: Carlos Gonzalez Diaz, Georgiana Cristina Dobre, Elisabeth André, Cristina Conati
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and eXtended and Virtual Reality (AIxVR)
abstract: This paper investigates in an exploratory manner small group interactions in Social VR classroom conversations using eye-tracking data (group eye-gaze configurations, blink rate and blink duration) in terms of task engagement. Task engagement has been previously studied in educational settings as it is an important construct for learning. Prior research showed that eye signals in groups can assess rapport, attention, agreement or communication engagement. However, little research focused on whether eye-tracking data can predict task engagement in educational small group Social VR conversations. We conducted an exploratory study with 52 pedagogy university students (8 dyads, 12 triads) engaging in free-flow conversations on a given topic. Results indicated that eye-tracking data could predict task engagement using multiple linear regression analysis. However, group eye-gaze configurations were the significant predictors in dyads, whereas the blink rate was the strongest predictor associated with task engagement in triads. Based on these findings, we discuss the higher complexity in triadic gaze dynamics and why the blink rate was the task engagement predictor. On the other hand, the simplicity of dyadic gaze configurations might as well explain why it was found as a predictor. Furthermore, we argue that future work can explore machine learning detectors of task engagement dependent of group size. The results from this exploratory study help understanding non-verbal group interactions in VR and contribute to further work on engagement models for more socially-abled virtual experiences in Social VR applications.

title: Intelligent AI-Based Service Provisioning and Task Offloading for Fog-Cloud Environment
authors: Swathi Pacha, Meenu, Prem Ranjan, Amit Kumar Dubey, Vishwa Nath Sharma, Ritu Sharma
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: In the era of Internet of Things (IoT) and the cumulative request for real-time services, the integration of Fog and Cloud computing has emerged as a promising solution for efficient resource management and task offloading. This paper presents an intelligent AI-based service provisioning and task offloading framework for Fog-Cloud environments. The proposed model leverages machine learning techniques to dynamically allocate resources, optimize task distribution, and reduce latency, thereby enhancing the overall system performance. By intelligently balancing computational loads between the Fog and Cloud layers, the framework ensures low energy consumption and high scalability while maintaining service quality. The system also incorporates context-aware decision-making mechanisms that adapt to varying workloads and user demands, ensuring efficient use of available resources. Simulation results demonstrate significant improvements in response time, energy efficiency, and resource utilization compared to traditional methods, making this approach highly suitable for delay-sensitive and resource-intensive applications.

title: Coarray DOA Estimation with Sparse Arrays Using Variable Window Size Spatial Smoothing
authors: Wesley S. Leite, Rodrigo C. de Lamare, Yuriy Zakharov, Wei Liu, Martin Haardt
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: In this work, we introduce a variable window size (VWS) spatial smoothing framework that enhances coarray-based direction of arrival (DOA) estimation for sparse linear arrays. By compressing the smoothing aperture, the proposed VWS Coarray MUSIC (VWS-CA-MUSIC) and VWS Coarray root-MUSIC (VWS-CA-rMUSIC) algorithms replace part of the perturbed rank-one outer products in the smoothed coarray data with unperturbed low-rank additional terms, increasing the separation between signal and noise subspaces, while preserving the signal subspace span. We also derive the bounds that guarantees identifiability, by limiting the values that can be assumed by the compression parameter. Simulations with sparse geometries reveal significant performance improvements and complexity savings relative to the fixed-window coarray MUSIC method.

title: Bias-Enhanced Successive Cancellation List Decoder for Polar Codes
authors: Jiajie Li, Sihui Shen, Warren J. Gross
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: Polar codes with the successive cancellation (SC) de-coder are proven to achieve the channel capacity asymptotically. However, polar codes with SC decoding suffer from performance degradation when decoding short-to-medium length polar codes. The successive cancellation list (SCL) decoder improves the decoding performance of the SC decoder when decoding short-to-medium-length polar codes. The SCL decoder faces many implementation challenges, such as the decoding latency and the area overhead. A recently proposed perturbation-enhanced (PE) SCL decoder with a list size of L reaches the error correction performance of the SCL decoder with a list size of 2L. However, the reason behind this improved decoding performance needs to be investigated. An ablation study on the PE SCL decoder is performed in this work, and we found that the bias added to the received soft information is key for improved error correction performance. Hence, we propose to remove the random noise added to the PE SCL decoder, and we call our simplified decoder the bias-enhanced decoder. From experimental results, our bias-enhanced SCL decoder returns similar decoding performance to the PE SCL decoder under various code lengths and rates.

title: An Optimized Multidimensional Cloud Load Migration Method Based on Three-Way Decision
authors: Xinyu Ma, Jianxin Gai
publish: 2025 International Conference on Big Data and Data Mining (BDDM)
abstract: Dynamic changes in cloud load become a significant challenge for cloud resource management today. To address this issue, a three-way decision enhanced strategy for multidimensional cloud load migration control (MLCM-3WD) is proposed in this paper. First, in the virtual machine selection stage, the virtual machine migration control method is combined with the idea of three-way decision. The virtual machine multidimensional resource consumption rate (MRCR) is divided into three parts, and the set of virtual machines with small MRCR is selected. Then, in the VM placement phase, the improved single-step reinforcement learning strategy is used to place the set of VMs with low MRCR onto the physical machine with the highest multidimensional resource balance, completing the VM migration process. Finally, experiments are conducted on the CloudSimPlus platform. The results show that the proposed migration strategy can more effectively reduce energy consumption while controlling the number of virtual machine migrations.

title: DRaGon: Robust Emitter Geolocation via Deep Learning with NTP-Synchronized RTL-SDR Receivers
authors: Zulhaidir Hamid, Elyas Palantei, Intan Sari Areni
publish: 2025 IEEE Asia-Pacific Conference on Geoscience, Electronics and Remote Sensing Technology (AGERS)
abstract: Accurate radio frequency (RF) geolocation is vital for spectrum management, public safety, and defense. The Time Difference of Arrival (TDoA) method is effective but requires sub-nanosecond synchronization, traditionally achievable only with costly GPS-disciplined hardware. In contrast, the Network Time Protocol (NTP) over public networks have millisecond-level jitter, making it not suitable for conventional TDoA. This paper introduces DRaGon (Distributed Radio Geolocation), a deep learning framework that bypasses explicit time-difference estimation by learning an end-to-end mapping from raw in-phase and quadrature (I/Q) samples to geographic coordinates. Using three low-cost RTL-SDR receivers synchronized solely by NTP over cellular networks, we trained and evaluated DRaGon on a real-world dataset of more than $\mathbf{1 9 8, 0 0 0 ~ F M}$ broadcast samples collected in Semarang, Indonesia. Results show that DRaGon achieves a mean absolute localization error of 426 meters, a 63.24% improvement over sophisticated RSSI-based trilateration baselines. These findings challenge the assumption that precise hardware synchronization is indispensable for TDoA and demonstrate that synchronization errors and multipath effects can be treated as part of a learnable $\mathbf{R F}$ fingerprint, enabling affordable and scalable RF geolocation systems.

title: Portable Near-Infrared Non-Invasive Imaging Tool for Early Detection of Oral Potentially Malignant Disorders
authors: R. A. I. A. Rajapaksha, Krishnasamy T., K. A. K. D. J. P. Jayasuriya, Uditha L. Wijewardhana, Umaya Balagalla, Sajith Edirisinghe
publish: 2025 IEEE 19th International Conference on Industrial and Information Systems (ICIIS)
abstract: Oral potentially malignant disorders (OPMDs) are established precursors to oral cancer, and early identification greatly improves treatment outcomes. This study introduces the design, development, and preliminary validation of a portable, low-cost, and non-invasive near-infrared (NIR) imaging device tailored for point-of-care OPMD screening in resource-limited settings. The system combines a 940 nm NIR light source, a compact USB endoscope camera, an ESP32-C3 microcontroller, and a VL53L0X time-of-flight (ToF) sensor to maintain a consistent 2–3 cm imaging distance. Image acquisition is supported by a Python-based Flask/OpenCV pipeline that performs real-time illumination correction (Gaussian blur subtraction), local contrast enhancement using contrast-limited adaptive histogram equalization (CLAHE), adaptive statistical thresholding, and morphological filtering, ultimately generating intuitive heatmaps of potential lesion sites. Bench tests confirmed stable hardware performance, robust distance control, and more than two hours of uninterrupted battery-powered operation. Initial qualitative evaluation by clinicians indicated enhanced lesion visibility in processed images compared to raw captures. These findings demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed device as an affordable and effective screening tool, with future work directed toward large-scale clinical trials and integration of machine learning for automated diagnosis.

title: Personalised and Secured Melanoma Diagnosis Using Meta-Learning Driven Decentralised Framework
authors: Sudha D., R. Naresh
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Trends in Computing and Communication (ETCOM)
abstract: Imperative necessity in the healthcare systems is of compelling need to be addressed for earlier detection and diagnosis, where Melanoma. In this dermis-based cancer, early detection saves the patient from invasive treatments. But the stages of melanoma develop at a higher rate, it becomes the deadliest one as melanoma is the most lethal cancer. The growing Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) helps to collect patient data and sends it to the hospital rapidly. To maintain privacy and data integrity, the decentralised system based on a Federated learning system is an efficient way of communicating data in the network. But it lacks in personalisation and is affected by model poisoning attacks. To overcome such issues, the proposed work uses a meta-learningbased Federated learning model that updates the local patient and hospital client based on the meta gradient updates, making the system more dynamic and reducing attacks as the models' updates change continuously. Based on this, the proposed framework was simulated using the PH2 and ISIC 2020 datasets, yielding a 89% Global AUROC. When the proposed work was poisoned by 30%, an 85% Global AUROC was achieved.

title: On the Death of NAO: Reflections on Robot Platform Choice and Stabilisation of the Human-Robot Interaction Research Field
authors: Katie Winkle, Katherine Harrison

title: Modeling Continuous Weak Temporal Trends for Video-based Micro-Expression Recognition_supp1-3676852.pdf
authors: Qiaoling Han
abstract: As spontaneous facial movements, micro-expressions (MEs) are subtle, uncontrollable, which can more accurately reveal genuine human emotions. Due to the short duration and low intensity of MEs, micro-expression recognition (MER) faces the challenge of modeling facial dynamics over time. Most existing methods focus on discrete key-frame sampling or local temporal modeling, which may disrupt the temporal structure and overlook the continuous and weak nature of ME dynamics. To this end, we propose a video-based MER framework that explicitly models continuous weak temporal trends in ME videos. A multi-timescale feature extraction module is first designed to preserve and capture weak temporal patterns across low-intensity ME frames. These features are then aggregated via hierarchical temporal modeling to progressively encode continuous temporal dependencies without mutual interference. Finally, a multi-head self-attention mechanism models the relationship between spatial and temporal continuous patterns. Compared to state-of-the-art video-based MER methods across three public databases, our method achieves superior performance with UF1/UAR scores of 0.8622/0.8614. Additionally, without spotting apex frames manually, our method attains performance comparable to state-of-theart apex-frame-based MER methods. These results demonstrate that modeling continuous emotion dynamics over time benefits MER.

title: Task-Oriented JSCC with Adaptive Deep Compressed Sensing
authors: Mohammad Amin Jarrahi, Eirina Bourtsoulatze, Vahid Abolghasemi
abstract: In this letter, we propose a Task-oriented Joint Source-Channel Coding framework based on Compressed Sensing (TCS-JSCC), designed to enable end-to-end wireless image transmission for downstream tasks without requiring full image reconstruction. The framework integrates a rate-adaptive CS module with the unique ability to adaptively encode semantically meaningful features informed by both image contents and channel conditions. Specifically, semantic feature representations and signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) are jointly exploited to enable dynamic rate allocation and enhance robustness against channel impairments. A lightweight decoder then processes the received features to perform task-specific inference, such as face detection and image classification, directly on the compressed data. Experimental results demonstrate that TCS-JSCC achieves competitive task accuracy under varying channel conditions, offering a scalable and effective solution for edge-intelligent, bandwidth-constrained applications.

title: Fast and Smooth Trajectory Planning and Control for Vehicle Platoons During Cut-In Maneuvers
authors: Zixuan Li, Wei Zhang, Zeyuan Xu, Antonella Ferrara
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Fast and smooth cut-in maneuvers in vehicle platoons are challenging due to abrupt structural changes that induce reference discontinuities and downstream disturbance propagation. From a theoretical perspective, existing string stability formulations do not explicitly account for reference discontinuities caused by cut-in–induced platoon reordering. To address this gap, a novel definition of local string stability is introduced and formally proven, ensuring local spacing regulation and attenuation of reference-induced disturbances within downstream sub-platoons. Building on this theoretical foundation, this paper proposes a three-stage framework that integrates offline pre-planning, traffic-aware online re-planning, and distributed MPC to accommodate time-varying traffic conditions while preserving local string stability during cut-in maneuvers. A hybrid reference tracking strategy with adaptive weighting is designed to enable fast and smooth trajectory transitions during cut-in maneuvers. Simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method in terms of maneuver execution quality and convergence performance under representative cut-in scenarios.

title: CondFusionSAR: A Bidirectional Multi-Conditional Fusion Network for Generating High-Resolution SAR Images with Complex Backgrounds
authors: Moran Ju, Mulin Li, Tengkai Mao, Sinian Jin
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Currently, data-driven deep learning models advance slowly in synthetic aperture radar (SAR) ship image processing, largely because acquiring SAR images is quite challenging. Though SAR generation models offer a solution, their practical application value is often limited due to inadequate modeling of scene elements, poor controllability of ship positions, and ship offset or loss issues in complex scenes, To tackle the above challenges, we design a bidirectional multi conditional fusion network named CondFusionSAR with the location and class of targets as multi-conditional inputs to controllably generate SAR ship images with complex backgrounds. We decouple SAR synthesis into bidirectional branches, namely a ship synthesis branch and a background synthesis branch. The former adopts anencoder-decoder architecture with the proposed Semantic Injection Module (SIM), which injects mask priors to address the issue of ships target loss in complex scene. The latter employs a mask-based diffusion model enhanced by Mask-Aware Cross-Attention (MaCA) to guide feature generation with the mask, and Multi-Scale Aggregation (MSA) to enhance background modeling capabilities. Moreover, a ship-background fuser with the Spectral Attention Module (SAM) is designed to integrate outputs by applying learnable attention in both spatial and frequency domains, generating complex SAR image. Extensive experiments conducted on improved dataset show that CondFusionSAR can generate high-fidelity SAR ship images. Particularly, with the provided target annotation, the synthesized images can be directly used in other SAR ship tasks, which saves the time required for manual annotation.

title: Multi-Terabit Coherent Free-Space Optical Communication Over a 4.6 km Urban Channel
authors: Vincent van Vliet, Menno van den Hout, Kadir Gümü¸s, Eduward Tangdiongga, Chigo Okonkwo
abstract: We established Reid Photonloop, a permanent 4.6 km testbed for free-space optical (FSO) communications, in Eindhoven, The Netherlands, to characterize the urban FSO channel in detail and validate the implementations of the FSO communication system. In this paper, we present an experimental field demonstration of a coherent wavelength-division multiplexed (WDM) system capable of transmitting data with a multi terabit-per-second net rate over the 4.6 km terrestrial urban FSO link, measured continuously for 12 days. By employing 22 WDM channels with 50-GHz channel spacing and using modulation formats 4-, 8-, and 16-QAM, we measure low density parity-check-decoded data rates of up to 7.73Tb/s. Additionally, we investigate the concurrent channel conditions by analyzing the fiber-coupled optical received power, as well as the impact of rainfall and visibility information collected by a weather station on the FSO data transmission. The 12-day measurements enable a detailed analysis of channel attenuation, scintillation, and outage events under varying weather conditions, revealing the impact on data transmission performance. We further quantify the outage probability of the link and its corresponding availability for different atmospheric conditions and modulation formats, highlighting the trade-off between availability and decoded data rate. The results advance understanding of the FSO communication channel dynamics in urban environments, demonstrate its multi-terabit-per-second potential, and can inform future designs of high-capacity FSO networks in urban areas.

title: Hela: A System Call Restriction Framework for Protecting the Entire Containers Lifecycle
authors: Shaohu Li, Jin Zhou, Xinxin Li, Weizhi Meng, Bei Gong, Jun Hu
abstract: Limiting the number of system calls used by container processes can effectively reduce the kernel attack surface. Existing container system call restriction schemes only focus on the minimum system call set of applications in containers, and lack restrictions on the container runtime runc and other container components that create containers. To solve these problems, this paper proposes Hela, a system call restriction framework that can limit container runtimes and container applications. Hela introduces the Attack Surface Exposure Score (ASES), defined as the dot product of a container's system call usage vector and a risk-weight vector, to quantify exposure. Hela calculates and compares the ASES indicators of various partitioning schemes and selects the best partitioning boundary in the common hook nodes of runc. Hela divides the container creation phase into two phases and generates a minimum set of system calls for each phase. The advantage of Hela is that it combines seccomp with eBPF to achieve accurate parameter checking and efficient system call whitelist switching. Experimental results show that Hela can reduce the kernel attack surface of runc in container runtime compared to traditional schemes. Security experiments prove that our method can mitigate vulnerabilities involving runc and system call parameters.

title: Cheaper Clicks from Boxes: Cyclic Querying for Interactive Small Object Detection
authors: Xiang Yuan, Ruixiang Yao, Junwei Han, Gong Cheng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Interactive object detection paves a promising way to alleviate the annotation burden, particularly for size-limited instances in remote sensing images. While early studies emphasize fortifying object representations through click-based interactions, the alternative strategy of harnessing image as a prior to derive informative clicks remains largely under-explored—despite its substantial potential in advancing interactive understanding. To address this limitation, we devise a CycLIc Querying (CLIQ) paradigm tailored for interactive small object detection in remote sensing imagery. The core lies in an Interactive Semantic Querying framework, where user clicks and visual representations interact in a mutually reinforcing fashion: the click prior first shepherds feature modulation and correlates with a set of class-wise queries to elicit rich semantic responses; the resulting heatmap, in turn, dynamically functions as a spatial activator to highlight small-instance regions. Furthermore, we devise a simple yet effective Informative Click Querying strategy to pinpoint potential click candidates, thereby completing the closed image-to-click interaction loop and ameliorating the performance under few-click scenarios. Thanks to its lightweight cyclic querying design, the proposed CLIQ framework achieves competitive results on the AITOD-R and Tiny-DOTA benchmarks with negligible computational overhead, significantly outperforming pioneering attempts in small object detection. More importantly, the superior performance of our interactive paradigm under low-data regimes underscores its strong potential as a cost-effective and adaptive alternative for data-efficient detection workflows. Code is available at https://github.com/shaunyuan22/CLIQ.

title: Diffusion Bridge Generation of Visible Light to Infrared Images Based on Multi-Scale Enhancement from Aerial Photography Perspective
authors: Xin Wang, Wei Cai, Yao Ding, Xingyu Di, Zhongjie Yin, Haoran Jia
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: To address the challenges of imbalanced multi-scale feature rhythm, network representation defects, and insufficient loss constraints in the generation of infrared images converted from aerial visible light images, a diffusion bridge generation model focusing on multi-scale enhancement is proposed. Starting from the full-link design of multi-scale generation, the model, in terms of diffusion process scheduling, designs a multi-scale scheduling optimized differential equation, adjusts multi-scale features by combining with a gated polynomial enhancement term, and realizes time-varying variance scheduling with a thermodynamic factor, so as to adapt to the logic of first constructing large-scale structures and then refining small-scale thermal details. In terms of network structure enhancement, it builds a time-modulated multi-scale enhancement network, which regulates the intensity of infrared thermal features at each scale according to time steps—ensuring the thermal distribution of large-scale structures in the early stage and improving the authenticity of small-scale details in the later stage; it also proposes a frequency decomposition and fusion module, which decouples high-frequency and low-frequency features through discrete wavelet transform, repairs high-frequency details by virtue of discrete Fourier transform, and realizes dynamic fusion by combining time parameters. In terms of the loss function, it uses high-, medium-, and low-frequency losses to constrain pixel accuracy, and matches LPPIS perceptual loss to optimize semantic consistency, thus constructing an information closed loop. In the comprehensive scene test on the DroneVehicle dataset, the model outperforms 14 mainstream Generative Adversarial Network(GAN) and diffusion models, with core indicators as follows: PSNR(Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio) of 15.06, SSIM(Structural Similarity Index Measure) of 49.78, LPIPS(Learning-Perceived Image Block Similarity) of 30.71, and FID(Frechet Inception Distance) of 43.88; it also shows outstanding performance in sub-scenarios—for single-target scenarios, PSNR is 16.78 and SSIM is 59.48; for dense-target scenarios, FID is 34.67; for mixed-target scenarios, compared with the second-best method, PSNR increases by 0.58% and FID decreases by 15.36%. The model successfully distinguishes cross-category multi-scale features and realizes the generation of multi-scale infrared images in complex aerial scenarios.

title: FEM-Based Quantification of Eddy-Current Losses for MV Cables in Trefoil Formation with Non-Magnetic Screens
authors: Marko Šućurović, Dardan Klimenta, Nikolay Hinov, Dragan Tasić, Mladen Banjanin, Darius Andriukaitis
abstract: Circulating- and eddy-current losses occur in non-magnetic screens and armors of three medium-voltage (MV) single-core cables installed in trefoil formation, where they are bonded and earthed at both ends. According to IEC 60287-1-1, eddy-current losses in non-magnetic screens and armors should be neglected, whereas the technical brochure CIGRÉ TB 880 recommends that these losses should always be taken into account. For the considered screen bonding design, a reliable quantitative separation of circulating- and eddy-current losses is therefore essential. Such a separation cannot be performed accurately while simultaneously using experimental methods alone, but it can be achieved through a combination of analytical modeling and finite element method (FEM)-based electro–magneto–thermal simulations. This paper proposes and applies a new hybrid FEM-based procedure for the quantitative determination of circulating- and eddy-current losses in MV cables. The method is applied to Cu/XLPE/CTS/PVC/AWA/PVC 1/C 19/33 kV (BS 6622) cables with twelve different conductor cross-sections. The accuracy of the proposed procedure is verified by comparisons with IEC-based analytical calculations that account for eddy-current losses in accordance with the technical brochures CIGRÉ TB 272 and CIGRÉ TB 880. The FEM-based electro–magneto–thermal models are calibrated and validated using experimental data already published for PL 1×300 RM 6/10 kV cables. The developed calibration strategy represents another novelty. The results demonstrated that, for the BS 6622 cables with conductor cross-sections ranging from 70 to 1000 mm², the screen and armor eddy-current loss factors lie within the ranges 0.00094–0.03625 and 0.00719–0.1951, respectively. The deviations between the FEM- and IEC-based eddy-current loss factors range from −6.398% to 5.302%, demonstrating a very good agreement.

title: Applicability of DIN ISO 14505-2 in short-term Neutral Thermal Comfort Assessment for Highly Automated Vehicles
authors: Manuel Kipp, Chira Beji, Klaus Bengler
abstract: Highly automated vehicles enable non-driving-related activities (NDRAs) such as working, reading, or listening to music which modify posture and local boundary conditions relevant for thermal comfort. DIN EN ISO 14505-2 provides an equivalent-temperature framework for vehicle comfort assessment but its application has primarily focused on conventional driver postures. This study evaluates the applicability of DIN EN ISO 14505-2 and the Nilsson comfort-zone concept to NDRA-relevant postures under stationary near-neutral conditions. A two-step workflow is applied in which a segmented equivalent-temperature dummy first identifies near-neutral combinations of heated-panel surface temperature and outlet air temperature under controlled radiant and convective conditions in a climatic-chamber vehicle mock-up. The resulting setpoints are then transferred to a subject study with 33 participants performing working, reading, and listening to music for 12 min each. Two counterbalanced sequence groups (1–2–3 vs. 3–2–1) are used to assess order effects under identical boundary conditions. The protocol targets short-term near-neutral exposures and is not designed to establish thermophysiological steady state. Global and local sensation and comfort are collected with standardized scales and a core-temperature proxy is recorded. Across activities, sensation remains between slightly cool and neutral and no significant activity-related comfort differences are observed within the short exposures, while localized effects occur in selected regions. Core-temperature proxy tendencies are small and interpreted within measurement uncertainty. The results support DIN EN ISO 14505-2 as a practical near-neutral target framework for NDRA-relevant postures and motivate longer and more thermally challenging scenarios for future work.

title: UAV Trajectory Planning for Computational Offloading and Resource Allocation Optimizing in UAV-assisted MEC with Unavailable Base Station
authors: Xiao Zeng, Guofeng Yan, Hengliang Tan, Jiao Du, Xia Deng, Heng Wu
abstract: The task offloading and resource allocation strategies aim to provide efficient edge computing services for ground mobile devices in mobile edge computing (MEC) environments. In this paper, we propose a UAV-assisted dynamic deployment and decision-making framework (dDDM) to address the issue of insufficient base station coverage in remote areas or emergency scenarios, which leverages the flexibility and computational capabilities of unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) to supplement terrestrial cellular networks. We focus on optimizing the UAV trajectory to support and enhance the effectiveness of task offloading and resource allocation strategies under conditions of user mobility and stochastic task arrivals. Therefore, a joint optimization problem is formulated with the objective of minimizing the weighted sum of the total latency of ground mobile devices and the overall energy consumption of UAV. To solve this problem, the original optimization problem is decomposed into two subproblems: UAV trajectory planning, and decision-making for task offloading & computing resource allocation. For the UAV trajectory planning subproblem, we propose an improved particle swarm optimization(PSO) algorithm integrated with K-means clustering, i.e., KMPSO algorithm, enabling dynamic UAV deployment in response to user mobility. For the task offloading and resource allocation decision-making, an improved black-winged kite algorithm (IBKA) is developed to determine optimal offloading and allocation strategies. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms existing benchmark algorithms in terms of both latency and energy consumption.

title: A Phase-Noise-Based Method for Calculating Random Jitter Amplification in Clock Channels
authors: Tao Wei, Xiuqin Chu, Yuhao Huang, Haiyue Yuan, Yajun Lv, Jun Wang, Yushan Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: As data transmission rates escalate across successive generations, the impact of timing jitter on the performance of high-speed systems becomes increasingly significant. Moreover, jitter is exacerbated by frequency-dependent channel characteristics as signals propagate through the interconnects. In this paper, an analytical methodology based on phase noise theory is proposed to accurately quantify the random jitter amplification factor in clock signals. To ensure the high accuracy, the proposed method incorporates the higher-order spectral components of the clock waveform rather than relying on sinusoidal approximations. Extensive simulations and empirical measurements conducted on transmission lines and 5th-generation double data rate (DDR5) channels validate the effectiveness and precision of the proposed approach. This methodology provides essential insights into jitter mitigation strategies pertinent to high-speed channel design.

title: Doubly Minimized Petz & Sandwiched Rényi Mutual Information: Properties
authors: Laura Burri
abstract: The doubly minimized Petz Rényi mutual information of order α is defined as the minimization of the Petz divergence of order α of a fixed bipartite quantum state relative to any product state. The doubly minimized sandwiched Rényi mutual information is defined analogously using the sandwiched divergence in place of the Petz divergence. In this work, we establish several properties of these two types of Rényi mutual information. In particular, for the Petz case, we prove additivity for α ∈ [1/2, 2]. For the sandwiched case, we establish a novel duality relation for α ∈ [2/3,∞] via Sion’s minimax theorem, and we subsequently use this duality relation to prove additivity for the same range of α. Previously, additivity for the sandwiched case was known only for α ∈ [1,∞], but it had been conjectured to hold for α ∈ [1/2,∞].

title: Eclipse Refactoring Preconditions
authors: N/A
abstract: None

title: Internet of Medical Things Through Explainable AI at the Edge: A Practical Case Study
authors: Arcangela Rago, Alessio Fascista, Giuseppe Piro, Gennaro Boggia, Luigi Alfredo Grieco
abstract: Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) is emerging as an enabler of trustworthy and human-aligned decision making in next-generation IoT ecosystems. As 6G envisions a highly distributed Global Internet of Things paradigm, embedding explainability of AI models at the network edge will be essential for supporting critical emerging domains such as the Internet of Medical Things (IoMT). In this paper, we explore a practical integration of XAI methods at the edge of 6G IoT architectures and propose novel explainable feature selection and adaptive sample selection strategies that allow low-cost IoT devices to improve data acquisition, on-device processing, and communication efficiency in large-scale deployments. Through a practical case study using an experimental IoMT dataset and comparing different learning models, we show how SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) can be used to interpret patient-related data, improve clinical insight, and significantly boost key performance and value indicators such as learning accuracy, energy consumption, and bandwidth utilization, outperforming traditional dimensionality-reduction techniques.

title: Front Cover
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Antennas and Propagation

title: Self-repair environmentally friendly insulation material based on disulfide bond and DA reaction
authors: Bo Zhu, Bo Yang, Yu Feng, Wenliang Xu, Zhiqing Liang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Cross-linked polyethylene (XLPE) is widely used in high-voltage cable insulation for its good insulation and mechanical properties. However, long-term use leads to damage, breakdown, and environmental pollution due to non-recyclability and high thermal degradation. This study designs an S-S-DA cross-linker with reversible Diels-Alder (DA) and disulfide bonds using isocyanate reactions. Graft cross-linking forms XLPE-S-S-DA, and doping silane-modified SiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> creates XLPE-S-S-DA/SiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> composite for self-repair. Tests show elongation at break increases by 17.89%, tensile yield stress by 17.26%, and breakdown field strength by 34.4% compared to XLPE. It self-repairs mechanical/electrical damages (three mechanical repairs restore strain to 91.26%, yield stress to 82.65%, strength to 92.37%; three breakdowns restore field strength to 76.04%). Three recycling retains 68.89% strain, 71.59% strength (yield stress increases 6.46%), and 79.57% breakdown strength. Lower thermal degradation helps recycling. Dynamic cross-linking and doping create self-repair, recyclable XLPE composites, improving service life and eco-friendliness for high-voltage cable insulation, providing a new design strategy.

title: On the Effectiveness of Radio Environmental Map Prediction Through Image-Based Deep Learning
authors: Mohammad Hossein Zadeh, Marina Barbiroli, Enrico Maria Vitucci, Vittorio Degli-Esposti, Franco Fuschini
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: This paper investigates the use of deep learning techniques for predicting 2D radio environment maps in urban microcellular scenarios. Starting from the common framework established by prior works, a comprehensive and systematic analysis is conducted to evaluate the impact of multiple factors—including neural network architecture, auxiliary input data, data augmentation strategies, and training dataset fidelity—on the prediction performance. A modified U-Net architecture, referred to as DeepUNet, is proposed, incorporating hybrid loss functions and deep supervision to enhance learning effectiveness. Extensive experiments are carried out by training the model via synthetically generated data using standard ray tracing, as well as multiple types of auxiliary data, including empirical path loss predictions, line-of-sight predictions, and sparsified or interpolated radio environment maps. Results demonstrate that, by properly combining the aforementioned techniques, the proposed deep learning model achieves high prediction accuracy, even in challenging configurations. Furthermore, generalization capabilities are evaluated by training and testing on different ray tracing datasets. A detailed performance analysis is provided to offer practical insights and design guidelines for future radio environment map prediction tasks.

title: Movable Antenna-Enabled MIMO Integrated Sensing and Communication: A Unified Mutual Information Framework
authors: Guangyi Chen, Ruoyu Zhang, Xinrong Guan, Qingqing Wu, Boyu Ning, Yu Zhang, Wen Wu, Rui Zhang
abstract: Movable antenna (MA)-enabled multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems offer a promising enhancement for integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) applications. Unlike conventional MIMO systems with fixed-position antenna (FPA) arrays, MAs can flexibly adjust their positions within a given region, enabling reconfiguration of both communication and sensing channels with additional spatial degrees of freedom. In this paper, we propose a unified mutual information (MI) framework for MA-enabled MIMO ISAC systems, where MI characterizes communication performance as reliably conveyable information and sensing performance as extractable target information in cluttered environments. We formulate an optimization problem to maximize the weighted sum of communication and sensing MI by jointly optimizing the transmit beamforming matrix under a transmit power constraint and the MA positions under practical constraints, with a weighting coefficient characterizing their trade-off. To tackle the non-convexity arising from the log-det objective, position constraints, and the nonlinear coupling between optimization variables, we develop an alternating optimization-based algorithm that iteratively updates the transmit beamforming matrix and the MA positions. Specifically, with the fixed MA positions, we optimize the beamforming by approximating the objective function using weighted mean square error and majorization-minimization methods, yielding a closed-form solution. Moreover, with fixed beamforming, the MA positions are sequentially refined by decomposing the position optimization into simpler subproblems, resulting in an efficient suboptimal solution. Numerical results show that the unified MI framework with MAs significantly outperforms conventional FPA systems in both communication and sensing. Channel amplitude heatmap visualizations further illustrate how MA positioning strategies exploit spatial flexibility in array geometry to enhance overall system performance.

title: Safe-DRL-Based Resource Allocation for Traffic Matching Between Transmission and Computation in QoS-Guaranteed MEC-IoT Networks
authors: Guang Chen, Yueyun Chen, Zhen Wang, Liping Du
abstract: In internet of things (IoT) networks, resource-constrained devices can offload data to mobile edge computing (MEC) servers for processing. Nevertheless, offloading incurs additional energy consumption, making energy-efficient offloading a critical issue. Furthermore, the mismatch of transmission and computation traffic rates further exacerbates energy inefficiency. Additionally, ensuring quality of service (QoS) requires not only meeting the per-slot processed data volume thresholds of devices but also satisfying their long-term energy consumption limits imposed by limited battery capacity. The coexistence of these heterogeneous constraints renders algorithm design more challenging. In this paper, we propose a safe deep reinforcement learning (Safe-DRL)-based resource allocation strategy for traffic matching of transmission and computation in QoS-guaranteed MEC-IoT networks, aiming to maximize energy efficiency. Specifically, we formulate an energy efficiency maximization problem that captures the interdependence among transmission, buffering, and computation traffic, subject to both data processing and energy consumption constraints. To reduce complexity, closed-form solutions for a subset of variables are derived via mathematical analysis. Subsequently, we develop a Safe-DRL algorithm, termed action projection augmented Lagrangian soft actor-critic (APAL-SAC), which integrates a penalty-based action projection mechanism to enforce per-slot constraints and a Lagrangian dual method to ensure long-term constraint satisfaction. Simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of APAL-SAC.

title: Chatter Forecast for Cold Tandem Rolling Mill Based on GRU
authors: Chi Lin, Weimin Yin, Yanbo Hu, Jiayi Zhang, Yanling Wang, Fei Xiang, Hui Yu, Xiaomin Zhou
publish: 2025 International Conference on Big Data and Data Mining (BDDM)
abstract: Chatter is a common and long-standing issue in the cold tandem rolling process. Due to its stochastic and nonlinear nature, chatter is difficult to predict accurately. It often leads to severe quality degradation and even poses potential safety risks. Furthermore, establishing an explicit physical model to suppress chatter remains challenging because of the complex and coupled dynamic mechanisms involved. With the widespread deployment of industrial sensors and the rapid development of artificial intelligence, data-driven approaches based on industrial big data have emerged as a promising solution to this problem. In this study, a chatter prediction model based on the Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) network is proposed. The performance of the proposed model is compared with conventional Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) models under identical experimental conditions. The results demonstrate that the GRU model achieves the highest prediction accuracy among the three, with a mean squared error (MSE) of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.7 \times 10^{-5}$</tex> and a coefficient of determination <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(R^{2}=0.99915\right)$</tex>. These findings confirm that the GRU-based approach effectively captures the temporal dynamics of the rolling process and provides a reliable framework for chatter prediction in industrial applications.

title: Prediction of Individual Taste Preferences from Climatic, Cultural and Lifestyle Features using Machine Learning
authors: Patatri Acharya, Sudipta Acharya
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Recent Trends in Electrical, Electronics and Computing Technologies (ICRTEECT)
abstract: Prediction of human taste preferences is a complex classification task decided by a variety of climatic, cultural, lifestyle and other factors. This work uses supervised machine learning to model individual taste preferences - sweet, salty, sour, and spicy - based on lifestyle, climatic and geo-cultural determinants. A dataset with 10,000 observations was obtained from the Kaggle website, having features like age, sleep cycle, exercise habits, climate zone, and historical cuisine exposure. Data preprocessing was done using imputation for handling missing values, Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) for class balancing, manual encoding for handling categorical values and feature normalization. Twelve classification models were evaluated: Logistic Regression, K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Support Vector Machine (SVM), Naïve Bayes, Decision Tree, Random Forest, XGBoost, AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting, CatBoost, LightGBM, and a multi-layer perceptron (MLP). Experiments were conducted using both 80:20 and 70:30 train–test splits. The MLP model achieved the highest performance, giving an accuracy of 89.57% with a 70:30 split and an accuracy of 88.10% with a 80:20 split. The logistic regression model achieved the lowest performance, giving an accuracy of 51.07% with a 70:30 split and an accuracy of 50.45% with 80:20 split. These results demonstrate the potential of ANN-based frameworks in capturing complex sensory preferences, establishing a foundation for personalized nutrition and targeted food product development using climatic, cultural and lifestyle data.

title: Detection and Parameter Estimation of Pulsed LFM Radar for Opportunistic Resource Sharing in Radar-Cellular Coexistence
authors: Michel Kulhandjian, Hovannes Kulhandjian, Debashri Roy, Michael Rahaim
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: This paper presents an integrated framework for radar detection and parameter estimation tailored for the coexistence of pulsed linear frequency-modulated (LFM) radar and 5G communication systems. The proposed solution enables next-generation base stations (gNBs) to sense incumbent radar transmissions and dynamically mitigate interference through spectrum adaptation. A sparse radar detector leveraging time–frequency ridge analysis achieves a detection probability of P<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">d</inf> = 90% while maintaining a false alarm probability below P<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">fa</inf> = 5% at SNRs as low as −15,dB. A semi-blind estimator, combining the Haar transform with chirp rate regression, provides accurate radar parameter estimation without prior knowledge of pulse characteristics. Experimental results using captured in-phase and quadrature (IQ) data confirm that the proposed approach supports radar–cellular coexistence with less than 5% throughput degradation. This work demonstrates that low-complexity sensing and estimation modules can be embedded in 5G gNBs to enable real-time integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) capabilities in shared spectrum environments.

title: Semantic Communication Architectures Using Transformers for 6G Systems
authors: Karrar Imran Ogaili, Hasan Abdulameer Hasan, Ali Z.K. Matloob
publish: 2025 International Conference on Innovation and Intelligence for Informatics, Computing, and Technologies (3ICT)
abstract: This work presents a CSI-adaptive transformerbased semantic communication framework for 6G networks aimed at maintaining high semantic fidelity and task accuracy under diverse channel conditions. The framework combines semantic feature extraction, transformer-based contextual encoding, channel state information-aware symbol mapping, and device-edge collaborative processing, optimized through a joint loss function balancing task accuracy, semantic similarity, and channel efficiency. Experiments on text, image, speech, and multimodal datasets under Rayleigh and Rician fading demonstrated superior precision, recall, F1-score, and accuracy, with the lowest Mean Squared Error (MSE) and Root Mean Squared Errors (RMSE). The system sustained robust performance in lowSNR conditions while reducing latency and energy consumption, making it suitable for mission-critical and resource-constrained 6G applications.

title: Decentralized Evidence Custody: Leveraging Ethereum and IPFS for Digital Integrity
authors: S. Parvathi, I Umamaheshwar Rao, Kummari Venkatesh, Yacharam Uma, GiriBabu K, Vaskula Srikanth
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Recent Trends in Electrical, Electronics and Computing Technologies (ICRTEECT)
abstract: Information is crucial in every area of activity in the contemporary digital era. Safe processing and storage of data are required, especially for software handling sensitive information. Because electronic data can be manipulated, it must be protected from unauthorized alterations. Organizations are always exposed to cyberattacks where malicious actors attempt to alter crucial information. Because preservation of the digital evidence as original and its source is still very important in an investigation, such an event is very alarming. Because every data breach case has a likely impact on the legal process, evidence preservation and maintaining watch at every point of existence becomes very vital. The custody of evidence relies on reports passing through several middlemen, i.e., pathology labs, physicians, and police. One healthy method of obtaining security, immutability, and transparency in the process is with the help of blockchain technology. Blockchain makes the transfer of evidence possible and traceable securely without relying on a single central entity by decentralizing data management. In this paper, we suggest an Ethereum blockchain-based system for evidence management with the help of Ethereum. Through use of IPFS in decentralized storage, the system optimizes security through secure storage of evidence and reports with simplicity of access. Additionally, interoperability between agencies without sacrificing security levels guarantees smooth transmission of data. The proposed solution effectively protects against tampering and guarantees evidence as credible in the course of the investigation process due to the high integrity, traceability, and immutability capabilities of blockchain.

title: AI-SEFAS: An AI-Driven Framework for Spectral Efficiency Optimization in Fluid Antenna Systems for 6G Networks
authors: El Miloud Ar-Reyouchi, Asmaa Kharouaa, Ayoub Hadj Sadek, Kamal Ghoumid
publish: 2025 International Conference on Innovation and Intelligence for Informatics, Computing, and Technologies (3ICT)
abstract: This paper introduces AI-SEFAS (Artificial Intelligence-powered Spectral Efficiency Fluid Antenna System), an artificial intelligence-driven framework designed to optimize spectral efficiency (SE) in sixth-generation (6 G) wireless networks utilizing Fluid Antenna Systems (FAS). By integrating Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for fast beamforming weight prediction and Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) for adaptive antenna repositioning, the proposed approach jointly addresses the challenges of dynamic user mobility, channel variations, and multi-user coordination. Unlike conventional methods that treat beamforming and antenna placement as separate problems, AI-SEFAS performs end-to-end optimization to maximize spectral utilization (bps/Hz) while effectively mitigating inter-cell interference in ultra-dense deployments. Extensive simulations under realistic 6 G scenarios demonstrate that AI-SEFAS achieves substantial gains in spectral efficiency, adaptability, and scalability compared to state-of-the-art Multiple-Input Multiple-Output (MIMO) and Reconfigurable Intelligent Surface (RIS)assisted systems. These results highlight the potential of AI-SEFAS as a key enabler for intelligent and flexible next-generation wireless networks.

title: Forecasting Macroeconomic Indicators with Transformer-Based Time-Series Models
authors: Shweta Goyal, Khayrilla Kurbonov, Saidislom Rashidov, Sindor Sapaev, Asadbek Sobirov, Temur Eshchanov
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Recent Trends in Electrical, Electronics and Computing Technologies (ICRTEECT)
abstract: It is important to be able to forecast macroeconomic indicators accurately for economic policy and financial decisions. Most conventional statistical models perform poorly in modeling complex patterns in high-dimensional, non-linear and long-range dependent financial time series. In this work we focus on the use of transformer-based architecture for time-series forecasting of macroeconomic indicators like GDP growth, inflation rates, unemployment etc. Furthermore, fatal combinations among a set of attributes can jeopardize the continued sales within the remaining set of attributes, and the products in such a deadly combination set are removed from further consideration. The transformer model based on the self-attention mechanism can dynamically encode a large-scale number of temporal dependencies and heterogeneous feature relationships, without the restriction of fixed-window observation and implicit order constraints. We fine-tune several transformer-based models, including vanilla Transformer, Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT), and Informer, on publicly available macroeconomic datasets at quarterly and monthly granular resolutions. We conduct rigorous comparisons with autoregressive integrated moving average (ARIMA) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) benchmarks and find that transformer models outperform these baselines in terms of mean absolute errors across the board and lead to more stable forecasts, especially under multi-step and turbulent economic settings. Ablation studies verify the necessity of positional encoding and multimodal attention for modeling economic seasonality and the exogenous shocks. These findings suggest that the transformer-based approach represents a powerful framework for time-series forecasting in economics: it provides not only a state-of-the-art accuracy, but also interpretability via attention weights as well as the flexibility to integrate related information such as policy changes or global events.

title: A Differentiable Digital Twin of Distributed Link Scheduling for Contention-Aware Networking
authors: Zhongyuan Zhao, Yujun Ming, Kevin Chan, Ananthram Swami, Santiago Segarra
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: Many routing and flow optimization problems in wired networks can be solved efficiently using minimum cost flow formulations. However, this approach does not extend to wireless multi-hop networks, where the assumptions of fixed link capacity and linear cost structure collapse due to contention for shared spectrum resources. The key challenge is that the long-term capacity of a wireless link becomes a non-linear function of its network context, including network topology, link quality, and the traffic assigned to neighboring links. In this work, we pursue a new direction of modeling wireless network under randomized medium access control by developing an analytical network digital twin (NDT) that predicts link duty cycles from network context. We generalize randomized contention as finding a Maximal Independent Set (MIS) on the conflict graph using weighted Luby’s algorithm, derive an analytical model of link duty cycles, and introduce an iterative procedure that resolves the circular dependency among duty cycle, link capacity, and contention probability. Our numerical experiments show that the proposed NDT accurately predicts link duty cycles and congestion patterns with up to a 5000× speedup over packet-level simulation, and enables us to optimize link scheduling using gradient descent for reduced congestion and radio footprint.

title: RAG SLIITbot: Sri Lankan Universities Develop AI Tutor Tailored to Unique Teaching Styles
authors: Kithusshand Raveendran, Gowrisha Karuneswaran, Vasavan Parthipan, Kanagasabai Thiruthanigesan, Uthpala Samarakoon, Nuwan Kodagoda, Roshan G. Ragel
publish: 2025 IEEE 19th International Conference on Industrial and Information Systems (ICIIS)
abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) has enabled personalized learning, but general-purpose chatbots like ChatGPT often lack curriculum alignment. This paper introduces RAG SLIITbot, a specialized AI tutor for Sri Lankan higher education, built on Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to deliver accurate, subject-specific responses grounded solely in lecturer-uploaded materials. The system processes PDF, DOCX, and TXT files through a multimodal pipeline that extracts text and visuals using AI transcription, then generates semantic embeddings via OpenAI models stored in a Pinecone vector database. Leveraging LangChain for orchestration, Pinecone for indexing, and GPT-4 for generation, RAG SLIITbot delivers responses through a Streamlit interface. Evaluated on ten software engineering queries, it achieved 92% accuracy and 90% curriculum specificity, significantly outperforming ChatGPT (70% accuracy, 60% specificity), with differences confirmed by the Wilcoxon signed-rank test (p < 0.01). A rubric-based assessment showed high inter-rater reliability (Cohen’s Kappa k = 0.87). User surveys of 50 students and 10 lecturers reported 88% overall satisfaction, with 92% rating ease of use and 89% curriculum relevance positively. RAG SLIITbot reduces hallucinations, ensures content fidelity, and supports student-centered learning in resource-constrained settings, demonstrating how domain-specific AI tutors can be effectively tailored to local educational needs.

title: Comparative Analysis of Transformer and CNN Architectures for OCT-Based Detection of Circular Leaf Spot Disease in Diospyros kaki
authors: Lakshan Madhushanka Bandara, Ovini Seneviratne, Deshan Kalupahana, Bhagya Nathali Silva, Udaya Wijenayake, Ruchire Eranga Wijesinghe
publish: 2025 IEEE 19th International Conference on Industrial and Information Systems (ICIIS)
abstract: Circular Leaf Spot (CLS) disease caused by Mycosphaerella nawae poses a significant threat to the cultivation of Diospyros kaki (persimmon). Early and accurate identification is essential to reduce yield loss, yet conventional diagnostic methods are often invasive, subjective, or time-consuming. Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT) offers a non-destructive imaging technique capable of revealing detailed leaf structures, allowing the use of deep learning models for automated disease classification. This study investigates the performance of transformer-based architectures, including ViT, Swin, CoaT, ConViT, LeViT, and MobileViT, for the classification of CLS disease OCT images. The same dataset as in previous CNN-based research was used, applying identical preprocessing techniques for fair comparison. Research was conducted using a 5-fold cross-validation, and transformer models were compared with traditional CNNs from the previous research, such as DenseNet121, VGG16, and Xception. The results show that Transformer architectures, particularly the hybrid CoaT model, achieve superior overall accuracy (87.6%) and micro-averaged AUC (0.974), surpassing the best CNN-based models. The findings highlight the strong potential of transformer architectures in the diagnosis of plant diseases using OCT, especially for early-stage detection. This research underscores the suitability of advanced vision models for high-resolution non-destructive classification tasks in agriculture.

title: Directly Processing Raw ECG Signal Classification Using 1D CNN with Noise-Robust
authors: Omar Al-Abdali, Abubaker Elbayoudi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Innovation and Intelligence for Informatics, Computing, and Technologies (3ICT)
abstract: Cardiovascular diseases, particularly arrhythmias, are a leading cause of global mortality, necessitating accurate diagnostic tools. This study addressed the classification of five types of cardiac arrhythmias using a one-dimensional convolutional neural network (1D CNN) that directly processes raw electrocardiogram (ECG) signals, thereby eliminating the need for noise removal and enhancing the model's accuracy and suitability for real clinical environments. Data from the MITDB and INCART databases were combined to create a more diverse and balanced dataset, utilizing the SMOTE-Tomek technique to address class imbalance, particularly for the underrepresented APB class. The model relied on short signal segments (only 121 samples) to reduce computational load while maintaining classification accuracy, achieving an accuracy of up to 96.9 % after applying SMOTE-Tomek, with a notable improvement in the performance of the less balanced classes. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of combining databases and resampling techniques in improving generalization and reducing model bias, making the proposed approach well-suited for real-world applications in diagnosing cardiac arrhythmias.

title: Norm-Q: Effective Compression Method for Hidden Markov Models in Neuro-Symbolic Applications
authors: Hanyuan Gao, Xiaoxuan Yang
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: Hidden Markov models (HMM) are commonly used in generation tasks and have demonstrated strong capabilities in neuro-symbolic applications for the Markov property. These applications leverage the strengths of neural networks and symbolic reasoning to create robust and interpretable AI systems. However, they may inherit and amplify the shortcomings of both approaches. Both components require dense computation and data transfer, and their communication further hinders performance. This paper proposes Norm-Q, a normalized linear quantization approach for compressing probabilistic symbolic models, such as HMMs. We reduce the bit width of the data with minimal impact, thereby alleviating memory and bandwidth stress and enabling deployment on potential custom hardware. Our method introduces a normalized quantization-aware expectation maximization process for probabilistic model training. The experimental results show that Norm-Q achieves a higher compression rate with reasonable score loss compared to traditional quantization methods. In the case of the constrained generation task of large language models, we successfully quantize an HMM of 4096 hidden states to 8 bits without loss and, at most, 3 bits with acceptable loss. Notably, the Norm-Q method can achieve a compression rate of 99% for the weights of the HMM. The code is open source at https://github.com/superstarghy/Norm-Q.

title: Missing Data Imputation Techniques: An Error Rate Evaluation Across Numerical Datasets
authors: Manuka Pinidiyaarchchi, Devaan D Walakumburage, Tilani Gunawardena
publish: 2025 IEEE 19th International Conference on Industrial and Information Systems (ICIIS)
abstract: Missing data is a critical challenge in numerical datasets, affecting the reliability of data analysis in domains such as healthcare, meteorology, and sales. This study evaluates seven imputation techniques: Mean, Median, Mode, K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Autoencoder, Multiple Imputation by Chained Equations (MICE), and Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) across three publicly available datasets with artificially introduced missing values ranging from 10% to 50%. The performance of each technique is assessed using Root Mean Squared Error, Mean Absolute Error, and R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. Results indicate that machine learning based methods, particularly XGBoost and MICE, consistently outperform traditional statistical approaches, maintaining higher accuracy across different domains and missingness levels. These findings provide practical guidance for selecting imputation techniques that ensure data quality and reliable analysis.

title: Real-Time Climatic Suitability Modelling for Ixodes Hexagonus Using Presence-Only Data in Urban UK Environments
authors: Mhd Saeed Sharif, Pardha Saradhi Chakolthi, Samantha Lansdell, Sally Cutler
publish: 2025 International Conference on Innovation and Intelligence for Informatics, Computing, and Technologies (3ICT)
abstract: Here we introduce an interpretable machine learning based real-time ecological prediction system for a medically important, yet understudied tick species, Ixodes hexagonus. Leveraging 187 records of presence from the NBN Atlas of the UK, we trained a presence-only One-Class Support Vector Machine (OC-SVM) model to predict the suitability of real-time climate conditions with hourly observations of temperature, humidity, dew point and precipitation. The workflow has been implemented as a scalable microservice architecture of React, Spring Boot, and FastAPI for web-based visualization and deployment. Evaluation through seasonal simulation, urban spatial tests, climatic threshold validation, and comparison to a spatial niche analysis with hedgehog hosts found that 96.1% of tick presence records were less than 2 km from an observation of hedgehog, suggesting a strongly nidicolous ecology. Despite the fact that model outputs correspond to ecological suitability and not abundance, predicted risk matches constraints imposed by host ecology and seasonality. We thus demonstrate a real-time, public health relevant and ecologically biologically based machine learning prediction system for a medically important host-associated urban vector.

title: Assessing Semantic Consistency of Court Judgments Using Domain-Specific Language Models and Multivariate Statistical Tests
authors: Lohitha Kanisettypalli, Jiya Borikar, Harsda Shrivastava, Priyanka Prabhakar, Peeta Basa Pati
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Trends in Computing and Communication (ETCOM)
abstract: We recognize that guaranteeing the fair enforcement of law across regional courts is critical to judicial consistency and public confidence. In our paper, we present a statistical approach to quantitatively evaluate the structural, linguistic, and sectional uniformity of legal judgments across various High Court jurisdictions in India. We represent legal judgments from civil and criminal domains as high-dimensional vectors using domain-specific transformer embeddings—specifically, InLegalBERT and a fine-tuned Custom-InLawBERT. We then run two strong multivariate statistical tests, ANOSIM (Analysis of Similarities) and PERMANOVA (Permutational Multivariate Analysis of Variance), on these embeddings to test for strong semantic differences between High Court regions (North, South, East, West).
