authors: Konstantin W. Kühlem, Jonathan Ehret, Torsten W. Kuhlen, Andrea Bönsch
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and eXtended and Virtual Reality (AIxVR)
abstract: Interactions with Embodied Conversational Agents (ECAs) are an integral part of many social Virtual Reality (VR) applications, increasing the need for free, context-sensitive conversations characterized by latency-optimized and multimodal ECA responses. Our presented methodology consists of three interdependent steps: We first present a holistic framework driven by a Large Language Model (LLM), which integrates existing technologies into a modular and extendable system that is developer-friendly and suitable for diverse use-cases. Building on this foundation, our second step comprises streaming-based optimizations that effectively reduce measured response latency, thereby facilitating real-time conversations. Finally, we conduct a comparative analysis between our latency optimized LLM-driven ECA and a conventional button-based Wizard-of-Oz (WoZ) system to evaluate performance differences in user engagement. Our insights reveal that users perceive our LLM-driven ECA as significantly more natural, competent, and trustworthy than their WoZ counterparts, despite objective measures indicating slightly higher latency in technical performance. These findings underscore the potential of LLMs to enhance engagement in ECAs within VR environments.

title: IPO-ResNet: Improved Parrot Optimization Algorithm-Based Optimization of Residual Network for Myocardial Infarction Classification
authors: Chang Qu, Xin Zhang, Yansong Lu, Yi Wang, Chengzhi Su
publish: 2025 International Conference on Big Data and Data Mining (BDDM)
abstract: Myocardial infarction (MI) causes permanent cardiac damage, making rapid and accurate diagnosis critical. Electrocardiography (ECG) serves as a key diagnostic tool for MI, and integrating computer-aided diagnosis technology enhances diagnostic efficiency and accuracy. This paper proposes an automated MI classification method based on an improved parrot algorithm (IPO) to optimize residual networks (ResNet). The core approach involves enhancing the parrot optimization algorithm through two strategies: adaptive switching factors and hybrid Cauchy-Gaussian mutation. The improved parrot algorithm is used to find optimal hyperparameter configurations for the ResNet network, thereby constructing a high-performance MI classification model. Experiments on the PTB-XL dataset demonstrate that this method achieves an accuracy of 88.18% and a ROC-AUC of 0.9647, outperforming existing methods and exhibiting high accuracy and practicality in MI classification.

title: Optimizing Waste Classification with Ensemble Learning: Fine-Tuned CNNs for Organic and Recyclable Waste Sorting
authors: Ratnesh Kumar Choudhary, Ansh Devendra Dulewale, Dishant Sevake, Riya Ramteke, Shweta Rewatka, Bharti Mathanka, Prachi Borade
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: Waste management research deals with the urgent need to implement sustainable waste management strategies and considers the effectiveness of ensemble learning and refined CNNs in the classification of waste. This research experiment would be associated with the waste-classificationdata dataset (more than 25,000 images) with the help of which the models are trained and tested according to paramount performance indicators (accuracy, precision, and recall). The optimized transfer learning pre-trained architectures (VGG16, ResNet50, and InceptionV3) achieved test accuracies of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$96.50,96.00$</tex> and 97.00 percent respectively. To improve classification, a majority voting ensemble design was applied that combines predictions of these optimized models and achieves a final test accuracy of 98.00 which is better than the individual model. Transfer learning with ensemble methods has shown to be effective in attaining accurate classification of waste and provides a sorting solution that is both automated and scalable. The findings of this study conclude that carefully trained CNNs are highly resilient to real-life waste management issues and make an important contribution to developing sustainable recycling systems.

title:
F
                    <sup>2</sup>
                    Opt: Novel Fine-Tuning and Folding Algorithms for FPGA-Based DNN Accelerators
authors: Muhammad Shakeel Akram, Bogaraju Sharatchandra Varma, Vincent Meyers, Mehdi Tahoori, Dewar Finlay
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Field-Programmable Logic and Applications (FPL)
abstract: FPGAs, with their parallelism, low power consumption, and reconfigurability, offer an ideal solution for accelerating quantized deep neural networks (qDNNs) on resource-constrained edge devices. They enable enhanced latency, reduced energy consumption, and improved computational efficiency. However, existing frameworks to accelerate qDNN inference on FPGA face challenges in fine-tuning the deployed models on the FPGAs, as expensive re-synthesis and re-mapping of the accelerator is required. Additionally, the folding algorithm for DNN compute engines in these frameworks introduces substantial padding overheads to align with memory widths. This paper introduces a novel evolutionary algorithm-based hardware-in-the-loop (EvoHIL) framework. EvoHIL uses hardware-level weight bit-flip operations to activate neurons and improve accelerator accuracy without requiring DNN re-training and rebuilding the entire accelerator. We also propose a novel algorithmic optimization (Aopt) for folding across DNN accelerator layers. Aopt optimally aligns folding factors with memory width, eliminating excessive padding overheads and improving throughput while reducing memory and resource utilization. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of these solutions. EvoHIL optimization enhanced the accuracy of a binarized convolutional neural network (BCNN) accelerator to nearly 86%. Aopt delivered significant improvements, including up to 96.77 % padding overhead reduction, 33.2 % increased throughput, and 25 % reduced runtime compared to FINN.

title: Efficient Wireless Power Transfer Considering Superdirectivity in Transmit and Receive Arrays
authors: Brian McMinn, Thomas L. Marzetta
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: We investigate wireless power transfer (WPT) systems using jointly optimized superdirective transmit and receive arrays as a possible solution to the numerically large currents of superdirective arrays. Modeling the system as a ported network and applying circuit theory and scalar Green’s function analysis, we propose and demonstrate that the optimal array gain scales approximately as M<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> × N<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, where M and N are the number of elements in the transmit and receive arrays, respectively. Notably, we find that current magnitudes within each array depend only on the local array size and are largely independent of the configuration of the other array. We further show that systems achieving the same array gain with paired superdirective arrays (e.g., M → M) require significantly lower currents than traditional configurations (e.g., M<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> → 1).

title: SmartTicket: Chatbot-Based Ticketing System for Museums
authors: Shanmugam Sundaramurthy, Meheraab Chothia, Arshaan Iqbal, Jothi Kumar C
publish: 2025 International Conference on Innovation and Intelligence for Informatics, Computing, and Technologies (3ICT)
abstract: Conversational AI is becoming a widely used tool in end-user applications. Through advances in technology, it is now capable of interpreting intent and extracting structured information from users, making it ideal for systems such as ticket booking, where accuracy and clarity of information are essential. This paper presents the development of an LLM-powered chatbot designed to handle user interactions concerning booking, updating, or deleting museum tickets, along with general museumrelated queries. Since the application's performance depends on the accuracy of the LLM model being used, a comparison of different models is conducted in this paper. We primarily compare three instruction-tuned variants of Meta's Llama model family: Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct, Llama-3.2-90B-Vision-Instruct, and Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct. Additionally, we compare results from two smaller models in the same family: Llama-3.1-8BInstruct and Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct. This comparison provides insight into how smaller models perform relative to larger models on the same task. Results indicate that the larger models (70 B and 90B) naturally outperform the smaller models, demonstrating higher accuracy in entity extraction. However, in classifying user intent, returning responses in valid JSON format, and identifying missing elements, the smaller models' performance closely matches that of the larger models. Among the larger models, Llama-3.3 narrowly outperforms Llama-3.1 and Llama3.2, despite Llama-3.2 having 90 billion parameters compared to Llama-3.3's 70 billion.

title: Integrating CBC Data with Artificial Intelligence for Early Detection of Dengue Fever
authors: Ashish Reddy Kotla
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Trends in Computing and Communication (ETCOM)
abstract: Dengue fever is a major health issue that affects the world community with early detection being the way forward. The current paper presents the newest framework that integrates complete blood count data and a hybrid artificial intelligence model to identify dengue early in its progression. The system combines a set of algorithms based on the principle of the Random Forest, XG Boost and neural networks, whose work has been optimized with specific focus on features selection. Another unique Query Fragment Caching Algorithm enhances further computing performance. The accuracy of clinical validation (94.99) was high, and the sensitivity (92.35) and specificity (95.78) were higher than the traditional diagnostic methods and machine-learning methods that exist. The results obtained prove that CBC-based AI screening is a cost-efficient, scalable method of early dengue disease detection, which is especially beneficial in cost-limited settings with high disease rates.

title: Deciphering Seasonal Cropping Patterns using Sentinel-1 Time-Series Data and Machine-learning Approach
authors: Swadhina Koley
publish: 2025 IEEE Asia-Pacific Conference on Geoscience, Electronics and Remote Sensing Technology (AGERS)
abstract: This research aims to extract the seasonal cropping patterns using Sentinel-1 time series datasets. The Radar Vegetation Index (RVI) was calculated from the Sentinel1 data and provided as input in a random forest classifier to extract the cropping pattern. A total of $\mathbf{1, 5 0 0}$ samples (divided into 70:30 training and testing data) were used to classify the results. The classifier achieved a training accuracy of 88.81% and a testing accuracy of 74.57%. The classified map indicates that approximately 30% of the total agricultural area is utilized as single-crop lands during the kharif season only. The rabi (mono-cropping) and the zaid (mono-cropping) lands occupy about 2.57% and 2% of the agricultural fields, respectively. About 62% of the croplands are occupied by the multi-cropping lands.

title: MedXAI: A Retrieval-Augmented and Self-Verifying Framework for Knowledge-Guided Medical Image Analysis
authors: Midhat Urooj, Ayan Banerjee, Farhat Shaikh, Kuntal Thakur, Sandeep Gupta
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: Accurate and interpretable image-based diagnosis remains a fundamental challenge in medical AI, particularly under domain shifts and rare-class conditions. Deep learning models often struggle with real-world distribution changes, exhibit bias against infrequent pathologies, and lack the transparency required for deployment in safety-critical clinical environments. We introduce MedXAI (An Explainable Framework for Medical Imaging Classification), a unified expert knowledge based framework that integrates deep vision models with clinician-derived expert knowledge to improve generalization, reduce rareclass bias, and provide human-understandable explanations by localizing the relevant diagnostic features rather than relying on technical post-hoc methods (e.g., Saliency Maps, LIME).We evaluate MedXAI across heterogeneous modalities on two challenging tasks: (i) Seizure Onset Zone localization from resting-state fMRI, and (ii) Diabetic Retinopathy grading. Experiments on ten multicenter datasets show consistent gains, including a 3% improvement in cross-domain generalization and a 10% improvmnet in F1 score of rare class, substantially outperforming strong deep learning baselines. Ablations confirm that the symbolic components act as effective clinical priors and regularizers, improving robustness under distribution shift. MedXAI delivers clinically aligned explanations while achieving superior in-domain and cross-domain performance, particularly for rare diseases in multimodal medical AI.

title: An Intelligent IDS/IPS Framework to Fortify Industry 4.0 Applications
authors: Samir Qurbani, Athirah Mohd Ramly, Pardha Saradhi Chakolthi, Mhd Saeed Sharif
publish: 2025 International Conference on Innovation and Intelligence for Informatics, Computing, and Technologies (3ICT)
abstract: The deployment of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) and Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) devices has been crucial to enable new business processes that convert traditional manufacturing to Smart Factory ecosystems, with higher levels of automation and operational efficiency. This digital transformation, however, also opens additional cybersecurity vulnerabilities to the system, including emerging threat types, such as advanced persistent threats (APTs), zero-day attacks, and insider threats. In this paper, a deep learning-based IDS/IPS based on BiLSTM with an attention mechanism is proposed. The BiLSTM model is trained on the KDDTrain+ dataset and validated on the KDDTest+ dataset, with class balancing addressed using the SMOTE technique. Hyperparameters are optimized using Bayesian Optimization with Keras Tuner. The model achieved 89.2% validation accuracy and a weighted F1-score of 0.91. Performance metrics, including confusion matrices and ROC-AUC scores, suggest effective detection across both common and rare attack classes, with a low false positive rate. Additionally, the system was integrated into a real-time environment using a Flask-based API, showcasing the practical feasibility of the proposed solution for Smart Factory cybersecurity.

title: An Intelligent Captioning System for Deaf Users Using Speech Recognition and NLP
authors: Patan Amrulla Khan, Gayatri Ketepalli, Srikanth Yadav
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Trends in Computing and Communication (ETCOM)
abstract: Effective communication requires real-time access to spoken content, especially for those who are deaf or hard of hearing (DHH). For dynamic, real-time settings like classrooms, meetings, or conferences, conventional solutions like post-event transcription or human interpreters are inadequate. This study suggests an AI-Based Real-Time Captioning System that uses cutting-edge Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) models, such as Mozilla's Deep Speech and OpenAI's Whisper. The system's features include multilingual support, high accuracy, and low latency while transcribing spoken speech into written captions. It is deployable on a variety of platforms, including as computers, tablets, and smartphones, and incorporates a user-centered interface. With the goal of offering a communication solution that is inclusive, scalable, and economical, the article describes the system design, methodology, evaluation findings, and major problems.

title: Transitive Indexing for Main-Memory: Adaptive Performance under Massive Interleaved Queries and Updates
authors: Imran Ali Mohammed
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Trends in Computing and Communication (ETCOM)
abstract: This paper introduces Transitive Indexing, a novel paradigm designed for efficient main-memory indexing in modern applications that demand seamless handling of massive interleaved reads and writes. Unlike traditional static indexing methods, which require high initialization costs and prior workload knowledge, or adaptive indexing methods, which struggle to maintain performance under continuous query and update workloads, transitive indexing evolves dynamically to optimize performance across varying workload stages. We present TRIMMER, a concrete implementation of transitive indexing, which begins as a collection of unordered vectors and incrementally adapts its structure in response to workload patterns. Over time, TRIMMER morphs into a trie for high-access data regions, balancing fast lookups and update absorption without any upfront initialization. Detailed experimental evaluations on synthetic and real-world datasets from the astronomy domain demonstrate that TRIMMER overcomes the limitations of traditional adaptive and static indexing, providing an efficient and resilient solution for interactive data exploration under massive update scenarios.

title: SMAP and Sentinel-2 Based Vegetation Dynamics and Soil Moisture Analysis for Evaluating impact of farm Pond interventions
authors: Veeramallu Satya Sahithi, Suneetha Manne
publish: 2025 IEEE Asia-Pacific Conference on Geoscience, Electronics and Remote Sensing Technology (AGERS)
abstract: Water conservation activities like farm ponds and check dams are crucial for improving the agricultural resilience in drought prone regions. Developmental activities in India like Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act (MGNREGA) and IWMP aim at constructing a wide variety of water conservation activities with focus on improving the soil moisture and crop productivity. Remote sensing data from optical and radar sensors can help in analyzing the impact of these activities on surrounding. The present study reports an integrated analysis using SMAP’s L band soil moisture data and Sentinel-2 optical data-based vegetation indices for evaluating the impact of farm ponds in Prakasam District, Andhra Pradesh. The SMAP’s all-weather, high temporal soil moisture data from 2016 to 2024 complements high-resolution Sentinel-2 imagery to track the surface soil moisture along with vegetation dynamics in a period before and after the construction of farm ponds. Results show a consistent improvement in soil moisture with an increase of $0.02-0.04 \mathrm{~m}^{3} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$ and an increase in NDVI $\mathbf{1 2}-\mathbf{1 8 \%}$ in areas influenced by new pond interventions. Field visits and farmer interactions corroborate the enhanced water retention in the study area. Correlation with annual mean rainfall of the area also indicated strongly that the positive agricultural changes were related to new farm pond construction but not due to rainfall. This study demonstrates the utility of combining microwave and optical data for scalable, seasonal impact analysis of water conservation efforts, particularly where cloud cover limits conventional remote sensing.

title: Adaptive Cyber Defense for IoT: A Real-Time Threat Monitoring Approach
authors: Saswat Ranjan, Balaram Ghosal, Bikromadittya Mondal, Asim Kumar Panda, Himadri Sekhar Mondal, Ranjit Halder
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Recent Trends in Electrical, Electronics and Computing Technologies (ICRTEECT)
abstract: The rapid growth of the Internet of Things (IoT) devices has significantly expanded the attack surface of networks. In contrast, the resource constraints of IoT devices hinder the use of traditional cryptographic methods or continuous patching. To address these challenges, an adaptive cyber defence framework is proposed that combines lightweight authentication with real-time Anomaly detection and automated responses. This framework involves three main parties: the IoT device, the Gateway server, and a centralised Threat-Control Service(TCS). All parties share long- term symmetric keys and participate in a five-step nonce-based challenge-response handshake over an unsecured network. The session key is derived using hash functions, XOR, concatenation, and symmetric encryption techniques. Additionally, TCS collects handshake metadata, such as timing, nonce reuse, and integrity- check failures, to detect anomalies through a one-class machine- learning classifier. When an anomaly is detected, TCS can revoke tokens, devices or initiate firmware updates. Experimental results show that the framework achieves an average handshake latency of 180 ms, with 95% accuracy in anomaly detection, enhancing IoT security with a dynamic, context-aware approach.

title: Hybrid Model ARIMA-Random Forest for Energy Forecasting of Smart Energy Monitoring System
authors: Norith Chealy, Channareth Srun, Sros Nhek, Virbora Ny, Chivon Choeung, Mengseu Pheng
publish: 2025 International Conference on Innovation and Intelligence for Informatics, Computing, and Technologies (3ICT)
abstract: The current contribution is a hybrid model interfacing ARIMA (AutoRegressive Integrated Moving Average) with a Random Forest for energy consumption forecasting with a proposed smart energy monitoring system. The ARIMA is used as a predictor to catch periodic time series data in periods, while the random forest would finalize these periodic patterns in energy consumption trends. The embedding of these models within a smart energy monitoring system creates a chance of improving the forecasting accuracy and enables the facilities consumption behaviors effectively, leading to support in energy optimization as a result. We validate our methodology by applying it to actual household data and find that the performance was significantly better than standalone ARIMA or Random Forest. When the dataset size increases, the hybrid model, as discussed, gives an accuracy (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R^{2}$</tex>) value of 0.98 and the lowest MSE from 0.34 to 0.03. ARIMA yields fairly steady <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R^{2}(0.56-0.61)$</tex> and an increase in MSE being relatively slight (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 4 6 - 0. 4 8}$</tex>). MSE drops from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 4 4}$</tex> to 0.16, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex> improves from 0.68 to 0.90 for Random Forest as sample size increases. This indicates the feasibility of how much this work is an applicable and reliable alternative that enhances the efficiency of energy administration, especially in domestic units.

title: Efficient Fog-Cloud Computing Networks: Task Placement and Binary Offloading Optimization
authors: Shweta Bansal, Pankaj Rahi, D Nagesh, Merajul Islam, Aakanksha Upadhyay, S. Naveen Kumar
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: The increase in low latency and high resource end applications in the global IoT, computing through the fog-cloud network model is becoming the most emerging one. The methodology for solving APTPO and binary process offloading proposed in this paper focuses on fog + cloud solution in cloudcloud computing networks. However, when task characteristics, resource available, and connection band width are integrated into the model, it is possible to minimize computing latency and overall energy utilized. To identify jobs that can be effectively offloaded and hence improve the effectiveness of computing, simple binary decision-making procedure is employed. From the results of all the experiments, it can be determined that the anticipated technique can be used in Internet of Things applications in real world environments which lead to an increased coverage area of the system, longer time consumed in all operations, cost and total overall network throughput as well. The specificity about the technique that has been described can be advantageously applied to a variety of IoT applications that will improve the credential of the network.

title: Optimizing Organizational Performance through Machine Learning Enhanced Management Practices
authors: Ashish Mishra, Nyangam Rime, Somil Vasal, T Aswani, Pankaj Agarwal, Sandeep Tomar
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: The digital sphere is seeing progressive application of machine learning (ML) to change managerial decision-making and operational performance of organizations. The paper discusses the ways to streamline organizational performance by using ML to improve management using predictive analytics, adaptive process automation, and data-driven leadership approaches to management. As opposed to traditional methods, ML makes it possible to conduct real-time analysis of mega-organizational data and contribute to evidence-based decision-making regarding resource allocation and workforce productivity or customer engagement. The study underlines the importance of the intelligent models in locating the performance bottlenecks, business predictions, and the improved collaboration of employees via dynamic task management. Also, an ethical study is examined, this encompasses transparency and fairness under responsible use of AI in the management environment. This paper proved how companies may gain resilience and sustainable competitive advantage even in fast-changing environments by developing an overall framework to enable machine learning skills to support managerial functions.

title: Smart System to Monitor Noise Using Machine Learning
authors: M. Bindu Madhavi, R. Swathika
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Trends in Computing and Communication (ETCOM)
abstract: Urban sound environments present complex challenges for modern city management systems, requiring sophisticated approaches to understand and classify diverse acoustic patterns. This research proposes a comprehensive machine learning framework that combines supervised and unsupervised learning techniques to automatically recognize, classify, and analyze urban sound patterns. The system integrates deep neural networks, ensemble methods, and feature learning algorithms to process real-time acoustic data from multiple urban locations. Our approach utilizes convolutional neural networks for spectral analysis, recurrent networks for temporal pattern detection, and clustering algorithms for automated sound categorization. The methodology incorporates adaptive learning strategies that continuously improve classification accuracy through online learning mechanisms. Experimental validation across multiple urban districts demonstrates significant improvements in sound classification accuracy, achieving 94.7% precision in identifying different acoustic events and 89.3% accuracy in temporal pattern prediction. The system provides real-time processing capabilities with response times under 300 milliseconds, making it suitable for practical deployment in smart city infrastructure.

title: Multi-Task Pulmonary Nodule Detection and Segmentation in CT Scans Using Hybrid 2D-3D Deep Learning with Size-Aware Attention
authors: Harshan, K Kiran, Upasana Singh, Hemant Kumar Yadav, Bhavik Kuchipudi, Jolly Sneh Kapoor
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: The early identification of pulmonary nodules within CT scans serves as a crucial element for enhancing lung cancer patient outcomes. This research introduces an innovative dual deep learning system which combines two-dimensional (2D) and three-dimensional (3D) convolutional neural networks to detect and segment nodules simultaneously. The proposed architecture divides into parallel 2D and 3D feature extraction streams which merge through a size-aware attention mechanism that adjusts weights according to nodule features. The approach uses multi-task learning to optimize classification and segmentation jointly which results in better accuracy alongside improved computational efficiency. Our approach demonstrates superior performance on the LIDC-IDRI dataset by achieving an AUC of 0.92 for malignancy classification and a Dice coefficient of 0.89 for segmentation than all other state-of-the-art 2D, 3D and hybrid models. The obtained results show how adaptive hybrid architectures can be used to create robust systems for clinical lung nodule analysis.

title: Adversarial Learning for Graph-Based Deep Models with a Focus on Crafting Effective Perturbations Against GNNs
authors: Neeta Nathani, G. Durga Sowjanya, Pankaj Agarwal, Gaurav Dheer, Rajesh Kumar Verma, Guddakesh Kr. Chandan
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: Using Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) more often in social networks, recommendation systems and bioinformatics has made it clear that such networks are prone to adversarial attacks. Such vulnerabilities can create major problems such as incorrect information circulating or the breakdown of predictive systems. This research proposes an innovative way to attack GNNs by using both K-Means clustering and Class Activation Mapping (CAM). In contrast to FGSM, IFGSM and Carlini-Wagner (CW) techniques, the approach presented here creates perturbations that are more difficult to notice, while continuing to be highly effective in generating attacks. The methodology transforms each MNIST image into a graph node and uses the pixel patterns to guide how the graph is made. The system reaches great misclassification rates, even though the range of visible defects is minimal. Performance evaluation reveals that the method proposed here lowers classifier accuracy and is more effective when an attack is designed to exploit specific security vulnerabilities in comparison to other approaches. The fact that SVMs can handle more data than Logistic Regression models verifies that how a classifier is built has a strong effect on its vulnerabilities. It provides a useful approach to assessing and improving resistance in GNNs, making a difference in adversarial machine learning and helping to build better defenses in practical uses. The suggested K-Means clustering and CAM approach reliably achieves an overall accuracy of up to 97.5% when applied to various datasets and classifiers.

title: Verifying Closed-Loop Contractivity of Learning-Based Controllers via Partitioning
authors: Alexander Davydov
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: We address the problem of verifying closed-loop contraction in nonlinear control systems whose controller and contraction metric are both parameterized by neural networks. By leveraging interval analysis and interval bound propagation, we derive a tractable and scalable sufficient condition for closed-loop contractivity that reduces to checking that the dominant eigenvalue of a symmetric Metzler matrix is nonpositive. We combine this sufficient condition with a domain partitioning strategy to integrate this sufficient condition into training. The proposed approach is validated on an inverted pendulum system, demonstrating the ability to learn neural network controllers and contraction metrics that provably satisfy the contraction condition.

title: Deep Learning-Based Fraud Detection in Digital Trade and Logistics Systems
authors: Trapty Agarwal, Madhur Grover, Madhusmita Sahu, Varun Ojha, S M Jahirul Islam, M. Saravanan
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: The flood in digital trade and cross-supply chain logistics systems has become a challenge now where the supplying entities require the ability to detect frauds. It is hard to miss security and compliance issues that would be much less likely to occur in rule-based systems, since those cannot adapt to evolving fraud patterns. The proposed application of this study is the use of deep learning in detecting fraudulent activities based on transactional and behavioural logistic network data of high dimensions. The model applies the attention-based LSTM architecture and graph the attention-based LSTM architecture. They capture sequential patterns and relations between objects with the purpose of enhancing the accuracy of detection. With real-time processing, it is feasible to perform dynamic threat assessment and the method of feature embedding shows a superior detection of anomalies of structured and unstructured data. The system has good generalization to the various logistics forms such as the e-commerce, B2B, as well as maritime dealings. It has been confirmed in experimental outcome that better performance is achieved against fraud prediction metric compared to the traditional models in terms of the amount of load and noise present. This will greatly minimize the occurrence of the false positive and enhance early detection of frauds, enhancing confidence in digital logistics. The proposed model of a deep learning solution is scalable, has real-time adaptability and is more than viable as well as future-proof in terms of digital fraud defence logistics operations. The results of the proposed system were better in comparison with those of other methods with the highest ACR of 93.1% and LLR of 88.7%.

title: Artificial Intelligence in the Detection of Knee Joint Injuries: A Comprehensive Review
authors: Baswarani Sandila, Rama Valupadasu, Manjula Sri Rayudu
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Recent Trends in Electrical, Electronics and Computing Technologies (ICRTEECT)
abstract: Knee joint injuries are a leading cause of musculoskeletal disorders, affecting millions worldwide. Traditional diagnostic methods, such as clinical examination and radiographic imaging, often have constraints in terms of performance parameters. Past one-decade, Artificial Intelligence (AI) has shown immense potential in enhancing the performing of diagnosis of knee joint injuries. The Machine Learning (ML) and Deep Learning (DL) models are applied to medical images from modalities viz. X-ray, MRI, and CT scans for the detection of knee disorders. Common knee injuries include knee osteoarthritis (KOA), Anterior Cruciate Ligament (ACL) tears, meniscal injuries, and fractures. This paper reviews the current state of AI applications in knee joint injury detection, AI models used, their advantages, challenges, and prospects. The findings suggest that AI can significantly enhance diagnostic performance, decrease detection time, speedup clinical decision-making, despite challenges related to data quality, interpretability, and generalization.

title: Pinpointing Radio Devices: Robust Fingerprint Localization in Industrial Environments
authors: Anton Schösser, Friedrich Burmeister, Joschua Bogner, Zhongju Li, Philipp Schulz, Gerhard Fettweis, Norman Franchi
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: The massive availability of wireless links will shape the factory of the future. Many envisioned wireless use cases rely on ultra-reliable low latency communication (URLLC). While providing single devices with URLLC is trivial these days, scaling URLLC networks up to many users remains a challenge. Channel-aware radio resource allocation is a method to face this challenge efficiently. However, fine-granular channel awareness is required for channel-aware radio resource allocation. A recently proposed approach to address this requirement is to reuse the channel state information (CSI) stored in a radio environment map (REM). To successfully apply the information from the REM, very accurate localization of the radio device(s) is required, which is the focus of this paper. Based on measurement data and spatially extremely fine-grained fingerprint sequences, it is demonstrated that localizing an automated guided vehicle (AGV) as a radio device is possible with sub-centimeter accuracy on its trajectory. The performance is achieved with the k-nearest neighbor (kNN) approach, and its robustness, even in a time-varying environment, is highlighted. As an enabler for channel-aware resource allocation, the presented work represents a step towards scaling up URLLC networks in industrial use cases.

title: Voicyx: An Intelligent Voice Assistant Driven by the Llama-3.1-Nemotron-70B-Instruct AI Model
authors: Aswathy PB, Kanegonda Ravi Chythanya, Vineeta Singh, Basanta Mahato, Bhavik Kuchipudi, Rohit Patel
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: The rapid evolution of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Natural Language Processing (NLP) has driven the creation of intelligent voice assistants capable of deep contextual understanding and adaptive interaction. Voicyx, an advanced AI-powered voice assistant built on Meta's Llama-3.1 70B-Instruct model and optimized using NVIDIA NIM, marks a significant leap in real-time, context-aware voice communication. This paper provides an in-depth analysis of Voicyx's architecture, core features, applications, challenges, and future prospects. By integrating the Web Speech API for precise speech recognition, Django for robust backend operations, and AI-based text generation for natural, human-like conversations, Voicyx delivers an intuitive and seamless user experience. Its adaptability allows implementation across diverse fields such as personal assistance, education, healthcare, customer support, and smart home automation, establishing Voicyx as a key milestone in the advancement of intelligent, voice-driven human computer interaction systems.

title: Extracting Two-Dimensional Signal Correlation Maps via Gaussian Process Regression with Zernike Means
authors: Sahar Khosravi, Peter Jendrichovsky, Katherine Maximov, Patrick O. Kanold, Behtash Babadi
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: Analyzing the functional connectivity of the brain is an enormous challenge, as deciphering it requires knowledge of both functional responses and circuit structure. A promising strategy involves examining the spatial pattern of stimulus-driven activity correlations across neuronal populations. In the mouse primary auditory cortex (A1), while a global tonotopic organization exists, local frequency preferences in layer 2/3 exhibit a fractured, heterogeneous map. This raises the question of whether functional connectivity is spatially ordered or disordered. To answer this question, we developed an inference methodology based on a spatial Gaussian Processes with Zernike means (GPRZ) to estimate robust and statistically principled 2D SC maps from in vivo two-photon calcium imaging of A1 during presentation of pure tones (PTs) and harmonic two tone pairs (TTs) that are smooth and interpretable. We found that PT-driven signal correlation maps were restricted to local neurons, whereas TT signal correlations showed more global textures. Harmonic stimuli elicited spatially distinct patterns in SC maps, revealing precise functional connectivity between harmonically tuned neurons in TT presentations. Our findings suggest that while neighboring neurons in A1 exhibit diverse frequency preferences, their functional connectivity is precise and selectively aligned with harmonic relationships.

title: An Ai Powered Information Assistant for Government Schemes - Development and Comparative Analysis
authors: Nagesh H R, Ravinarayana B, Prakhyath Rai
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Trends in Computing and Communication (ETCOM)
abstract: An AI-powered information assistant -Abhiyan is developed to streamline access to government schemes, especially for rural users who may lack technical knowledge. Its core purpose is to simplify data retrieval, enhancing information accessibility and bridging the digital divide. Utilizing advanced Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Large Language Models (LLMs), the developed chatbot can understand user queries, including those in regional languages, to provide relevant, userfriendly responses. Abhiyan leverages a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework, enabling it to pull real-time information from structured government notices and documents. By preprocessing and organizing data on various schemes, the system ensures accurate and efficient response delivery, addressing common inquiries like eligibility criteria and application processes. This work integrates LLMs for natural language understanding with RAG for data retrieval, creating a platform that promotes digital inclusion. Abhiyan's scalable infrastructure and ability to offer real-time updates make it a powerful tool for citizens and government agencies alike, empowering users with timely access to essential information on government schemes.

title: Comparative Analysis of Shading Strategies on Solar Heat Gain Through Glass for Energy-Efficient Buildings
authors: Jay Prakash Mathiya, Ankit Goyal, Sashi Kumar Jain, Priyavrat Kumar
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: This study investigates the influence of different shading strategies—no shading, fixed shading, and louver shading—on solar heat gain through glass surfaces. Solar radiation, comprising direct and diffuse components, significantly contributes to indoor heat buildup and cooling loads in buildings. Without shading, daily heat gain values reach approximately 4.4-4.5 kWh, indicating maximum solar penetration. The application of fixed shading reduces this value to about 3.3 kWh, demonstrating its ability to block a portion of incident radiation, though with limited adaptability to variations in solar angle. In contrast, louver shading proves to be the most effective strategy, lowering daily heat gain to around 2.1-2.2 kWh. This reduction, nearly 50% compared to unshaded conditions, highlights the superior performance of louvers in minimizing direct and diffuse solar transmission while maintaining controlled daylight entry. Overall, the results emphasize that dynamic shading systems such as louvers are critical for improving energy efficiency, reducing cooling demand, and enhancing thermal comfort in sustainable building design.

title: Spatiotemporal Analysis of Drought Trends in Key Towns across the Philippines using Machine Learning
authors: Dessa Louise Clemenia, Jane Charmaine Wong, Jazzie R. Jao, Edgar A. Vallar, Ibrahim Hameed
publish: 2025 IEEE Asia-Pacific Conference on Geoscience, Electronics and Remote Sensing Technology (AGERS)
abstract: This study investigates spatiotemporal climate variability and drought dynamics in four Philippine munici-palities-Coron, Kabugao, Virac and Tagum-using a 20 -year record (2003-2023) of key meteorological variables, including Precipitable Water (PW), 2-meter Temperature (T2M), Wind Speed (WS2M), Relative Humidity (RH2M), and Surface Soil Wetness (GWETTOP, GWETPROF). Analysis reveals distinct seasonal patterns, with increasing rainfall during the wet season and declining precipitation in the dry season, alongside a consistent upward trend in surface temperatures. Vegetation decline was detected in Virac, while Coron, Kabugao and Tagum showed relative stability. The study highlights the intensifying impacts of climate change and extreme events such as El Niño on drought frequency and severity, posing significant risks to agriculture and rural livelihoods. Drought characterization was conducted using the Standardized Precipitation-Evapotranspiration Index (SPEI), and predictive modelling was performed using both machine learning (Linear Regression, Support Vector Machine) and deep learning (Long Short-Term Memory, Gated Recurrent Units) approaches. Working within a limited data resource, linear regression, demonstrated best predictive performance, achieving an $\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$ of $\mathbf{7 2. 0 \%}$, RMSE of $\mathbf{0. 4 3 0}$, and MAE of $\mathbf{0. 4 4 0}$ for a stacked dataset with all four locations, having a 6 -months look-back period.

title: Rapid High-Resolution Breast Imaging with Ring-Array Multi-Subarray Synthetic-Aperture Frequency-Domain UltrasoundComputed Tomography Method
authors: Xi Zuo, Hongliang Wang, Xuting Yuan, Dan Gao, Changde He, Yansong Wang, Runze Yang, Rui Ren, Wendong Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Ultrasound computed tomography (USCT) using ring array technology has shown great potential in breast diagnosis. This study addresses the problems of low imaging resolution and long acquisition time in USCT for breast diagnosis by proposing a frequency domain imaging method based on a ring array and multi-subarray synthetic aperture. This method achieves omnidirectional transmission and reception through beam focusing synthesis, enabling tomographic scanning, supporting complete 3D CT reconstruction, and simulating the performance of a large-aperture system with a small array, achieving sub-millimeter-level imaging resolution. By combining a frequency delay-compensated beamforming method and a designed adjustment strategy, effective signal components are filtered from the large amount of data received by the ring array, completing lossless signal conversion between the time and frequency domains, reducing the reconstruction time to less than 6 seconds, a 7.4-fold improvement over traditional time-domain methods and approximately 2.6 times faster than existing clinical breast CT systems. Experiments show that The breast tissue membrane reconstruction demonstrates high fidelity, and Preliminary clinical pilot study and MRI results are trend-level agreement in boundary, and spatial distribution, validating the effectiveness and accuracy of this method in clinical practice.

title: Where-to-Learn: Analytical Policy Gradient Directed Exploration for On-Policy Robotic Reinforcement Learning
authors: Leixin Chang, Xinchen Yao, Ben Liu, Liangjing Yang, Hua Chen
abstract: On-policy reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms have demonstrated great potential in robotic control, where effective exploration is crucial for efficient and high-quality policy learning. However, how to encourage the agent to explore the better trajectories efficiently remains a challenge. Most existing methods incentivize exploration by maximizing the policy entropy or encouraging novel state visiting regardless of the potential state value. We propose a new form of directed exploration that uses analytical policy gradients from a differentiable dynamics model to inject task-aware, physics-guided guidance, thereby steering the agent towards high-reward regions for accelerated and more effective policy learning. We integrate our exploration approach into a widely used on-policy RL algorithm, Proximal Policy Optimization, to test and demonstrate its effectiveness. We conduct extensive benchmark experiments and demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed exploration augmentation method. We further test our approach on a 6-DOF point-foot robot for velocity tracking locomotion, and conduct the simulation test and implement a successful sim-to-real deployment as the ultimate validation.

title: Spectrum-Modulated Frequency-to-Time Domain Signal Conversion for Microwave Imaging
authors: L. Guo, K. Sultan, A. Abbosh
publish: IEEE Journal of Electromagnetics, RF and Microwaves in Medicine and Biology
abstract: Most microwave imaging systems rely on transceivers or vector network analyzers that operate in the frequency domain. Since these systems measure S-parameters over a limited bandwidth, the inverse Fourier transform is often applied with zero padding to produce time-domain signals for radar-based imaging algorithms and machine learning models. However, zero-padding introduces truncation errors that distort waveforms and mask weak reflections, thereby reducing imaging accuracy. To solve this problem, a general spectrum-modulated method is introduced to convert limited-band S-parameters into accurate time-domain signals by incorporating the known transmitted signal spectrum. While a Gaussian-shaped pulse is used in this paper as a representative example, given its widespread use in microwave imaging, the method is applicable to any transmitted signal spectrum. The resulting signals are mathematically equivalent to the cross-correlation of the incident and reflected waves, providing more reliable input for imaging and classification. Medical imaging experiments using a 16-antenna head-imaging system demonstrate significant improvements. The signal-to-clutter ratio increases from 0.5 dB to 4.4 dB, the position error drops from 13.5 mm to 3.6 mm, and stroke classification accuracy improves from 90% to 97% . These findings show that the proposed method offers an effective and practical way to enhance time-domain signal quality in frequency-domain microwave imaging systems.

title: Calibrated Uncertainty Estimation based on Bayesian Models for EEG Epileptic Seizure Detection
authors: Hu Jiahao, Muhammad Mahboob Ur Rahman, Taous-Meriem Laleg-Kirati
abstract: Machine learning-empowered electroencephalogram (EEG)-based Epileptic seizure detection methods often overlook issues such as model uncertainty and model calibration. Model calibration is crucial for compensating for the model uncertainty as it ensures that the predicted probabilities reflect the true likelihood of outcomes. This paper introduces a novel end-to-end EEG-based Epileptic seizure detection framework via calibrated model uncertainty learning. In the proposed framework, we combine Bayesian models and calibration methods (i.e., Platt scaling, Isotonic regression, Beta calibration, Spline scaling, and temperature scaling). Three calibrated Bayesian models are tested and compared: Gaussian Process, Monte-Carlo Dropout, and Bayesian Neural Network. Two datasets have been used to test the performance of the proposed framework: the Bonn dataset and the Temple Lemon dataset. The results show that the proposed model calibration approach improves the model accuracy evaluation metrics such as area under the curve (AUC), precision, and sensitivity, as well as the calibration metrics such as expected calibration error (ECE) score, compared with previous benchmark methods. The obtained AUC, precision, sensitivity, ECE scores are given by 0.918, 0.972, 0.9829, 0.0893 (for Bonn dataset), and 0.997, 0.952, 0.987, 0.0385 (for Temple Lemon dataset), respectively. Thus, the results confirm that, under the proposed framework, the models could effectively provide precise uncertainty estimates of their predictions, as well as are capable of doing robust, calibrated detection of EEG Epileptic seizures.

title: Inter-Agent Beliefs for Learning to Communicate in Large-Scale Multi-Robot Visual Object Search
authors: Jernej Puc, Gašper Škulj, Jan Pleterski, Primož Podržaj, Rok Vrabič
abstract: We present Century Maze,1 a simulated environment for cooperative navigation of groups numbering over 100 robots with primarily visual observations. The underlying task of large-scale object search is designed to push the scalability and efficiency of decentralized multi-robot systems relying on communication to overcome the limitations of separate units. To enable the training of robotic agents in large-scale visual embodied applications such as Century Maze, we propose two reinforcement learning (RL) components that facilitate the emergence of effective communication: Differentiable Inter-Agent Belief Learning (DIABL), which provides a clear learning signal by optimizing the agents' beliefs about their goals and propagating gradients back over a differentiable communication channel to include the appropriate observers, and Informative Event Replay (InfER), which compensates for the scarcity of samples relevant to DIABL by maintaining a dedicated buffer for auxiliary learning. We demonstrate our combined method through an actor-critic neural network architecture encompassing a decentralized multi-agent system of variable size with individual goal-based belief modules. Results on Century Maze show that our method is able to significantly improve performance through cooperation between over 100 agents against comparable baselines and maintains its effectiveness in realistic conditions.

title: Silent Guardians: Independent and Secure Decision Tree Evaluation Without Chatter_supp1-3675596.pdf
authors: Liang Feng Zhang
abstract: As machine learning as a service (MLaaS) gains increasing popularity, it raises two critical challenges: privacy and verifiability. For privacy, clients are reluctant to disclose sensitive private information to access MLaaS, while model providers must safeguard their proprietary models. For verifiability, clients lack reliable mechanisms to ensure that cloud servers execute model inference correctly. Decision trees are widely adopted in MLaaS due to their popularity, interpretability, and broad applicability in domains like medicine and finance. In this context, outsourcing decision tree evaluation (ODTE) enables both clients and model providers to offload their sensitive data and decision tree models to the cloud securely. However, existing ODTE schemes often fail to address both privacy and verifiability simultaneously. To bridge this gap, we propose PVODTE, a novel two-server private and verifiable ODTE protocol that leverages homomorphic secret sharing and a MAC-based verification mechanism. PVODTE eliminates the need for server-to-server communication, enabling independent computation by each cloud server. This “non-interactive” setting addresses the latency and synchronization bottlenecks of prior arts, making it uniquely suitable for wide-area network (WAN) deployments. To our knowledge, PVODTE is the first two-server ODTE protocol that eliminates server-to-server communication. Furthermore, PVODTE achieves security against malicious servers, where servers cannot learn anything about the client's input or the providers' decision tree models, and servers cannot alter the inference result without being detected.

title: A Fast Phaseless Inversion Method With Characteristic Modes for Near-Field Measurements
authors: Zheng He, Kai Huang, Lixiao Wang, Han Lin, Richard Xian-Ke Gao, Qing Huo Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: This paper proposes a fast phaseless inversion method based on characteristic mode analysis (CMA) for nearfield measurement systems. Targeting applications such as antenna characterization and EMC diagnostics, the method reconstructs mode currents on an equivalent surface by combining surface integral equations with characteristic mode solutions. It replaces the basis functions used in traditional techniques, significantly reducing the number of unknowns. Crucially, by utilizing magnitude-only data, this approach eliminates the need for complex phase calibration, thereby enhancing measurement robustness and quality in practical environments. To validate the effectiveness, near-field magnitude data are collected from simulations of a filtering magneto-electric dipole antenna, a dualpolarized dipole filtenna, and a planar inverted-F antenna, as well as measurements of a patch antenna and a patch antenna array. The results show that the proposed method is approximately 100 times faster than traditional methods with a relative data error below 1%, demonstrating its potential for high-efficiency and calibration-free radiation prediction.

title: Error Analysis of the Normalized Sine-Wave Histogram Test Algorithm for ADC Linearity Test
authors: Cheng Ban, Jia Shen, Minshun Wu, Jiangtao Xu, Li Geng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: The sine-wave histogram test (SHT) is a prevalent method for characterizing the static linearity of analog-to-digital converters (ADCs). The normalized SHT (NSHT) algorithm has gained wide adoption in histogram data processing owing to its conceptual simplicity and intuitive extension from the classic ramp histogram test (RHT). However, this paper reveals a fundamental and long-overlooked algorithmic error inherent in the NSHT, which degrades the accuracy of integral nonlinearity (INL) estimation. This error originates from the normalization process, where the measured non-uniform histogram is divided by an ideal sine-wave distribution to estimate code bin widths. This process erroneously assumes a proportional relationship between histogram count and code bin width. This assumption holds only for linear stimuli or ideal ADCs in the SHT. Consequently, estimation errors are introduced in code bin widths, systematically accumulated in code transition levels, and ultimately propagated to differential nonlinearity (DNL) and INL. Critically, since this error does not scale linearly with the ADC intrinsic nonlinearity, it persists even after applying standard static gain and offset correction. To isolate this error, the IEEE standard SHT (SSHT) algorithm and the RHT are employed as reference methods in our comparative analysis. Simulation and experimental results show that although the NSHT yields a DNL estimation error on the order of 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-3</sup> LSB, its accumulated INL error reaches −1.99 LSB, a magnitude that is 1.27 times the reference INL. Therefore, this paper exposes a critical flaw in the NSHT, and calls for a re-evaluation of any normalized histogram-based test that employs nonlinear stimuli.

title: An Underwater Target Tracking Method with Non-Gaussian Measurement Noises based on SGαS Distribution Modeling
authors: Chaofeng Lan, Rui Guo, Huan Chen, Jinghan Wang, Jintao Yang, Shilong Zhao, Yingqi Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: In underwater target tracking, complex environmental interference and sensor anomalies introduce outliers into observations, leading to suboptimal state estimation. To address this challenge, a robust Kalman filter (SGαSSWMRKF) based on sliding window median and Sub-Gaussian α-stable (SGαS) distribution is proposed. The SGαS distribution models measurement noise in underwater tracking, with the Inverse Wishart distribution as the prior for measurement noise covariance. For the lack of a closed-form expression of the SGαS mixture probability density function, an estimator integrating Gamma Series approximation and Gauss-Laguerre Quadrature is developed to adaptively update asymptotic residual variance. Additionally, a median-based adaptive sliding window method with dynamically adjusted size computes the smoothed target state posterior, balancing real-time performance and estimation accuracy. Leveraging the Variational Bayes framework, SGαSSWMRKF suppresses outlier interference by iteratively updating the target state, measurement noise covariance, auxiliary random variables, and degrees of freedom parameters. Simulation results demonstrate that under an unknown non-stationary HMN environment, the SGαSSWMRKF outperforms the SWRKF filter. Specifically, it reduces the Average Root Mean Square Error by 16.74%, 8.14%, and 7.19% in distance, bearing, and velocity, respectively. Consequently, more accurate state estimation is achieved with SGαSSWMRKF. Additionally, experiments on a publicly available real-world dataset demonstrate that SGαSSWMRKF enables more accurate state estimation.

title: RWKV-CD: A Vision-RWKV Framework for Building Change Detection From Remotely Sensed Imagery
authors: Chunyang Liu, Yating Pan, Tianyang Chen, Chao Liu, Shenghua Xu, Bo Mao
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Buildings serve as a key indicator of human land use, and building change detection plays a crucial role in urban growth and shrinkage monitoring, land use planning, and urban development assessment and management. Current mainstream change detection methods mainly rely on Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), Transformer, and their variants. Although CNN excel at extracting local image features, their fixed and limited receptive fields hinder effective capture of long-range dependencies along complex building boundaries. Transformer, on the other hand, leverage self-attention mechanisms to effectively capture global information but suffer from quadratic computational complexity, leading to high demand of computing resources and limiting their practical efficiency. Meanwhile, since the spectra of edge pixels often represent a mixture of building and surrounding surfaces (e.g., roads or vegetation), existing methods typically overlook the variations in spectral similarity between the main body and its edges. To address this challenge, we leverage the Receptance Weighted Key Value (RWKV) architecture for its ability to capture and propagate global dependencies with linear complexity. Building upon this foundation, we propose the RWKV-CD, a novel change detection method that integrates RWKV architecture with specialized feature extraction and noise suppression strategies. RWKV-CD employs a dual-branch encoder separately capture global building information and fine-grained edge details, while a cross-attention module adaptively fuse these features. In addition, a noise removal module combining gating mechanism is introduced to reduce the spectral noise and irregular patterns. Comparative experiments on three publicly available datasets (e.g., LEVIR-CD, WHU-CD, and S2Looking) demonstrate that RWKV-CD significantly outperforms existing methods in terms of F1 score and Intersection over Union (IoU), underscoring the strong potential of the RWKV architecture for CD tasks.

title: CTSNet: Cross-Modal Token Selection Network for Hyperspectral and Multispectral Image Fusion
authors: Yi'ao Jia, Yurong Qian, Jun Long, Yuanxu Wang, Sen Luo, Dongbin Hu, Weijun Gong
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: The fusion of low-resolution hyperspectral images (LR-HSI) and high-spatial-resolution multispectral images (HR-MSI) aims to combine the advantages of both modalities to enhance both spatial and spectral resolution, thereby generating high-spatial-resolution hyperspectral images (HR-HSI). However, existing methods still struggle to balance global-local feature modeling and computational efficiency, and they face a core challenge: spectral distortion during the upsampling due to the lack of cross-modal guidance. To address these issues, this paper proposes a cross-modal token selection network, termed CTSNet. A novel cross-modal guided spatial implicit upsampling pyramid (SIUP) structure is introduced. Unlike conventional implicit neural representation (INR) methods, SIUP directly incorporates MSI features as conditional inputs during the local Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) prediction stage, providing precise spatial priors to the HSI upsampling process. This design enables early-stage and deep fusion of cross-modal information, effectively resolving the problems of spatial detail blurring and spectral distortion during upsampling. Secondly, a token selection Transformer block (TSTB) is proposed to collaboratively extract global-local spatial and spectral features through a parallel dual-branch structure. A token selection attention mechanism (TSAM) is further introduced to significantly reduce computational complexity by employing an adjustable token selection rate. Finally, we design a multi-scale hybrid fusion (MSHF) module to achieve deep feature reconstruction. Experiments on four public hyperspectral datasets demonstrate that CTSNet outperforms current state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods in both qualitative and quantitative evaluations.

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A

title: Randomized Estimation for Low-Light Imaging with Unknown-but-Bounded Noise
authors: Konstantin Amelin, Victoria Erofeeva, Oleg Granichin, Irina Len
abstract: This paper addresses parameter estimation in low-light imaging when the number of observations is limited and the dominant uncertainty is unknown-but-bounded dark noise. We adopt a unified linear model with bounded disturbances and a sparsity prior in a suitable transform domain, and develop two complementary reconstruction schemes. The first scheme is based on randomized per-pixel exposures and a modified sign-perturbed sums (MSPS) procedure. A randomized shutter mechanism is used to symmetrize the effective noise while preserving the underlying pixel intensities, which enables full-resolution estimation together with finite-sample uncertainty regions compatible with bounded-noise specifications. The second scheme relies on compressed sensing with unknown-but-bounded perturbations: a random preconditioning of the measurements converts the bounded disturbance into an effective ℓ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>-bounded term, allowing the use of standard ℓ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sub>-based recovery with non-asymptotic stability guarantees. Numerical experiments on synthetic data and real low-light images demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods in the considered bounded-noise setting and provide improvements over simple averaging and a classical ℓ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>-noise compressed sensing baseline in terms of mean squared error, structural similarity, and peak signal-to-noise ratio under non-Gaussian and spatially structured dark noise.

title: IRET: IRS-Assisted Energy-Delay Incentive-Aware Transmission for Future InterPlanetary Network
authors: Chengcheng Lv, Fei Shen, Feng Yan, Zhiyong Bu, Yanli Xu
abstract: The Earth-Moon InterPlanetary Network (IPN) is a critical infrastructure for future lunar exploration, enabling real-time data transmission between lunar sensors and Earth-based clients. However, its extreme transmission distance, limited onboard resources, and the selfish behavior of heterogeneous participants pose significant challenges in achieving energy-efficient and low-latency communication. To address these issues, in this paper, a real-time Earth-Moon IPN is proposed to transmit lunar data to the Earth for processing. Due to the great distance between the Earth and the Moon, intelligent reflecting surfaces (IRSs) are deployed on the relay satellites (RSs) within the network to enhance signal strength and improve data transmission efficiency. Besides, building the Earth-Moon IPN will require substantial communication and satellite resources. To encourage Earth clients (ECs) and satellites to actively participate in data transmission, an incentive mechanism that allocates rewards based on the volume of the transmitted data is proposed and modeled as a Stackelberg game. Based on the game, the utility optimization problem of energy-delay between satellites and ECs is solved. To improve the Earth-Moon transmission efficiency, an IRS-assisted energy-delay aware transmission (IRET) algorithm is proposed. Intensive simulations show that compared to other IRS-assisted algorithms, the IRET algorithm can reduce transmit power by 0.5 dBW to 3 dBW, shorten transmission delay by 20% to 80%, improve EC utility by 20% to 80%, and enhance satellite utility by 10% to 70%. The results demonstrate that the proposed framework offers a scalable and incentive-compatible solution for future Internet of Space Things (IoST) networks.

title: UGLP: Unifying Global and Local Preferences for Multi-behavior Recommendation
authors: Zhichao Liao, Ke Lu, Jingxi Xie, Jingjing Li, Lei Zhu, Heng Tao Shen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: Multi-behavior recommender systems have demonstrated their effectiveness in mitigating issues such as data sparsity by incorporating auxiliary behaviors into the target behavior. However, existing multi-behavior recommendation approaches typically take one of two directions: (1) fusing behavior-specific preference features from various behavior interaction graphs explicitly or implicitly for recommendation; or (2) utilizing behavior-unified preference features from the unified interaction graph for recommendation or to initialize features for subsequent modeling. These methods fail to exploit the integration of behavior-unified global and behavior-specific local preference features, resulting in incomplete preference modeling. To address this issue, in this work, we propose a novel method called Unifying Global and Local Preferences (UGLP) for multi-behavior recommendation. In UGLP, we design a behavior feature fusion network that consists of global and local fusion modules for comprehensive and fine-grained user preferences. The global fusion module performs graph convolution on behavior-unified global and behavior-specific local interaction graphs to obtain behavior-unified and behavior-specific features. The behavior-unified and behavior-specific features are then fused into globally fused features via a gating network. The local fusion module then performs cross-behavior fusion on these globally fused features via another gating network. We introduce a contrastive learning module to promote preference alignment and knowledge transfer from auxiliary behaviors to the target behavior. Additionally, we incorporate a GCN refinement module to adjust the fused features to ensure that both global and local user preferences are learned. Experimental results on three real-world datasets verify that our method is able to surpass various state-of-the-art models. For instance, our method outperforms the best baseline by an average of 16.43% and 14.36% in terms of HR@10 and NDCG@10, respectively.

title: Practical PID Tuning for Quadcopter Stability: Experimental Results
authors: Aybak Ahmad, Sereen Alzu’bi, Tarek A. Tutunji
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Unmanned Vehicle Systems-Oman (UVS)
abstract: This paper presents practical results for PID tuning of a small-scale quadcopter through a series of flight experiments conducted on the roll, pitch, and yaw axes. Flight data were collected using the Betaflight firmware and Blackbox tool. Then, analyzed with PIDtoolbox and MATLAB to evaluate key performance metrics, including overshoot, settling time, rise time, and steady-state error. The PID parameters were manually tuned and iteratively optimized to achieve the best dynamic response. The experiments were performed on a First Person View (FPV) quadcopter, and the optimized PID gains resulted in noticeable improvements in flight stability and responsiveness. Experimental results confirm that the tuned parameters significantly reduced overshoot and rise time, demonstrating the effectiveness of manual PID tuning in enhancing quadcopter performance.

title: MuLTEcast-Bandit: Lightweight Adaptive Multicast/Unicast Selection for Massive Event Streaming in 5G/6G Edge Networks
authors: Omar Dario Delgado Brito
publish: 2025 International Conference on Innovation and Intelligence for Informatics, Computing, and Technologies (3ICT)
abstract: Unicast adaptive bitrate (UABR) streaming maximizes individual Quality of Experience (QoE) but wastes radio re-sources when thousands request the same content simultaneously. Conversely, static multicast saves spectrum yet degrades QoE because it ignores time-varying context. We present MuLTEcast-Bandit, a lightweight contextual bandit (LinUCB) that runs at the mobile edge and decides, per group and decision epoch, whether to transmit via multicast or unicast and at which bitrate, using features such as group size, channel quality, cell load, and recent stall events. Across massive-event scenarios, MuLTEcast-Bandit consistently approaches the QoE of pure unicast while substantially reducing spectrum consumption, and it clearly out-performs static multicast and threshold-based heuristics without manual tuning. These findings highlight a practical trade-off: near-unicast QoE at a fraction of the resource cost, enabled by a deployable, computation-light policy.

title: Early Prediction of Pregnancy Risk based on Cardiometabolic and Psychological Factors using Machine Learning
authors: Patatri Acharya, Sudipta Acharya
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Recent Trends in Electrical, Electronics and Computing Technologies (ICRTEECT)
abstract: Early detection of high-risk pregnancies is critical for the improvement of maternal and fetal outcomes, especially in resource-constrained environments. In this study, we propose a machine learning-based method for classifying pregnancy risk levels (categorized as high or low) using a multidimensional dataset comprising cardiometabolic and psychological factors. A dataset with 1,205 observations was obtained from the Kaggle website, having features like Age, Systolic BP, Diastolic BP, Blood Sugar, Body Temperature, Previous Complications, Preexisting Diabetes, BMI, Gestational Diabetes, Heart Rate and Mental Health and Heart Rate. Data preprocessing was done using Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) for class balancing, one-hot encoding for handling categorical values and feature normalization. Twelve classification models were evaluated: Logistic Regression, K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Support Vector Machine (SVM), Naïve Bayes, Decision Tree, Random Forest, XGBoost, AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting, CatBoost, LightGBM, and a multi-layer perceptron (MLP). Experiments were conducted using both 80:20 and 70:30 train–test splits. This study takes into consideration mental health data of the pregnant individual along with physical health data and finally introduces a predictive model based on Gradient Boosting giving 99.13% accuracy with 80:20 split for identifying high-risk pregnancies. The proposed solution offers a scalable, interpretable, and clinically relevant tool that can aid healthcare professionals in early risk detection and intervention planning, thus helping to improve maternal care and reduce mortality rates.

title: Peak Delta Frequency Regulates the Timing of Decisions
authors: Logan Peters, Gregory Bales, Vincent Tran, Ariana Moghbel, Zhaodan Kong, Karen Moxon
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: Despite extensive study on how the brain decides, the neurophysiological underpinnings of when a decision is made remain poorly understood. Previous modeling work in our lab using human intracranial local field potentials demonstrated that the time at which a decision was made was time-locked to a particular phase of the participants on-going slow wave activity (delta, .5-4Hz). To evaluate whether the on-going delta was driving the time of decision, we repeated our study but forced participants to make a decision at random times throughout their delta cycle. We hypothesized that if delta was driving the time of decision, the phase of the delta prior to decision would be well time locked to the time of decision. Alternatively, if the decision drove the phase of delta by resetting the phase at the time of choice, the time of decision would be time-locked to the phase of the on-going delta after choice. Because we are interested in this low frequency, we could use surface electroencephalogram (EEG). Results showed that participants consistently made their decision during a specific phase of their peak delta (.5-4hz) cycle, regardless of where in their delta cycle they were prompted to make their decision, consistent with our prior study using intracranial local field potentials. Moreover, time of decision was phase-locked to the on-going delta prior to choice selection. Since it has been shown that information about decision making is widely distributed across the brain, these results suggest that delta oscillations could be used to synchronize information from multiple brain regions.

title: Live Demonstration: Continuous Processing of Event-Data with Graph Convolutional Neural Networks Implemented for SoC FPGA
authors: Piotr Wzorek, Kamil Jeziorek, Marcin Kowalczyk, Krzysztof Blachut, Tomasz Kryjak, Marek Gorgon
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Field-Programmable Logic and Applications (FPL)
abstract: For perception systems in mobile robotics - particularly in demanding, highly dynamic environments - event cameras (DVS - Dynamic Vision Sensors) are being increasingly utilised as an alternative to conventional vision sensors. The real-time processing of the registered spatio-temporal sparse point cloud must satisfy stringent requirements in terms of latency, throughput, and energy efficiency. In this demonstration, we present our method for implementing Graph Convolutional Neural Networks on a heterogeneous SoC FPGA platform, aimed at meeting these constraints. We address the challenges associated with integrating event-based sensors with reconfigurable hardware, as well as the intricate relationship between latency, performance, and hardware resource utilisation.

title: Data as Property: Reimagining Digital Rights under Contemporary Legal Systems
authors: Nalini Bhadwal, Chander Parkash Singh
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Recent Trends in Electrical, Electronics and Computing Technologies (ICRTEECT)
abstract: Digital revolution has also challenged the conventional thinking regarding ownership thereby requiring a re-definition of digital rights in modern judicial systems. The theoretical roots of data property rights are explored in this research in terms of Locke labor theory, the theory of will by Kant and the theory of justice by Rawls. It solves the problem of duality of data, both as legal property and nonrival good, discovering the dark sides of law and jurisdiction issues when it comes to digital property. The concept of the potential of technological solutions like blockchain and differential privacy to help support Promise-Based Ownership models and protect data ownership entities are presented in the paper to protect data privacy. It also examines economic issues in terms of market failure and a conflict between incentives to innovate and safeguard the public good. Such ethical considerations as consent as well as rights of a data-subject are underlined as pivotal in the field of responsible policy-making.
