These features include thermal band, blue band, Normalized Difference Red Edge index (NDREi), Green Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (GNDVI), and the blue-to-green ratio. The Lazy Predict library was utilized to fit 42 regression models and to sort them based on their predictive performance. Four of the best-performing models, namely, Extra Trees, LightGBM, NuSVR, and AdaBoost regressors, were selected. After hyperparameter tuning, they achieved a coefficient of determination (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>) of 0.70,0.68,0.67, and 0.67, respectively. These models were used as base-learners for the ensemble Cubist meta-learner model, which enhanced the R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> score to 0.82. The results show the effectiveness of coupling Lazy Predict with the Cubist model to improve chlorophyll-a prediction accuracy in reservoirs.

title: A Trunk Full of Swords
authors: George V. Neville-Neil
abstract: None

title: FARFLAG: Fully Automatic Radio-Frequency Layout Generation and Its Application to Design of CMOS Millimeter-Wave Low-Noise Amplifiers
authors: Yifan Wang, Jing Gao, Hanyu Liu, Guqiao Chen, Yuzhen Song, Qi Wu, Guangyi Lu, Haiming Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: The design of millimeter-wave (mmWave) integrated circuits (ICs) is traditionally a slow process that requires extensive iterations and computationally intensive full-wave electromagnetic (EM) simulations. This paper presents a fully automatic radio-frequency layout generation (FARFLAG) method for the end-to-end synthesis of mmWave ICs, from device generation to final layout. The proposed method utilizes a mixture of machine-learning-assisted optimization, lookup tables, and fixed parameters for device generation. We introduce a hybrid placement and routing algorithm tailored for mmWave ICs, which is enhanced with a two-restart mechanism to improve placement robustness and exploration. The synthesis is driven by an improved Bayesian optimization approach with constrained multi-acquisition functions to navigate the design space efficiently. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework by synthesizing two mmWave low-noise amplifiers. By integrating high-fidelity EM simulations, the FARFLAG method significantly reduces the search space compared to conventional methods, enabling the rapid synthesis of complex mmWave ICs with superior performance.

title: Intelligent Tree Canopy Segmentation from UAV Imagery to Support Urban and Rural Garden Design
authors: Liping Chen, Yao Chen
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Accurately segmenting the tree canopies from UAV images is a crucial prerequisite for conducting canopy morphology analysis and performing precise pruning in intelligent gardens and orchards. However, canopy segmentation based on unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) remains challenging due to the significant intra-class scale variations among tree canopies and the prominent outdoor lighting differences. These factors often result in blurred canopy boundaries, fragmented segmentation results, and limited robustness. Moreover, most existing methods were initially designed for ground scenes or trained under restricted imaging conditions, lacking effective optimization for multi-scale feature representation and light interference adaptation in aerial agricultural scenarios. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a tree canopy semantic segmentation network named Trans-CNN, which is based on a multi-scale collaborative enhancement framework. Firstly, a semantic-preserving color diversification (SPCD) strategy is introduced, which expands the distribution of training data through controlled color perturbations to enhance the model's adaptability to lighting changes. Secondly, to mitigate the feature degradation during down sampling and improve the perception of scales, a cross-stage feature enhancement (CSFE) module is designed. It effectively fuses high-order and low-order features to enhance spatial positioning accuracy and the ability to depict tree canopies. Additionally, a multi-scale attention map fusion (MAIF) module is introduced, which dynamically aggregates multi-scale features through an adaptive attention mechanism to improve discrimination performance in complex vegetation backgrounds. A large number of experiments conducted on public aerial datasets and self-collected UAV tree canopy datasets demonstrate that the proposed Trans-CNN method outperforms existing methods. It provides a reliable and effective solution for UAV canopy segmentation and supports intelligent pruning and precise landscape management applications.

title: A Chirped Fiber Bragg Grating-Based Force Sensor for Minimally Invasive Surgery
authors: Wenwen Qu, Yanxia Chen, Shuangqiang Liu, Le Luo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: We propose a novel force sensor applied to the precise measurement of axial contact forces between the tip of a surgical instrument and biological tissue during minimally invasive surgery (MIS) palpation procedures. The sensor incorporates a linearly chirped fiber Bragg grating (LCFBG), with a portion of the grating bonded at both ends and suspended at the center of an elastic hollow structure, while the remaining section is left free and unaffected by axial forces. This distinctive structural design leverages the differing force sensitivities of the two sections and enables independent measurement of temperature and force by employing a decoupling matrix. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first reported study that applies a chirped fiber Bragg grating as the sensing fiber in the research on tactile sensors specifically designed for MIS palpation. Experimental results indicate that the proposed sensor exhibits a force sensitivity of 205.19 pm/N within the 0–4.5 N range and a temperature sensitivity of 16.35 pm/°C within the 0–60 °C range, and dynamic temperature experiments further verified its temperature decoupling performance. Stability tests conducted after immersion in porcine blood showed that the maximum output deviation in a blood environment was only 0.69%, demonstrating good long-term stability. Dynamic loading experiments confirmed the sensor’s excellent measurement accuracy. Furthermore, in in vitro simulated palpation experiments, the sensor was able to precisely distinguish small steel blocks embedded at different depths within a silicone phantom, further validating its feasibility for surgical palpation applications.

title: A Generative Model-enhanced Deep Image Prior Framework for Unsupervised Three-dimensional Electrical Impedance Tomography
authors: Yuehui Wu, Xinhao Bai, Jiewei Lu, Jianda Han, Ningbo Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Electrical impedance tomography (EIT), a non-invasive, radiation-free, and real-time imaging modality of tissue conductivity changes, has become an important clinical tool. Three-dimensional EIT offers superior spatial characterization of lung function, but its image reconstruction remains challenging due to the severely ill-posed inverse problem and high sensitivity to measurement noise. This paper presents a generative model-enhanced deep image prior framework for unsupervised three-dimensional electrical impedance tomography reconstruction, where the deep image prior provides implicit regularization and the generative model prior imposes explicit shape constraint to enforce physiologically plausible image. A dual-output network is designed to simultaneously reconstruct the conductivity image and update the generator latent variable. The reconstruction is performed through instance-wise unsupervised optimization with a unified objective that combines data-consistency loss and shape constraint, allowing both the network parameters and the latent representation to be jointly updated. In addition, a residual Noise2Noise denoiser module is devised for EIT data to suppress measurement noise in the voltage input by leveraging residual learning strategy and reciprocal voltage pairs. Extensive simulations and experiments are conducted to evaluate the reconstruction quality, generalization performance, and denoising ability under diverse pulmonary ventilation conditions. The results demonstrate the proposed framework significantly improves 3D pulmonary ventilation imaging with accurate conductivity distribution, high shape fidelity and effective artifact suppression.

title: Wind Farm Cooperative Yaw Predictive Control Against Model Uncertainty Via Continuous Learning From SCADA Data
authors: Yu Wang, Shanbi Wei, Fanghong Zhang, Wei Yang, Yi Chai
abstract: Cooperative yaw control is a critical solution to reduce wake effect and improve power generation of wind farms, and its implementation based on analytical wake model is crucial to realizing its engineering practice. However, the inherent model uncertainty raises challenges. To this end, this paper proposes an application-oriented framework that allows SCADA data to engage cooperative yaw control, which can improve model adaptability and ensure power gain. First, considering the operation principle of industrial yaw system, a novel cooperative yaw predictive control with yaw rate as control action is introduced. Importantly, a continuous learning strategy against model uncertainty is proposed, which is composed of three mechanisms: outlier online detection using model prior knowledge, working scenario generation by distance dependent Chinese restaurant processes, and parameter learning with Bayesian inference. Finally, the result derived from Penmanshiel wind farm demonstrates that the proposed strategy can assimilate SCADA data well with a promising potential for engineering application of model-based cooperative yaw control.

title: Real-Time Experimental Investigation of Open- and Closed-Loop Speed Control for a Prototype EV Powertrain Incorporating a 2.2 kW BLDC Motor and Planetary Gearbox
authors: Ayman Ibrahim Abouseda, Ali Hennache, Resat Doruk, Ali Amini
abstract: This study presents an experimental investigation of open- and closed-loop speed control applied to a 2.2 kW brushless DC (BLDC) motor integrated with a planetary gearbox and evaluated under variable-speed and fixed-load conditions. The aim is to characterize the dynamic response, energy-conversion efficiency, and battery state-of-charge (SOC) behavior of the powertrain under real-time control strategies. A complete laboratory platform was developed using a commercial BLDC motor controller, a 60 V–17.4 Ah lithium-ion battery pack, an optical speed sensor, and an eddy-current dynamometer capable of applying load torques from 1.5 to 7.5 N·m. Open-loop operation regulated speed through direct voltage modulation, whereas closed-loop control was implemented using a proportional–integral–derivative (PID) algorithm executed in real time on a microcontroller. Real-time measurements of voltage, current, torque, and speed were used to compute electrical power, mechanical power, and system efficiency, while battery SOC variation was estimated using the Coulomb-counting method. The results indicate that open-loop control exhibits substantial speed-tracking errors, pronounced transient power surges during speed transitions, reduced efficiency, and an increased SOC depletion rate over the investigated speed range of 1000–2600 rpm. In contrast, the closed-loop PID controller maintains steady-state speed errors within approximately 1 % across all load levels, suppresses current fluctuations, mitigates transient losses, and enhances overall system efficiency by approximately 14–19 percentage points. Closed-loop operation further reduces SOC depletion by 0.49–0.75 percentage points, depending on the applied load torque. These findings demonstrate the effectiveness of feedback control in improving stability, energy utilization, and electric powertrain performance in BLDC-based propulsion systems, with direct relevance to electric-vehicle and hybrid powertrain systems.

title: An interpolation-free cell-centered finite volume scheme for 2-D Magnetotelluric forward modeling on polygonal meshes
authors: Ziyi Ou, Emilio Porcu, Guibin Zhao
abstract: We present an interpolation-free cell-centered finite-volume method (IFFVM) for two-dimensional magnetotelluric forward modeling on arbitrary polygonal meshes. ifUsing the conditions of normal gradient continuity and tangential flow continuity on the edge of the cells, a gradient conversion operator is designed to effectively handle Robin boundary conditions. The second-order convergence of the finite volume scheme is verified through typical examples, including the international standard model, the terrain model, and the anisotropic model. Numerical results show that the finite-volume method can solve magnetotelluric problems with arbitrary terrain with high accuracy and, on comparable meshes, shows improved accuracy relative to the finite-element method.

title: Effect of Winding Densities on Screening Current Behaviors in REBCO Coils
authors: Junichiro Takei, Takanobu Mato, So Noguchi
abstract: The rare-earth barium copper oxide (REBCO) exhibits remarkably high current densities under very-high field. The development of the REBCO magnets is advancing towards high-field applications such as nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and fusion reactors. The maturation of magnet technology has paved the way for the high field generation. One of the issues in REBCO magnets towards high-field applications is the screening currents. The screening currents cause several issues; e.g., the deterioration of coil stability, the coil deformations, and the unexpected field distribution. In this presentation, we focus on the effect of the winding densities on screening current distributions. To this end, the densely and sparsely wound REBCO coils are simulated with finite element method (FEM) using the T-A formulation. The sparsely wound REBCO coils experience larger radial fields than the densely wound coils, resulting in a higher susceptibility to induced screening currents. Meanwhile, at high operating currents, the screening current induced fields in the sparsely wound coil is smaller than those in the densely wound coils. Furthermore, the BJR stress applied to the sparsely wound coils is lower than that of the densely wound coils. We also discuss the strategies for the high field coils considering the winding densities.

title: Automating Cyber Risk Assessment with Public LLMs: An Expert-Validated Framework and Comparative Analysis
authors: Nezih Mahmut Unal, Baris Celiktas
abstract: Traditional cyber risk assessment methodologies face a critical dilemma: they are either quantitative yet static and context-agnostic (e.g., CVSS), or context-aware yet highly labor-intensive and subjective (e.g., NIST SP 800-30). Consequently, organizations struggle to scale risk assessment to match the pace of evolving threats. This paper presents an automated, context-aware risk assessment framework that leverages the reasoning capabilities of publicly available Large Language Models (LLMs) to operationalize expert knowledge. Rather than positioning the LLM as the final decision-maker, the framework decouples semantic interpretation from risk scoring authority through a transparent, deterministic Dynamic Metric Engine. Unlike complex black-box machine learning models, our approach anchors the AI’s reasoning to this expert-validated metric schema, with weights derived using the Rank Order Centroid (ROC) method from a survey of 101 cybersecurity professionals. We evaluated the framework through a comparative study involving 15 diverse real-world vulnerability scenarios (C1−C15) and three supplementary sensitivity stress tests (C16 −C18). The validation scenarios were independently assessed by a cohort of ten senior human experts and two state-of-the-art LLM agents (GPT-4o and Gemini 2.0 Flash). The results show that the LLM-driven agents achieve scoring consistency closely aligned with the human median (Pearson r ranging from 0.9390 to 0.9717, Spearman ρ from 0.8472 to 0.9276) against a highly reliable expert baseline (Cronbach’s α = 0.996), while reducing the assessment cycle time by more than 100× (averaging under 4 seconds per case vs. a human average of 6 minutes). Furthermore, a dedicated context sensitivity analysis (C13 − C15) indicates that the framework adapts risk scores based on organizational context (e.g., SME vs. Critical Infrastructure) for identical technical vulnerabilities. Importantly, the system is designed not merely to replicate expert intuition, but to enforce bounded, policy-consistent risk evaluation under predefined governance constraints. Overall, these findings suggest that commercially available LLMs, when constrained by expert-validated metric schemas, can support reproducible, transparent, and real-time risk assessments.

title: Hiding in Plain Sight: RIS-Aided Target Obfuscation in ISAC
authors: Ahmed Magbool, Vaibhav Kumar, Marco Di Renzo, Mark F. Flanagan
abstract: Integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) has emerged as a promising technology for sixth-generation (6G) communication networks. At the same time, ensuring the privacy of targets in ISAC is important in contexts where a malicious sensor is present. In this paper, we investigate a reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS)-assisted ISAC system designed to protect a sensing region against an adversarial detector (AD), where the base station (BS) has imperfect knowledge of the AD’s location. The RIS consists of both reflecting and absorptive elements (the latter serving as sensing elements), which can be adaptively reconfigured to meet system requirements. Specifically, the system is designed to maximize the jamming power from the BS to the AD by jointly optimizing the transmit beamformer at the BS, the RIS phase-shift matrix, the receive beamformer at the RIS, and the allocation between reflecting and absorptive elements at the RIS while ensuring a minimum sensing signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) at sample points within the sensing region, as well as a minimum communication SINR for each user. To address this challenging optimization problem, we propose an alternating optimization framework combined with a successive convex approximation method tailored for each subproblem. Our results show that the proposed system model offers significant protection of the sensing area compared to the case where the target privacy is not considered. Simulations also confirm that the proposed adaptive RIS partitioning outperforms the fixed RIS partitioning approach.

title: Beyond Space Routers: Revisiting the Role of LEO Satellite Constellations in Future Global Internet Architecture
authors: Songshi Dou, Shengyu Zhang, Xianhao Chen, Zehua Guo, Tony Q. S. Quek, Kwan L. Yeung
abstract: Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite constellations are redefining global Internet infrastructure with transformative potential. This article first reviews the evolution of LEO satellite networks from early bent-pipe models to sophisticated space routers with inter-satellite links, which enable data to hop across satellites in space. Beyond providing wide-area coverage and low-latency connectivity, modern LEO satellite constellations are taking on multi-faceted functions traditionally associated with terrestrial networks. Specifically, we discuss emerging paradigms in which satellites perform in-network content caching to bring data closer to users. We also highlight the use of LEO satellite networks for enhancing Internet resilience during disasters and outages, as well as initiatives for cross-constellation interoperability that envision an integrated “Internet of satellites” spanning the globe. We aim to provide a synthesis of LEO satellites’ expanding capabilities (beyond space routers) and an analysis of the architectural shift towards a space-based multi-role infrastructure. Finally, several future research directions and opportunities are outlined to highlight the broader significance of LEO satellite constellations in the design of future Internet architectures and their potential to extend services beyond traditional terrestrial networks.

title: Development of a Capacitance-Based Sensor for Flexible Rolling Contact Joint Systems
authors: Hao Liu, Jia Yu, Ting Wang, Hongqiang Wang
abstract: This paper presents a rolling-contact joint embedded with a capacitive angle sensing method fabricated using a flexible printed circuit board process. By leveraging the inherent mechanical constraints of the joint's crossed-band structure, enabling high-precision angular measurement without requiring additional rigid supports. The method is applicable to both rigid and soft joint configurations, offering a compact, lightweight, and compliant sensing solution with an extended measurement range compared to conventional flexible sensors. A 2-DOF cable-driven joint with decoupled motion was realized through optimized cable routing, and a 3-DOF robotic arm integrating the proposed sensor achieved real-time position control. The sensing approach was further validated in inflatable soft joints, demonstrating adaptability across diverse robotic platforms.

title: AoI-Driven Dynamic Multipath Congestion Control for Space-Air-Ground-Sea Integrated Network
authors: Xueli Wang, Xiaoqiang Di, Yuchen Zhu, Jinhui Cao
abstract: Space–air–ground–sea integrated network (SAGSIN) is widely envisioned as a key architecture for 6G, offering seamless global communication coverage. However, conventional congestion control schemes that do not consider data freshness are not suitable for SAGSIN, which consists of cooperative multilayer networks with time-varying topologies operating in open communication environments. In this paper, to support efficient, real-time communication in SAGSIN, we propose the age of information (AoI)-driven dynamic multipath congestion control (AMCC) scheme, which uses data freshness as the primary constraint for congestion control. By integrating the centralized control features of Software Defined Networking (SDN) with the content-centric communication paradigm of Named Data Networking (NDN), a hierarchical congestion control framework is designed. At the global level, the SDN controller employs a Nash bargaining game to dynamically compute the optimal forwarding weight for each interface according to the interface quality and the AoI constraints. At the local level, intermediate forwarding nodes establish a bidirectional congestion detection mechanism that monitors the interest queue length and AoI of data packets. The results are fed back to the consumers to enable real-time traffic adjustment, forming a closed-loop control system integrating awareness, feedback, and adaptation for fully adaptive traffic control. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed AMCC scheme reduces the AoI from 46.2% to 71.3% and improves throughput from 18.5% to 25.8% when compared with representative schemes PCON, HoBHIS, MIRCC, and JAQMCC. The proposed AMCC scheme can provide high-timeliness data transmission of SAGSIN for scenarios such as air-based disaster warning, vehicle–edge–cloud collaboration, and real-time ocean monitoring.

title: Dual Objective Model Predictive Current Control Method for PMSM Drives
authors: Chenguang Zhang, Tianyu Yuan, Xiaoguang Zhang, Niko Nevaranta, Pasi Peltoniemi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems II: Express Briefs
abstract: Model predictive current control (MPCC) is recognized as a promising control method for permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM) drives. In conventional MPCC, only current tracking is considered while the switching transition term is neglected, which can reduce system efficiency and overall control performance. To balance the influence of current error and switching transition term, a dual objective MPCC (DOMPCC) method is put forward in this paper. First, the conventional MPCC method is introduced and the limitations are formulated. Then, the proposed method is presented where a new cost function including switching transition term is designed. In addition, the design process for the balance factor and weighting coefficient is developed, and the stability analysis is provided. Finally, comparative studies are conducted on a 1.25 kW experimental platform to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed DOMPCC method.

title: Guest Editorial: Introduction to the Special Section on New Optimization Strategies for Synthesizing RF, Microwave, mmWave, and Optical Devices
authors: Arkaprovo Das, Manushanker Balasubramanian, Douglas H. Werner, Alberto Toccafondi, Andrea Massa
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Antennas and Propagation

title: A Matrix-Exponential-Based Symplectic DGTD Algorithm for Simulating Drude-Type Dispersive Media
authors: Guoda Xie, Chenfeng Wang, Danfeng Han, Zhixiang Huang, Sheng Sun, Ran Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: This communication presents a p-order symplectic discontinuous Galerkin time-domain (Sp-DGTD) method incorporating the matrix exponential (ME) technique for accurate simulation of the electromagnetic properties of Drude-type dispersive media. In the proposed approach, the auxiliary field equations describing the dispersive properties of the medium are reformulated together with Maxwell’s equations into a compact first-order matrix differential form, which is then discretized using a multistage symplectic time-integration scheme. The resulting exponential matrix operator is evaluated via the ME technique, while the spatial differential operators are discretized using the Discontinuous Galerkin (DG) scheme. Numerical results demonstrate that, under identical simulation conditions, the proposed Sp-DGTD method achieves higher accuracy than the traditional leapfrog DGTD (LF-DGTD) method in modeling the electromagnetic scattering characteristics of complex dispersive media.

title: Enhancing Event Organization Efficiency through Intelligent Chatbot: A Study on Automated Assistance and Engagement
authors: Ryakam Lavanya, S.Paramesh, V Venkataiah, T Satyanarayana, Selvanathan N, Dr.S.Nagasundaram
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Recent Trends in Electrical, Electronics and Computing Technologies (ICRTEECT)
abstract: Given the growing number of Android malware, it is important to build better and stronger systems that can detect it. Typically, using traditional methods to detect malware results in a high rate of mistakes, uses a lot of computer resources, and struggles with keeping up with emerging threats. For solving these issues, this study suggests optimizing which features to use in Android malware using Genetic Algorithms (GA) with Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), leading to improved malware recognition. Highlighting the most important features through GA allows you to cut down on the number of features and still get more accurate results while using fewer computing resources. The feature set is next used to train a CNN model, which identifies complicated aspects in the way malware behaves. Experiments on benchmark Android malware show that the GA-CNN method detects malware more accurately, reduces the number of false-positives, and is faster than standard feature selection and machine learning tools. The study informs the creation of strong and flexible systems for detecting Android malware, in line with shifting cyber security threats.

title: Evaluating RPL Topologies in IoT: A Statistical Analysis of PDR, PLR, and Energy Consumption
authors: Vaibhav Ajay, Virender Ranga
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Trends in Computing and Communication (ETCOM)
abstract: Low-Power and Lossy Networks (LLNs) underpin many IoT deployments but exhibit strong sensitivity to node geometry, radio duty cycling (RDC), and control-plane timing. We present a reproducible assessment of RPL across three deployment layouts-Random, Linear, and Elliptical-implemented in Instant Contiki 2.7/Cooja with Sky motes (IEEE 802.15.4). Consistent with our journal study, we made explicit implementation changes: (i) removed randomised pre-send delays from the sender path, (ii) fixed the inter-packet interval at 60 s per node, (iii) selectively disabled RDC (NETSTACK_RDC.Off (1)) for the sink and designated relays, and (iv) tuned RPL timers (DIO_INTERVAL_MIN=8, DIO_INTERVAL_DOUBLINGS=12) and parent hysteresis to curb flapping. Across 120 independent simulations (40 per topology), we measured Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR), Packet Loss Ratio (PLR), average hop count, CPU power, and Energy Consumption per Packet (ECPP). Statistical validation (Shapiro-Wilk, Levene, ANOVA/KruskalWallis, Tukey HSD, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\eta^{2}$</tex>) confirms topology as a dominant factor (p<0.001, large effects). Random topology achieved the highest PDR, Linear reduced hop count and reduced CPU power consumption, but exhibited bottlenecks, while Elliptical offered balanced performance. Beyond point estimates, we report distributional behaviour, significance, and effect magnitudes, offering reproducible insights for topology-aware planning in IoT deployments.

title: Predicting Urban Heat Island Using Machine Learning Techniques
authors: E. Ramesh, Srikanth Meda, S J R K Padminivalli V
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Recent Trends in Electrical, Electronics and Computing Technologies (ICRTEECT)
abstract: Urban Heat Island (UHI) means cities get hotter than nearby villages because of buildings and roads. This causes problems like more heat, higher electricity use, and health issues. In this paper, we study UHI in Amaravati, a new planned city in India. We use special computer methods called ensemble learning to predict how hot Amaravati will get. We collect data about weather, land, and buildings in Amaravati. Then, we use three methods—Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, and Voting Regressor—to make predictions. Our results show that these methods work well, with the Voting Regressor being the best. We also show how hot different parts of Amaravati might get and suggest ways to make the city cooler.

title: Voltage Sag Classification and Cause Identification Based on Pattern Recognition
authors: Haodong Cai, Haoyong Chen
publish: 2025 3rd Power Electronics and Power System Conference (PEPSC)
abstract: With the rapid development of economy and society, demand for electricity and sensitive power-consuming devices is increasing, raising the standards for voltage stability and power quality. For modern manufacturing industries, voltage sags and short-term interruptions have become critical issues affecting safe and reliable operation. This paper analyzes three typical causes of voltage sags: large induction motor starting, short-circuit faults, and transformer switching, and summarizes their characteristic RMS voltage waveforms. Pattern recognition principles are introduced, and support vector machine, neural network, and decision tree classification methods are applied. Simulation models of the three sag sources are built, and a MATLAB program extracts key sag features, including magnitude, duration, phase-angle jump, three-phase balance, pre-and post-sag voltage differences, and transient voltage rise. Using 150 training and 60 test samples, the three classifiers are trained and evaluated, verifying the effectiveness of the extracted features in voltage sag source identification.

title: AI-Powered Inventory Management: Enhancing Forecast Accuracy and Operational Efficiency in Modern Supply Chains
authors: Parveen Bajaj, Lalit Kumar, Anuradha, Ankita Nainwal, Kumari Mayko Muni, Manmohan Singh
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: This research examines how artificial intelligence reengineers traditional inventory management systems to meet today's supply chain issues. The study examines AI-based solutions that enhance the accuracy of demand forecasting, stock optimization, and operational processes in more complex business environments. Our analysis of 47 companies across different industries discovers significant performance improvement with AI implementation: a 37 % reduction in stockouts, a 29 % reduction in excess inventory, 42 % improvement in forecast accuracy, and a 23 % reduction in operational expenses. In addition, 18 supply chain executives' interviews also revealed critical success factors including data infrastructure quality, cross-functional integration, phased implementation, ongoing retraining of models, and executive sponsorship. In addition, 18 supply chain executives' interviews also revealed critical success factors, including data infrastructure quality, cross-functional integration, phased implementation, ongoing retraining of models, and executive sponsorship. Four technologies, the study maintains, are the most critical drivers of innovation: predictive analytics for forecasting demand, prescriptive analytics to enable optimal inventory, autonomous decision-making solutions, and smart automation of manual inventory processes.

title: DSFA-ICA Monitoring Method for Strip Flatness Abnormality in the Cold Tandem Rolling Process
authors: Weimin Yin, Yanbo Hu, Chi Lin, Jiayi Zhang, Yanling Wang, Fei Xiang, Hui Yu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Big Data and Data Mining (BDDM)
abstract: The cold tandem rolling process is characterized by short sampling intervals, numerous variables, mixed Gaussian and non-Gaussian data distributions, and strong dynamic coupling. Flatness, as a key quality indicator of cold-rolled strips, is particularly sensitive to these complex dynamics. Traditional process monitoring techniques often struggle to capture the nonlinear and temporal dependencies inherent in the process, making it difficult to effectively detect flatness abnormalities. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a combined monitoring method based on Dynamic Slow Feature Analysis (DSFA) and Independent Component Analysis (ICA) for strip flatness abnormality detection in cold tandem rolling. In the proposed approach, time-delayed variables are incorporated to construct a dynamic dataset, after which DSFA is employed to extract the principal dynamic features. Subsequently, ICA is applied to capture the non-Gaussian characteristics of the residual signals using exponential kurtosis. Experimental results on industrial cold rolling process data demonstrate that the proposed DSFAICA model achieves a monitoring rate of 96.31% with a false alarm rate of only 1.26%, exhibiting superior performance and robustness compared with other monitoring models.

title: A Novel Deep Learning Framework for Coral Reef Image Classification Using CNNs, Handcrafted Features, and Self-Attention
authors: Harshul Gupta, Rishita Yadav
publish: 2025 IEEE Asia-Pacific Conference on Geoscience, Electronics and Remote Sensing Technology (AGERS)
abstract: Corals are stationary marine invertebrates that form vibrant underwater ecosystems known as reefs. However, due to their sensitivity to temperature fluctuations, corals undergo bleaching: a process in which they lose their color and, more critically, the symbiotic organisms essential to marine ecosystems and pharmacognosy. In response to this environmental threat, researchers are actively developing treatment strategies to restore bleached corals. A crucial prerequisite for these efforts is the precise localization of bleached corals, particularly within the Great Barrier Reef. Although several visual classification frameworks have been proposed, their performance often deteriorates under variations in illumination, orientation, scale, and viewing angle. To overcome these challenges, we propose a novel hybrid deep learning architecture that integrates Self-Attention mechanisms with pretrained convolutional neural networks such as ResNet50 and InceptionV3. The Self-Attention component enables the network to model longrange dependencies across feature maps, allowing it to selectively focus on the most relevant spatial regions for accurate classification. The architecture is fine-tuned on coral reef datasets to distinguish between bleached and healthy corals. Our method achieves a classification accuracy of 98.15% on a dataset collected from the Great Barrier Reef. Additionally, we evaluated the model’s localization capability on 342 images comprising both training and testing segments. Results demonstrate that our hybrid model significantly outperforms existing standalone and hybrid deep neural networks as well as traditional handcrafted approaches reported in the literature.

title: Enhancing Fashion Recommendations Through Multimodal Graph Neural Networks with Cold-Start User Handling
authors: M. L. Rathnayaka, K. L. P. P. Liyanaarachchi
publish: 2025 IEEE 19th International Conference on Industrial and Information Systems (ICIIS)
abstract: In fashion e-commerce, providing relevant product recommendations to new users is a challenge known as the "cold-start". This problem is very difficult because these users have little to no interaction history. To address this gap, this study proposes a novel recommendation system (RS) built on a multimodal Graph Neural Network (GNN). The solution is designed to fuse rich product information (product images, textual descriptions, and price) with a simple but effective demographic attribute: the user’s age. This fusion creates an effective user representation even when interaction data is sparse. This approach was evaluated on a large-scale fashion dataset. Comparing the age-aware model to a baseline model that did not use demographic data. The results demonstrate a significant improvement in recommendation quality. The proposed model achieved an NDCG@10 of 0.2743 (compared to 0.1006 for the baseline) and a Recall@10 of 0.4915 (versus 0.1783). This main finding confirms that integrating even a single demographic feature like age with multimodal content can materially and effectively improve recommendation quality for cold-start users.

title: TruthMedic: A Multi-Modal Fake News Detection System for Health Misinformation
authors: Manisha. R, Monica. A, Gokulnath Rao. H, K. Regin Bose
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Recent Trends in Electrical, Electronics and Computing Technologies (ICRTEECT)
abstract: In the age of digital information, the rapid spread of health-related misinformation poses a serious hazard to public well-being. This paper presents TruthMedic, an AI-powered platform designed to detect and flag false health claims from both textual input and image-based text . The system combines Optical Character Recognition (OCR), Natural Language Processing (NLP) and real-time web data abstraction from trusted sources such as WebMD and Mayo Clinic. A chatbot interface enables users to input health queries or upload images containing health claims. These ideas are semantically analyzed, fact-checked against verified datasets or (Application Programming Interface) APIs and returned with a credibility score and sourced answer. This multi-modal approach improves the reliability and accessibility of health misinformation, especially for laypersons. Keywords—Health misinformation, Fake news detection, Health fact-checking, Multi-modal system,Public health safety.

title: Hybrid Weightless Neural Networks for Efficient Edge Inference
authors: Mugdha Jadhao, Alan T. L. Bacellar, Shashank Nag, Igor D. S. Miranda, Felipe M. G. França, Lizy K. John
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Field-Programmable Logic and Applications (FPL)
abstract: Deploying fast, accurate, and efficient machine learning on edge devices remains a key research challenge. While deep neural networks (DNNs) excel in vision tasks, their computational and storage demands hinder deployment on resourceconstrained hardware. Optimization strategies such as quantization, sparsity induction, and multiplication-free architectures have been explored to address these challenges. Weightless Neural Networks (WNNs), based on look-up tables, offer high energy efficiency and low-latency inference, making them attractive for edge applications. However, WNNs struggle with complex vision tasks due to their lack of support for positional invariance. To enable machine learning models that are both lightweight and accurate for edge inference, we propose a hybrid weightless neural network model (H-WNN) that integrates the efficiency of WNNs with the spatial feature extraction capabilities of quantized convolution. FPGAs serve as an ideal platform for exploring the hybrid approach, as they facilitate efficient design space exploration for quantized convolution, allow direct mapping of look-up tables in WNNs, and enable seamless integration of both models. We additionally present a workflow to explore hardwareaccuracy tradeoffs for H-WNN models on hardware platforms. We evaluate H-WNN on multiple classification datasets relevant to edge applications and compare them against similar existing studies, such as FINN-R and DWN. Across the benchmarks, our results consistently demonstrate that H-WNN achieves lower resource usage and latency while maintaining competitive accuracy and throughput. For example, on CIFAR-10, H-WNN achieves 87.72 % accuracy while being <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 \times$</tex> smaller than competing approaches at the same throughput, resulting in improved energy efficiency.

title: Real-Time Inertial Sensor Fusion for High-Speed Human Motion Tracking in Unconstrained Environments
authors: Aderajew Tilahun, Jeronimo Cox, Tomonari Furukawa, Gamini Dissanayake
publish: 2025 IEEE 19th International Conference on Industrial and Information Systems (ICIIS)
abstract: This paper presents an inertial sensor fusion approach for estimating high-speed human motion using a wearable Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs) in unconstrained environments and over long durations. The method targets dynamic activities in unlimited fields such as athletic activities, where traditional camera-based motion tracking systems are impractical due to limited field-of-view or occlusions. Accelerometers, gyroscopes and magnetometers are strategically mounted and modeled to maximize measurement accuracy through the past work of inertial sensor fusion using Extended Kalman Filter (EKF). A torque-free dynamic model is employed as the motion model, while IMU threshold-based motion phase detection adaptively adjusts process noise, enabling robust tracking across varying dynamic conditions. The system was experimentally validated through human lower-limb and benchmarked against marker-based ground truth from an optical motion capture system. Experimental results show that the proposed approach significantly improves joint-angle estimation accuracy during high-speed motions. Compared to the traditional methods, the proposed approach reduces the mean RMSE across the three axes by up to 60.5%, 19%, and 62.1% for the hip, knee, and ankle joints in jumping, and by 62.5%, 57.2%, and 59.4% during leg swing, demonstrating strong robustness and potential suitability for full-body tracking in high-speed, unconstrained scenarios.

title: Port Selection in Fluid Antennas: GP Interpolation with Spatial Jitter Modeling
authors: Daniel Dinis, Risto Wichman
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: Fluid antenna system (FAS) achieves spatial diversity by selecting the best radiation port among many closely spaced candidates. However, measuring all ports leads to excessive pilot overhead, switching latency, and implementation complexity. This paper proposes a Gaussian processes (GP)-based spatial interpolation framework that estimates the unmeasured port responses from a small subset of observations, enabling near- optimal port selection with low spatial over-sampling. The model also accounts for antenna position jitter by treating positional deviations as uncertainty in the sampling locations. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves performance close to the ideal case with perfect channel knowledge, while requiring significantly fewer measurements. Moreover, it consistently outperforms direct port selection and even exhaustive scanning schemes, highlighting the efficiency and robustness of the proposed technique.

title: Kinesthetic Drills for Enhancing Children's Engagement in Four Basic Arithmetic Skills
authors: Itsuki Arita, Nobuyuki Umezu
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and eXtended and Virtual Reality (AIxVR)
abstract: We propose kinesthetic drills for children to enhance engagement in basic arithmetic practice. By performing the four core operations-addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division-using their bodies, learners experience a more enjoyable study session than traditional paper-based methods. This approach is expected to increase motivation for math drills and improve learning outcomes through sustained engagement. The method employs camera-based pose estimation with Google Mediapipe to detect the user's nose, shoulders, elbows, and hands in two-dimensional coordinates captured by a USB camera. During a drill, the player repeats actions such as reaching out with their hand to ‘grab’ a number button, sometimes while stretching their back or squatting. Questions require the player to select two or three operands that combine to a specified result-for example, choosing 2 and 5, multiplying them, and obtaining 10. We demonstrated the system at a science museum and conducted user experiments. More than 50 visitors rated the system on a five-point Likert scale. Compared to traditional paper-based drills, the proposed system received high evaluations for interest and perceived effectiveness, although there is room for usability improvements.

title: Software-Defined Radio Implementation of M-PSK Transceiver for Narrowband PLC Using MATLAB and USRP
authors: Kenneth Chauke, Akintunde Oluremi Iyiola, Thokozani Shongwe
publish: 2025 International Conference on Innovation and Intelligence for Informatics, Computing, and Technologies (3ICT)
abstract: This paper presents a real-time implementation and experimental evaluation of a single-carrier <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$M$</tex>-ary PhaseShift Keying (M-PSK) (4/8/16-PSK) transceiver for narrowband power line communication (NB-PLC). MATLAB/Simulink and Universal Software Radio Peripheral (USRP) hardware were used, with a galvanic isolation coupling circuit. Signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) levels of 10,15, and 20 dB are emulated via digital scaling. A “Hello world” payload is transmitted across 1,000 frames under three controlled test conditions: no active load, dimmer-only, and dimmer-plus-heater. System performance is assessed through frame synchronization and bit error rate (BER) analysis. Synchronization misalignments account for up to 80% of errors in high-noise conditions. Detection probability dropped from 99.5% to 78%, while root mean square error (RMSE) rose from 0.15 to 1.20 samples. BER dropped below <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$8 \times 10^{-4}$</tex> for 4 -PSK at 20 dB but reached 0.40 for 16-PSK at 10 dB. These results highlight NB-PLC's potential and constraints for reliable, low-cost smart city communications, including smart metering, street lighting, and distributed IoT sensing.

title: A Novel Autoencoder Framework for Cyber Attack Classification in IIoT Networks for Detecting Unseen Anomalies
authors: Kishor Krishna Kumar Soanpet, Raushan Kumar, Raushan Kumar
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Recent Trends in Electrical, Electronics and Computing Technologies (ICRTEECT)
abstract: The expansion of the Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) has created complex cybersecurity challenges, as traditional supervised and signature-based detection approaches often fail to detect emerging and previously unknown attack vectors. To address this challenge, a novel deep learning framework has been developed, wherein a deep symmetric autoencoder is employed to learn denoised, latent feature representations from raw, high-dimensional IIoT data in an unsupervised manner; these representations are then utilized by a supervised neural network classifier composed of dense layers and a softmax output for precise multi-class cyberattack classification. The integration of unsupervised anomaly detection through autoencoder reconstruction loss, together with supervised classification driven by categorical cross-entropy and macro-averaged F1-score optimization, constitutes a key methodological innovation, further enhanced by an optimized Tensor Flow data pipeline and check pointing mechanisms. When evaluated on Edge-IIoTset Cyber Security Dataset, the proposed hybrid model has demonstrated a marked improvement over conventional machine learning and deep learning baselines—including Decision Trees, SVMs, ResNet50, and CNNs—achieving an accuracy of 97.19%, a macro F1-score of 0.87, and perfect multi-class AUC values. From these results, it can be concluded that the integration of unsupervised representation learning and supervised classification not only enables superior detection of both typical and novel attack types but also offers a scalable, generalizable, and resilient solution for IIoT cyber security, substantially advancing the capabilities of intelligent threat detection in industrial environments.

title:
A Comprehensive Comparative Analysis of Synthesis Methodologies, Physicochemical Properties, and Diverse Applications of Titanium Dioxide (TiO
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    ), Zinc Oxide (ZnO), and Iron (III) Oxide (Fe
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    O
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    ) Quantum Dots: An In-Depth Investigation
authors: Shrushti K. Chaudhary, Himalay M. Kolavada
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Recent Trends in Electrical, Electronics and Computing Technologies (ICRTEECT)
abstract: The rapid advancement of nanotechnology has attracted great interests in semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) more often metal oxide-based quantum dots. Among them, TiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>, ZnO, and Fe<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> QDs have attracted considerable attention due to their low cost, environmental-friendly, and programmable to nanoscale effects. The inordinate physicochemical characteristics (dependent on the size) of metal oxide quantum dots (QDs): iron (III) oxide Fe<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>, zinc oxide (ZnO) and titanium dioxide TiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> have rendered them extremely versatile nanomaterials. This research has comparative research of production processes, structural and optical features and variety of application of these QDs. Every quantum dot is evaluated depending on stability, tunability of band gap, surface chemistry, control over particle size, synthesis methods (sol-gel, hydrothermal, microwave, and plant-based synthesis), and stability. The use of them in optoelectronics, biological imaging, environmental cleanup and photocatalysis is critically analysed. The study attempts to guide future research through the identification of the strengths and weaknesses of each material platform in relation to some applications.

title: Non-Uniform B-Spline Optimization Method for Generating Swept Surfaces
authors: Xiaoyan Kui, Min Yang, Songpeng Yao, Hao Wang, Enya Shen, Qinsong Li, Beiji Zou
publish: 2025 International Conference on Big Data and Data Mining (BDDM)
abstract: Swept surface construction is widely used in computer-aided design. We propose a novel optimization method using non-uniform B-splines to improve the approximate accuracy of swept surfaces. First, discrete points on the swept shape are computed, and geometric properties such as surface area, discrete curvature, first-order derivatives, and their rotation angles are used to derive a distribution function representing surface irregularity, with weights adjusted from samples. Then, feature points are selected based on the distribution function to determine control points for the approximate non-uniform B-spline surface via inverse calculation, producing an optimized approximation. Finally, the number of feature points is adjusted based on the estimated approximation error. Experiments on 969 randomly generated sweep samples and 1 pipe example show that the proposed algorithm achieves similar accuracy with fewer control points, reducing them by about 15.85 % at a specified accuracy of 0.01. Moreover, with ample sampling points, it reduces the average error by approximately 51.35 % when the feature point multiple is 10 times the path control points, outperforming comparable methods.

title: Non-contact Surface Detection of Plastics using a Portable Mie-Scattering Lidar (MSL) System
authors: Mark Nickole Tabafa, James Roy Lesidan, Jessa Neolpes, Jumar Cadondon, Maria Cecilia Galvez, Tatsuo Shiina, Edgar Vallar
publish: 2025 IEEE Asia-Pacific Conference on Geoscience, Electronics and Remote Sensing Technology (AGERS)
abstract: This study presents the development and evaluation of a compact 405 nm laser diode Mie-scattering Lidar (MSL) system for the non-contact detection and characterization of plasma-treated plastic surfaces. Five common polymers, such as polypropylene (PP), polystyrene (PS), polyvinyl chloride (PVC), polyethylene terephthalate (PET), and high-density polyethylene (HDPE), were exposed to Atmospheric Cold Plasma (ACP) for 0, 5, and 10 minutes. The Lidar backscattered signal intensity was analyzed alongside conventional surface degradation parameters, including weight loss and contact angle. Results showed that Lidar intensity decreased with prolonged plasma exposure, correlating strongly with surface roughness and hydrophilicity. PP, HDPE, and PVC exhibited the strongest correlations between Lidar signal and contact angle ($\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}} \boldsymbol{=} \mathbf{0. 8 6}, \mathbf{0. 8 5}$, and $\mathbf{0. 9 3}$, respectively), confirming Lidar’s high sensitivity to surface wettability. The developed Lidar system demonstrated high sensitivity in detecting plasma-induced surface modifications in polymers, with the most significant signal reduction and surface correlation observed after $\mathbf{1 0}$ minutes of plasma exposure. This confirms the system’s effectiveness in identifying pronounced surface changes and establishes its potential as a portable, real-time tool for monitoring surface modification processes, bridging laboratory-based analysis with field-deployable sensing applications.

title: Threshold Effect in Period Estimation for Short Periodic Pulses
authors: Sebastian Schertler, Stefan Schuster, Stefan Scheiblhofer, Alexander Haberl, Clemens Staudinger, Johann Reisinger, Oliver Lang
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: We consider the estimation of the fundamental frequency of periodic pulses. Three estimators are applied, one based on a multi-harmonic signal model and two on time-domain signal models that incorporate knowledge of the pulse shape or pulse length. It turns out that these estimators feature approximately the same asymptotic performance. Still, in the threshold region, the estimators based on the time-domain produced better results because they can include information about the pulse’s length. As the threshold region is defined by the increasing occurrence of outliers, we derive an analytical approximation for the mean square error (MSE) and analyze the impact different signal models have on it. The results are compared with Monte Carlo simulations for all signal models. An important insight is that the threshold effect is directly related to the number of parameters in which the signal model is linear. As time-domain signal models explicitly model pulse trains, less parameters are required, which improves performance.

title: Prognosticate of Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (GDM) to Anticipate Preeclampsia
authors: Srinivasa Reddy Seelam, Bala Veeravatnam, Veena Nanda Jaya Krishna, Mantri Gayatri, M V Kamal, Animoni Nagaraju
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Recent Trends in Electrical, Electronics and Computing Technologies (ICRTEECT)
abstract: This paper introduces a new way to predict Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (GDM) using deep learning, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), achieving 92% accuracy in identifying at-risk pregnancies. GDM is a condition that can cause serious complications for both mothers and infants, such as preeclampsia. Early prediction is especially important in places where regular checkups are limited. By analysing clinical data, including patient demographics and medical history, this approach enhances the understanding of risk factors and allows for timely interventions like dietary changes and blood glucose monitoring. Overall, this method represents a significant improvement in maternal care, especially for vulnerable populations, by providing high predictive accuracy and enabling proactive health measures to improve outcomes for mothers and babies.

title: Optimization and Simulation of Optical Device Performance Parameters based on Deep Learning Algorithm
authors: Manli Wei
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: In order to solve the problems of complex parameter adjustment in the design and optimization of traditional optical devices, this study adopts an optimization and simulation method combined with deep neural network (DNN) technology. This paper analyzes the working principle and performance parameters of optical devices and clarifies the optimization objectives and constraints. Subsequently, a DNN model is constructed, taking the performance parameters of optical devices as input and the key performance of the devices. In the model training stage, the supervised learning method is adopted. By continuously adjusting the parameters such as weights and biases of the deep neural network model, the performance of the model output is made as close as possible to the real data. After the model training is completed, it is applied to the performance parameter optimization of optical devices, setting the objective function and using the trained model to efficiently search the parameter space. During the search process, the search direction is intelligently adjusted according to the performance of the current parameter combination to find the optimal or approximately optimal parameter combination. By comparing the data before and after optimization, the optimized fidelity is generally improved to above 0.80, the system loss is reduced, and the average thermal stability is reduced from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.44 \text{dB} /{}^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$</tex> to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.23 \text{dB} /{}^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$</tex>. These results show that the optimization method in this paper can not only effectively improve the performance of optical devices but also provide a new way to improve the performance and reduce the cost of optical communication systems.

title: From Data to Decisions: Deep Learning Models for Urban Livability Analysis
authors: Hariharan R, Obulaneni Bharadwaj, Nagaraj P, Pranav B H, Niveditha G. Y, Prashanth Kannadaguli
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Trends in Computing and Communication (ETCOM)
abstract: Assessing urban livability is a crucial part of sustainable city development. Conventional approaches use surveys or domain-specific indexes, which can be expensive and time-consuming. In this work, a deep learning-based framework for estimating a city's livability score based on socioeconomic variables is presented. We implemented and compared five neural models: TabNet, ResNet-style 1D CNN, Wide & Deep Network, Deep ResMLP, and FT-Transformer. The suggested method shows strong performance on a variety of assessment measures, proving that deep learning is a good fit for datadriven urban planning.

title: Optimized Feature-Based Machine Learning Framework for Predicting Chemical Biodegradability
authors: M. Venkata Subbarao, G. Pavana Sri Asritha, K. Padma Sri, D. Ramesh Varma, D. Girish Kumar, G. Challa Ram
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Trends in Computing and Communication (ETCOM)
abstract: Predicting the biodegradability of chemical compounds is a pressing need in environmental science, where the accumulation of non-biodegradable substances poses ecological and health hazards. Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationship (QSAR) modelling combined with machine learning (ML) offers a reliable computational alternative to traditional experimental testing, enabling rapid and cost-effective evaluation of chemical properties. This study presents a comparative analysis of multiple ML algorithms for biodegradability prediction using the QSAR biodegradation dataset, which consists of 1,055 compounds described by 41 molecular descriptors. To improve prediction accuracy and computational efficiency, the Minimum Redundancy Maximum Relevance (MRMR) algorithm was applied for feature selection, reducing descriptors to subsets of 37,33,29, and 25 optimized features. These subsets were used to train and evaluate a wide range of classifiers, including Decision Trees, Support Vector Machines (SVM), k-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Neural Networks, and Ensemble methods. The models were assessed using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. Results indicate that ensemble classifiers, particularly RusBoosted Trees, and Cosine KNN achieved superior accuracy of up to 88.2%, even with reduced feature sets. The findings demonstrate that MRMR-based feature optimization, when coupled with robust ML models, significantly enhances the predictive capability of QSAR-based biodegradability studies.

title: Scalable Machine Learning Feedback System for Adaptive Database Index Tuning Using Random Forest and Multi-Layer Perceptron Models
authors: Azmath Khan, K. Vamshi Babu, Lenin Kumar, D. Joseph Jeyakumar, Talari Swapna, K. Sharada
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: The increasing volumes of data used in contemporary applications have highlighted the necessity of database management systems (DBMS) that are effective in supporting high volumes of transactions and at the same time achieve the best response to queries. Implementing traditional methods of indexing in a static manner has been shown not to be dynamic enough to keep up with changing query patterns producing a decrease in performance. This paper suggests the scalable machine learning feedback model of adaptive database index tuning implementing the Randi Forest (RF) and Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) models to classify the predictive optimal indexing approach to query workloads. The system keeps track of database use, removes those important features like query type, frequency, and time of execution and offers adaptive feedback to index creation, modify or remove index. Synthetic and real-life databases were evaluated experimentally to the time of query response time, index maintenance overhead and throughput of the system. Its results indicate that the proposed hybrid solution can result in a query performance that is 35 percent faster than that of the traditional fixed indexes besides providing near scalable scalability to dynamic workloads. The paper highlights how machine learning can be useful in the database tuning exercise on a real-world setting, as well as the challenges facing machine learning in terms of computational overhead, selection of features and how it can interact with existing database management systems infrastructures. The next steps are the expansion to distributed databases and adaptive retrieval and indexing where reinforcement learning is used to drive continuous optimization.

title: System Architecture of a Real-Time E-Rickshaw Tracking and Availability Mobile Application Designed for University Transportation Management
authors: Mariatherasa V, R Sujitha, Nidhi Sharma, Sandhya Bhardwaj, Viraj Gadde, Shailendra Singh Sikarwar
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: Urban transportation is undergoing a transformation driven by digital innovation, with a strong focus on sustainability and operational efficiency. This research presents a Real-Time E-Rickshaw Tracking and Booking System, a cross-platform mobile solution aimed at improving accessibility and optimizing e-rickshaw operations in both urban and campus environments. The system employs a Flutter/React-based frontend for a seamless user experience, a Node.js/Express backend for robust data handling, and a MongoDB geospatial database for precise location management. Key features include real-time vehicle tracking via Google Maps API, ride booking, and dedicated dashboards for drivers, passengers, and administrators. The integration of WebSocket technology enables instant updates, while secure authentication mechanisms protect user data. Performance evaluations demonstrate high scalability, accuracy, and efficiency, highlighting the system's potential to strengthen last-mile connectivity and promote sustainable smart mobility solutions in modern urban ecosystems.

title: Explainable AI for Financial Fraud Detection
authors: Jai Dahiya, Alankrita Aggarwal, Devesh Ojha, Monu Kumar, Samridhi Singh
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Trends in Computing and Communication (ETCOM)
abstract: Fraud detection systems must strike a compromise between regulatory accountability and transparency and high predictive accuracy. This paper proposes an Explainable AI (XAI) framework for detecting and explaining fraudulent financial transactions by combining sophisticated interpretability techniques like SHAP, LIME, and DiCE with Gradient Boosting Machines (GBM). The suggested method guarantees model transparency by using feature attribution, counterfactual reasoning, and decision logic visualization, which helps stakeholders and auditors comprehend the behavior of the model. The robustness and fairness of the model are improved by dynamic data balancing with adaptive oversampling approaches. The study shows that including explainability into fraud detection improves trust, compliance, and the ethical use of AI in financial systems without compromising detection capabilities. It also showcases a replicable and legally compliant framework for contemporary fraud analytics.

title: Predicting Telecom Customer Churn with Combined Machine Learning Models
authors: Sayantan Sinha, Saket Maheshwari, Rick Chatterjee, Puneet Sharma, Saksham Saxena, Harsh Rajoria
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Trends in Computing and Communication (ETCOM)
abstract: One of the biggest challenges facing telecom companies in an effort to build customer retention and tailor services according to customers' needs is the prediction of customer turnover. This paper compares 21 customer-related attributes by which machine learning models used to predict telecom customer attrition are forecasted. The four models that we assess are Support Vector Machines (SVM), Random Forest, Logistic Regression, and a hybrid multimodel method using Artificial Neural Networks (ANN). These performance metrics involve accuracy, precision, recall, and the F1-score. From the outcome, it was found that a hybrid model of ANN could attain an accuracy score of 85%, but more conventional models such as Random Forest and Logistic Regression were generating good scores. This study will provide telecommunication firms with the vital information needed in the strategy of customer retention through an understanding of the importance of feature selection and possibilities for integrating multiple machine learning models in churn prediction.

title: Deep Learning-Based Denoising Fusion for Improving Taro Varietal Recognition in Noisy Field Images
authors: Aljon L. Abines
publish: 2025 IEEE Asia-Pacific Conference on Geoscience, Electronics and Remote Sensing Technology (AGERS)
abstract: Taro (Colocasia esculenta) is a staple crop in Southeast Asia and critical for food security in rural communities. Dagami, Leyte, Philippines, is a major taro-producing municipality. Field-collected images often suffer from uneven lighting, motion blur, and partial occlusion, which significantly degrade automatic varietal classification performance. This paper compares four deep learning denoisers, namely DnCNN, DAE-CAE, Restormer, and Uformer, and investigates different fusion strategies to improve classification on noisy conditions. Restormer was the best-performing single model, with PSNR =30.94 dB, SSIM=0.01, RMSE=0.029, while DAE-CAE showed signs of overfitting and weaker generalization. The fused model of DnCNN + Uformer demonstrated the best results for both restoration and classification: PSNR =25.37 dB, SSIM = 0.694, AF 1=0.1230, ABA=0.2057; it outperformed the raw images by 39.75% in terms of classification accuracy and by 17.07% in terms of balanced accuracy. Fusion leverages complementary denoiser strengths, preserving both global structure and fine details to improve image clarity for classification. This approach provides a low-cost, practical tool for reliable crop variety identification in farm settings. The framework supports sustainable digital agriculture and extends to other crops facing similar imaging challenges in varietal recognition.

title: Learning to Spot Stationarity: Classifying Transient vs. Steady CGL Fields with Random Forests and Convolutional Networks
authors: Imran Ali Mohammed
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Trends in Computing and Communication (ETCOM)
abstract: This paper formulates transient-steady discrimination in simulations of the complex Ginzburg–Landau (CGL) system as a supervised image classification problem and benchmarks three model families—logistic regression, random forests, and convolutional neural networks (CNNs). A labeled dataset of amplitude/phase snapshots is generated from controlled CGL simulations, with matched-pair design, standardized preprocessing, and 60/20/20 train-dev-test splits to prevent leakage and enable fair model selection. Models are trained with regularization/solver scans (logistic regression), tree-count tuning (random forests), and CNN hyperparameter sweeps (channels, kernels, batch normalization), and are evaluated using accuracy alongside PPV/NPV, F1, sensitivity, and specificity; feature weights, impurity reductions, and learned filters reveal that amplitude-driven defect density and boundary structure are key separability cues. Results show strong performance for random forests and tuned CNNs on held-out data, indicating that modern ML classifiers can reliably detect stationarity in pattern-forming dynamics and providing a reproducible baseline for AI-driven analysis of transient phenomena in physics-inspired systems.

title: Innovative Approaches to Dark Web Monitoring for Cybersecurity Intelligence
authors: Anusha Reddy, K. Santhi Sri, Gayatri Ketepalli
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Trends in Computing and Communication (ETCOM)
abstract: The Dark Web Monitoring Tool (DWMT) is intended to identify, monitor, and assess threats, illegal activity, and security lapses on dark web platforms. This tool focusses on tracking illicit trade, identity theft, data breaches, and cybercrime-all of which frequently take place in encrypted and anonymous settings. To find sensitive information about a company, a person, or vital infrastructure, the DWMT searches dark web forums, marketplaces, and communication channels using advanced web scraping techniques, artificial intelligence (AI), and machine learning algorithms. The tool's features include risk assessment, real-time alerts, and the creation of useful insights to lessen possible risks. For businesses, cybersecurity experts, and law enforcement agencies, it offers a complete, automated solution to protect sensitive data, strengthen cybersecurity posture, and fight cybercrime.

title: Towards Accessible Home Safety Assessment: An AI-Driven Mobile XR Application for Older Adults
authors: Yu-Ju Liao, Chang Liu, Hiroaki Ueshima, Kazumasa Kishimoto, Yukiko Mori, Goshiro Yamamoto, Tomohiro Kuroda
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and eXtended and Virtual Reality (AIxVR)
abstract: Falls at home are a major concern for aging populations, but traditional occupational therapy assessments are time-intensive and resource-demanding. In this study, we introduce the preliminary validation of a mobile device-based home risk assessment tool that combines AI-driven semantic scene reconstruction, LiDAR-based spatial mapping, and deep learning-based object detection. A pilot study in three real bathrooms demonstrated substantial inter-rater reliability (Fleiss's <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\kappa =0.73-0.88)$</tex>, promising overall accuracy (0.90), and significant time reduction (62%) compared to manual assessment. While measurement inaccuracies and object detection errors remain challenging, our preliminary findings suggest that this approach enables non-professionals to perform rapid, bathroom-specific risk assessments. This paper describes our system design, initial validation results, identified technical limitations, and proposed enhancement strategies.

title: CNN-to-ViT Distillation for Efficient Remote Sensing Scene Classification
authors: Cao Vu Bui, Quoc Trinh Vo
publish: 2025 International Conference on Big Data and Data Mining (BDDM)
abstract: Remote sensing scene classification (RSSC) has increasingly benefited from Vision Transformers (ViTs) due to their strong ability to capture long-range spatial dependencies. However, lightweight ViTs typically underperform compared to high-capacity convolutional neural networks (CNNs), particularly when training data are limited or when models must operate on resource-constrained platforms such as UAVs and on-board satellite systems. Knowledge distillation (KD) provides a practical pathway to bridge this gap by transferring knowledge from a strong teacher network to a compact student model. This paper investigates a simple yet effective CNN-to-ViT distillation framework designed to enhance the performance of lightweight Transformers for RSSC. A high-capacity RegNetY16GF CNN is used as the teacher, while a DeiT-Tiny ViT serves as the student. The student learns jointly from ground-truth labels and softened teacher predictions through temperature-scaled Kullback-Leibler divergence, enabling the transfer of the CNN's inductive biases and decision structure without relying on additional feature- or attention-level supervision. Experiments on the EuroSAT benchmark demonstrate that the distilled ViT significantly improves over its non-distilled counterpart, achieving accuracy closer to larger models while preserving excellent computational efficiency. These results high-light that CNN-to-ViT distillation is a promising and lightweight strategy for deploying Transformer-based models in practical Earth observation and remote sensing applications.

title: Explainable AI for Trustworthy Predictions: The Comparative Analysis of SHAP, LIME, and Counterfactuals
authors: SriHarsha Anand Pushkala
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Recent Trends in Electrical, Electronics and Computing Technologies (ICRTEECT)
abstract: As artificial intelligence becomes increasingly embedded in critical sectors, the demand for transparency in model predictions has intensified. Explainable AI (XAI) techniques, especially SHAP, LIME, and counterfactual explanations, have emerged as essential tools for deciphering complex model behaviour. This study presents a comparative analysis of these methods based on recent literature (2023–2025), outlining their respective advantages and limitations. SHAP offers both global and local interpretability, with a solid theoretical foundation; however, it is computationally demanding and assumes feature independence. LIME delivers rapid, model-agnostic local explanations, though it can suffer from instability and lacks theoretical guarantees. Counterfactual explanations resonate with human reasoning by illustrating how minimal changes affect outcomes, yet they often face challenges regarding feasibility and standardized evaluation. We highlight the promise of hybrid approaches and advocate for the development of standardized, uncertainty-aware evaluation frameworks to ensure the trustworthy deployment of AI across diverse domains.

title: Agri Lifeline: Empowering the Farmers
authors: Pathmanaban J, Aswin R, Udhaya Kiran M V, Sachein D, Somasundaram K, Kaliappan S
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Recent Trends in Electrical, Electronics and Computing Technologies (ICRTEECT)
abstract: "Empowering the Farmers" is a Powerful Sentence in this modern world. Crop(s) are vulnerable to the disease- causing agents and the pests which demolish the entire livelihood of the husbandman. To solve this, an existing system approaches the grower to use the fertilizers such as calcium, potassium, zinc, etc., can give a tolerance to the crops from pests and disease-causing agents. However, this is still developing known as modern fertilizers which is more effective even in the evolution of agents and pests against the traditional medicines. But an unorganized way of approach, unavoidable decisions and expensive solutions are the major factors for the cultivators to lose their earnings and time. Wrong disease classifications and traditional remedies leads to loss of pay, unwavering efforts and loss of mental peace.
