title: Artificial Intelligence Driven Oversampling Technique for Addressing Imbalanced Data
authors: Shaheen Layaq, T.Sampath Kumar
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Recent Trends in Electrical, Electronics and Computing Technologies (ICRTEECT)
abstract: Imbalanced data signifies a situation at which the distribution among classes within a dataset is not uniform it means a single class (majority class) contain many samples than that of comparative class (minority class). On this inequality data if classification is performed it leads to misleading result as traditional machine learning methods were dependent towards the majority class. As a result, minority dataset is been ignored which is actually more important to consider than majority class. To address this problem many solutions were present. Among all oversampling the minority class methods are mostly used. But, traditional oversampling methods faces problem with scalability, overfitting due to redundant samples, synthetic samples may not represent true distribution, ignoring noisy and borderline instances, increased computational complexity, potential for misleading model evaluation, and risk of generating irrelevant samples. To overcome the problem of earlier oversampling methods AI based oversampling technique IDR_CTGAN (Imbalanced Data Reduction using Conditional Tabular Generative Adversarial Networks) is proposed. Initially the imbalanced dataset is extracted, minority and majority samples are separated and minority dataset is passed to the CTGAN (to produce synthetic minority data). By using CTGAN indirectly the accuracy improves as it balances the dataset and makes the classifier more fair. Software Defect Prediction imbalanced dataset from PROMISE repository is considered, by using python programming the implementation is done and the performance measures are calculated. The final output result is displayed in tabular format and by analyzing it was found that our proposed algorithm showed good performance than previous methods.

title: Enhanced Productivity of Tubular Solar Stills by Using Machine Learning Model
authors: Sandeep Kumar Shah, Anurag Kulshreshtha, Tejkaran Naroliya, Ashish Kumar Shrivastava
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: Directing attention to the crises of fresh water production in the burgeoning global population and expanding economical activity a greater demand of fresh water and putting notable pressure on the planet's natural water resources. The greater demand of clean water for drinking has turn out to be a serious issue in the worldwide. Tubular solar stills (TSS) are the systems designed to avail solar energy for desalination or accumulate the fresh water productions. These systems typically consist a series of parallel tubes in which water flows through the tubes covered by transparent glass materials that allows sunrays to strike directly to increase the evaporation rate. Recent advancements in TSS technology include such as Heat storage materials (SHMs), wick material, PCM, Nano materials and Nano-fluids, and New promising geometrical designs. In addition, Cumulative yield of CTSS without NPGO is 4.65 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{kg} / \mathrm{m} 2$</tex> and Cumulative yield of MTSS is <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$6.6 ~\text{kg} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$</tex> so the percentage of cumulative yield is increased up to 41.94 % in MTSS, hourly productivity is increased up to 42.62 % and Thermal efficiency of CTSS is 49 % and in MTSS with NPGO, Its Thermal efficiency is increased up to 70.2 %. Overall the wide range of innovations are highlighted in this paper emphasizes repeatedly attempts to enhance solar stills for more for more effective and environmental friendly solar water distiller. The authors conclude with their insights and predictions on the future development of TSS Technology.

title: Decentralized continual learning: A Multi-objective Minimization technique
authors: Yara Zgheib, Gustavo Faia Fagundes, Marc Antonini, Roula Nassif
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: In this work, we formulate decentralized continual learning using multi-objective optimization. For a given inference task t (which corresponds to the shared minimizer of all agents’ individual cost functions), agents collecting data in a streaming manner can carry out local computations and collaborations. As the tasks evolve over time, agents must adapt to new tasks without forgetting old ones. To address the stability-plasticity dilemma, agents randomly store data from past tasks, and through a proper multi-objective formulation, this data is incorporated into the learning process. The resulting fully-decentralized continual learning approach is analyzed (in the mean-square-error sense) under some general assumptions on the individual costs and gradient noise processes. Finally, the theoretical findings and the effectiveness of the proposed approach are illustrated through simulations.

title: Binary Sentiment Classification on the IMDB Movie Review Dataset Using an Ensemble Approach
authors: Ravi Dongre, Himanshu Yadav, Ajit Kumar Shrivastava
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: This is an information age, where data plays a crucial role in predicting business performance. In this work, the primary goal is to analyse movie reviews to predict whether a review is positive or negative. By classifying reviews, we can provide end users with meaningful insights such as the star rating or overall quality of a movie Text data classification plays a major role in taking decisions from the given text content. In this paper the ensemble approach has been used with three different models. This method uses the voting classifier in ensemble technique which shows the ensemble technique gives the batter results as complier to Random Forest, linear regression and Xgboost. The work has been performed on IMDB Movie review dataset.

title: A Parallel ANN Framework for Transmission Pricing Prediction in Competitive Spot Power Market
authors: Abhishek Saxena, Abhishek Tripathi, Mohit Deshpande
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: This paper proposes a novel approach of transmission price prediction through Levenberg Marquardt Artificial Neural Network using parallel computing. As the transmission pricing for various loads and generators varies in wide range in large power system, more than one ANN have been developed for accurate prediction of transmission pricing with the use of parallel computing. Effectiveness of the proposed approach has been demonstrated on Garver's 6-bus test System In the system, the developed ANNs provided very fast and accurate prediction of transmission pricing. Artificial Neural network based transmission price prediction is not mentioned anywhere else, hence a comparative study of the work may not be possible.

title: Multi-Scale Power Demand Prediction for Risk Prevention of Medium and Low Voltage Power Distribution Systems
authors: Zhifang Zhu, Haisheng Hong, Jing Chen, Tingjie Lu, Hanyu Li, Xian Tang
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: For the multi-scale and multi-level risk prediction task in the power distribution network, this paper proposes a hybrid machine learning-based prediction framework with the extreme value of distribution transformer load rate as the core indicator. It is used for hierarchical and phased load rate prediction, covering multiple power management levels (low-voltage branch level, distribution transformer level, and grid area level) and multiple time scales (day, week, and month). During the model construction, various external factors were considered, including weather, geography, expansion and construction plan for power facilities, and an overview of regional power supply characteristics. Social perception data and meteorological data were used to assist in enhancing the ability to perceive the operational situation of the distribution network. The proposed solution is assessed through experiments and the numerical results have confirmed the effectiveness of the proposed solution.

title: Optimizing AI Training Sustainability Through Renewable Energy Integration in Data Centers
authors: Ravi Chandra
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Trends in Computing and Communication (ETCOM)
abstract: The rapid progress of what is referred to as Artificial Intelligence (AI) has suggested a rise in the power required in the computational Soap Fires, mainly to greater “Energy Consumption” (EC) and higher “Carbon Emissions” (CE) levels in the so-called Data Centers (DCs). Due to the larger resource requirement for training AI models, environmental sustainability has now become a major concern. Renewable Energy (RE) and Carbon Footprint (CF) monitoring are examined in this paper as tools to market the sustainable AIdriven DC. Mixed method has been chosen, wherein a mixture of structured surveys of 121 professionals was employed along with secondary data from the academic literature, industry reports, and case studies. The analysis carried out with surveydriven data and statistical tools, including descriptive analysis, reliability testing, and ordinal regression, is a research effort that evaluates the effectiveness of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R E}$</tex> adoption and compares it with other sustainability metrics. The results indicate that even though there is a consistent idea of RE integration with green AI efforts, its impact on the decrease of CE and EC is not statistically significant. Still, the results highlight the crucial role of carbon monitoring systems and Energy-Efficient (EE) practices as the principal enablers of the sustainable AI ecosystem. The research emphasizes the necessity of multifaceted strategies, increased policy support, and long-term investment in the case of the implementation of the environmentally aware AI development.

title: AI-Driven Cyber-Physical Systems and IoT for Enabling Intelligent, Secure, and Adaptive Ecosystems
authors: Ravikumar Thallapalli, B. Prabhanjan Yadav, Boggarapu Srinivasulu, P. Venkateshwarlu, M.Srinivas, E.Kiran Kumar
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Recent Trends in Electrical, Electronics and Computing Technologies (ICRTEECT)
abstract: The Internet of Things (IoT) and Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are pacesetters to advance intelligent and connected environments, and their ubiquitous adoption will bring radical impact to critical domains including smart cities, healthcare, and autonomous systems. The transformative impact of Artificial Intelligence (AI) on the CPS-IoT ecosystems is investigated in this paper. With AI, real time decision making, predictive maintenance, and even system optimisation is possible — all possible while solving the problem of scalability, latency, adaptability in highly dynamic environments. This paper provides unique insights into the convergence of AI, CPSs and IoT by presenting novel technologies like edge computing, digital twins, and AI-based cybersecurity. Overall, these advances illustrate the potential of AI-driven CPSs to realize next generation smarter, safer and more efficient systems. Finally, the paper also discusses critical challenges, primarily related to data privacy, ethical considerations as well as interoperability and suggests possible ways to overcome these barriers. The main findings underscore AI’s central importance to next generation CPS-IoT systems and suggest a pathway for building secure, adaptive, and sustainable infrastructures.

title: Square Microstrip Patch Antenna Design for IoT Systems Operating in the C-Band
authors: Vandana Sharma, Geetam Shukla
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: At present, wireless networks are predominantly based on fourth-generation (4 G) technology, even though fifthgeneration <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(5 \mathrm{G})$</tex> communication systems are under active development and deployment. While many contemporary software applications continue to depend on the established 4 G infrastructure, research efforts directed toward the IoT and other emerging technologies are still in their early stages. The proposed antenna has physical dimensions of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$30 \times 30 \times 1.6 \text{mm}^{3}$</tex>, making it a suitable candidate for integration into portable and miniaturized communication devices. Simulation results indicate that the antenna resonates effectively at a frequency of 7.47 GHz while achieving a usable bandwidth of 0.56 GHz, which is sufficient to support reliable high-speed data transmission. The antenna further exhibits a Voltage Standing Wave Ratio (VSWR) of 1.44, indicating good impedance matching and minimal reflection losses within the operational frequency range. Additionally, the structure demonstrates a relatively high directivity of 7.29 dBi, signifying its ability to focus radiated energy in a preferred direction, which is highly advantageous for improving communication efficiency in 5G environments.

title: Intelligent Lane Detection with Machine Learning
authors: Priyanka Kaushik, Jayapal Lande, Anshu Chaudhari, Anandakumar Haldorai, Deepsikha Mehra, Aviash Kumar Namdeo
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: The lane detection is the basic aspect of modern autonomous driving systems and advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS). It allows vehicles to perceive their environment and maintain a lane discipline. This article represents a robust and efficient detection framework for lane detection using machine learning techniques focusing on a convolutional neural network (CNN) trained on various data sets on the road scene. The proposed model shows powerful performance across various lighting and weather conditions that deals with common challenges such as occlusion, faded lanes and complex road topology. The system reaches the level of inference in real time and high accuracy in the scenarios of cities and motorways. Extensive experiments and ablation studies confirm the efficiency of the model, so it is suitable for deployment in the real world in autonomous navigation systems.

title: Design and Verification of Gray Coded FSM based 3D NoC Router using Verilog HDL
authors: Yadvendra Prasad Dwivedi, Somesh Kumar
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: As transistor dimensions shrink and operating frequencies rise, conventional on-chip interconnects like point-to-point and shared buses struggle to meet the scalability, latency, and energy efficiency demands of modern multi-core systems. Network-on-Chip (NoC) provides a structured and scalable communication paradigm to overcome these limitations. In this work, we present a 3D NoC router design based on a Gray coded Finite State Machine (FSM) to minimize power consumption by reducing switching activity. The FSM uses Gray coding to ensure that only one bit changes during state transitions. The proposed architecture is verified through functional simulation and synthesized using FPGA platforms. Comparative analysis shows significant improvements in logic utilization and power consumption over existing 2D and 3D NoC router architectures.

title: Anomaly Detection in Resilient Networks: On the Impact of Delayed and False Decisions
authors: Friedemann Laue, Maximilian Lübke, Norman Franchi
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: This paper studies a resilient communication network that monitors a key performance indicator (KPI) to detect anomalies and disruptions. Upon detection, the network automatically reconfigures to a recovery state, ensuring reduced but stable performance. To analyze the impact of the detection and recovery process on the network resilience, we propose two discrete-time Markov chains (DTMCs) that model the network dynamics under both normal and challenging conditions. The proposed DTMCs account for the effects of delayed and false decisions in the detection process and model the recovery capabilities of the considered network. Based on the statistical properties of the DTMCs, we derive a cost function to evaluate the long-term performance of the network, serving as a measure for resilience. In addition, we evaluate the performance of an exemplary network using a Gaussian signal model for the KPI and a jamming detector from the literature. Our results reveal that the proposed cost function is minimized at low probabilities of false alarm and relatively high probabilities of miss detection, indicating that false decisions can significantly affect the network performance. Furthermore, our evaluation shows that shorter measurement intervals for the KPI and faster recovery from disruptions improve the resilience of the considered network.

title: Optimizing Peak Age under Intermittent Satellite Connectivity and Store-and-Forward
authors: Çağrı Arı, Özkan Tuğberk Kartal, Andrea Munari, Leonardo Badia, Elif Uysal, Onur Kaya
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: We consider a real-time task-oriented application operating over an intermittently available satellite-based communication network, aiming to collect status updates generated by a remote sensing device. The system is modeled as a scheduling problem over a finite horizon, corresponding to the duration of the task, to minimize the peak Age of Information at the destination. The number of updates that can be transmitted is constrained by a transmission budget. Moreover, the status updates are subject to delays caused by the store-and-forward operation of the satellites, which may vastly vary depending on the network conditions. We investigate three levels of awareness regarding the connectivity conditions of the satellite network: (i) scheduling without any information about connectivity conditions, (ii) scheduling based solely on the current conditions, and (iii) scheduling based on full connectivity knowledge. The first case admits a relatively simple structure, for which a periodic transmission strategy is adopted. The latter two cases are formulated as semi-Markov decision processes and solved to obtain the optimal transmission scheduling policy. Simulation results demonstrate the impact of connectivity awareness on the application performance at the destination. Through a simple modeling approach, we provide first insights into the practically relevant setting of store-and-forward satellite architectures.

title: Rethinking Chronological Causal Discovery with Signal Processing
authors: Kurt Butler, Damian Machlanski, Panagiotis Dimitrakopoulos, Sotirios A. Tsaftaris
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: Causal discovery problems use a set of observations to deduce causality between variables in the real world, typically to answer questions about biological or physical systems. These observations are often recorded at regular time intervals, determined by a user or a machine, depending on the experiment design. There is generally no guarantee that the timing of these recordings matches the timing of the underlying biological or physical events. In this paper, we examine the sensitivity of causal discovery methods to this potential mismatch. We consider empirical and theoretical evidence to understand how causal discovery performance is impacted by changes of sampling rate and window length. We demonstrate that both classical and recent causal discovery methods exhibit sensitivity to these hyperparameters, and we discuss how ideas from signal processing may help us understand these phenomena.

title: Accelerating K-Means: A Vectorized Approach for AI Engines & Neural Processing Units
authors: Eleonora Cabai, Giuseppe Sorrentino, Marco Domenico Santambrogio, Davide Conficconi
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Field-Programmable Logic and Applications (FPL)
abstract: K-Means is a clustering technique widely employed in AI workloads, from image processing to data mining. Given its importance, researchers propose different algorithms and hardware-accelerated implementations. While algorithm suitability can depend on the target use case, there is much less doubt about the architecture: FPGAs are the de facto standard, as the design can be perfectly tailored to the target use case. Despite this, AI accelerators such as GPUs and Neural Processing Units (NPUs) are gaining traction. The former attains remarkable performance at the cost of low energy efficiency. The latter, instead, promises to maximize both, but they are strongly underutilized due to the lack of a clear approach for K-Means acceleration. Considering AMD NPU, for example, the main computing cores are AI Engines that require algorithm reshaping and code optimization to harness data parallelism effectively. Thus, this research analyzes different K-Means versions to propose a vectorized algorithm that fully uses AI Engine (AIE) features. We validate our vectorized K-Means on Versal VCK5000, using FPGAs for data movement only, as the Memory Transfer Engines and Shim Tiles of NPUs, and the AI Engine for computation. This design reflects features of modern NPUs, making the validation fair. We attain up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$59.5 \times$</tex> speedup against Torch library on GPUs while being comparable but more energy efficient than further optimized GPU solutions.

title: Performance Analysis of Point Cloud Classification Techniques Using UAV LiDAR Data
authors: Calvin Wijaya, Ruli Andaru, Harintaka, Catur Aries Rokhmana
publish: 2025 IEEE Asia-Pacific Conference on Geoscience, Electronics and Remote Sensing Technology (AGERS)
abstract: Point cloud data has become increasingly important with the growing interest in 3D modeling and spatial analysis. As the foundational dataset for 3D model generation, accurate classification of point clouds remains a critical yet challenging task. This research evaluates and compares three approaches to point cloud classification: conventional geometrybased methods, machine learning algorithms, and deep learning techniques. The dataset used in this study was acquired using UAV-based LiDAR with a point density of 54.866 points per square meters. For the machine learning approach, Random Forest (RF) and Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGB) algorithms were applied using RGB features extracted from the point cloud. For the deep learning approach, PointNet and Dynamic Graph Convolutional Neural Network (DGCNN) models were implemented and evaluated. The results demonstrate that deep learning algorithms achieve the highest classification accuracy, with DGCNN reaching 93% overall accuracy, followed by PointNet at approximately 89%. However, the machine learning methods exhibited lower overall performance. While deep learning yields superior accuracy, it requires significantly more computational resources, highlighting a trade-off between performance and computational efficiency. These findings underscore the importance of accurate point cloud classification, which serves as a critical foundation for subsequent processes such as 3D city model generation from UAV LiDAR data and other spatial analysis applications.

title: Performance Comparison of YOLO Architectures for Lung Cancer Detection in Medical Imaging
authors: Shital D. Bhatt, Mitesh B. Astik
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: This study aims to develop and evaluate independent deep learning models capable of distinguishing between cancerous and non-cancerous nodules in computed tomography (CT) scans. Three advanced object detection algorithms—YOLOv3, YOLOv4, and YOLOv5—were trained and validated using fourteen distinct lung CT datasets to ensure model robustness and generalization. The models' performance was assessed using standard evaluation metrics, including precision, recall, F1-score, and mean Average Precision (mAP). Comparative analysis revealed that YOLOv5 achieved superior detection accuracy compared to YOLOv3 and YOLOv4. Specifically, YOLOv5 attained a precision of 90%, a recall of 80%, an F1-score of 0.85, and mAP of 85%. These results demonstrate YOLOv5's enhanced capability in effectively identifying lung nodules, thereby reducing false detections and improving diagnostic reliability. The findings suggest that the YOLOv5 model holds significant potential for integration into computer-aided diagnostic systems to support radiologists in early lung cancer detection.

title: Industrial Sustainability in Plywood Manufacturing: A Fuzzy-ISM Approach to Optimize Quality Factors
authors: Kiran Vijayan, K R Kiron, V R Pramod
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: Industrial sustainability is one of the major goals need to achieve in the current global scenario. Comparing with large scale industries SME sectors are striving to attain sustainability in their production. Plywood manufacturing sector is one among the crucial SME in this category based on its substantial contribution to the nations GDP. Ensuring high quality in plywood products is essential for maintaining this contribution. This work related to Fuzzy-ISM methodology used to optimize and prioritize the quality-related issues in plywood manufacturing SME and its impact on the attainment of sustainability principles. A comprehensive literature review revealed that sixteen key issues are predominantly affect the quality of this composite. Prioritization of these issues can be used to identify and strategize more sustainable production practices in their industry. Conducting Fuzzy-ISM methodology among these factors revealed that market-driven compromises on plywood costs with 8.79 IP vale is central to the emergence of quality problems in these SMEs. Addressing this core issue will mitigate the occurrence of other quality issues too. This methodology provides a foundation for researchers aiming to develop new production techniques in plywood SMEs that enhance product quality while ensuring industrial sustainability.

title: Multimodal Smart Video Surveillance System for Real-Time Detection of Anomalous Activities
authors: Mohideen Badhusha, Yumlembam Henba Singh, Ansil Kumar, Arun, Darshan B
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Trends in Computing and Communication (ETCOM)
abstract: The growing need for intelligent CCTV has spurred research and development for automated anomaly detection from video streams. This paper describes an end-to-end multimodal smart video surveillance system capable of detecting three key anomalous activities: fire detection, weapon detection, and fall detection, in real time. The system we propose combines state-of-the-art deep learning models like YOLO variants and its derivatives, CNNs, and pose estimation models to create a fast and reliable monitoring system. The system is implemented as a software module easily compatible with any present CCTV infrastructure. Experimental outcomes show considerable improvement over existing works with 94.7% accuracy, 93.8% precision, 95.6% recall, and 94.7% F1-score for detecting fire; 92.3% accuracy, 92.3% precision, 90.1% recall, and 91.2% F1-score for detecting weapon; and 96.1% accuracy, 95.8% precision, 96.4% recall, and 96.1% F1-score for detecting fall while achieving 30 FPS processing speed at a standard hardware level. The Spatio-Temporal Priority Fusion (STPF) framework boosts detection reliability substantially and decreases false positives compared to single-modality based detection systems.

title: Analysis of Real-Time Human Identification Using Edge Computing
authors: Souradeep Datta, Abhishek Kumar, Sachin Ahuja
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Trends in Computing and Communication (ETCOM)
abstract: Real-time human identification is a highly important feature of modern smart systems in terms of surveillance, access control, healthcare monitoring, and autonomous operation. The development of edge computing paradigms has made it possible to implement decentralized processing architectures to tackle latency limitations, bandwidth constraints, and privacy demands of cloud-centric architectures. The entire survey methodically reviews the use of the state-of-the-art approaches to implement human identifying systems in the edge computing platform with special attention to the deep learning models such as the introduction of the YOLO based object identification, convolutional neural networks to identify faces and methods to optimize resource consumption to implement the systems. This paper represents a synthesis of the recent developments in lightweight model architecture, federated learning system models, and privacy preserving methods by studying 35 publications published in the year 2016-2025. Critical analysis demonstrates that major issues such as the limitations with computational resources, accuracyefficiency trade-offs, and scalability are still present. The results show that, although big strides have been made in the field of model compression and edge-optimized architectures, there are still enormous research gaps on the strong real-life deployment, adversarial resilience, and privacy-conscious adaptation mechanisms.

title: VLSI Architecture of a Deep Unfolded ADMM-based Infinity Norm MIMO Detection
authors: Md Salman Sakib, Shahriar Shahabuddin
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: We introduce a deep unfolded ADMM based infinity norm (ADMIN) multiple input multiple output (MIMO) detector for uplink massive MIMO systems with small base station to user antenna ratios, along with a corresponding VLSI architecture. Starting from ADMIN, we unroll K = 7 iterations and replace the hand tuned parameters with values generated by a lightweight neural network. This lightweight affine head is trained end to end to learn β<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">k</inf>, γ<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">k</inf> across iterations and minimize the BER. We design a VLSI architecture of the neural network employing multipliers followed by an adder tree for accumulation. To support efficient hardware deployment, we also apply fixed point quantization. The design was synthesized on a Xilinx XCVU37P device and operated at a clock frequency of 100 MHz. Simulations for 8 × 8 MIMO with QPSK over show that deep unfolded ADMIN consistently outperforms linear MMSE and standard ADMIN. Typical gains are approximately 3–4 dB versus MMSE and about 1 dB versus unrolled ADMIN. Overall, the proposed architecture pair delivers high performance with hardware friendly complexity for next generation base station receivers.

title: Enhanced Uplink Data Detection in Massive MIMO with 1-Bit ADCs: Analysis and Joint Detection
authors: Amin Radbord, Italo Atzeni, Antti Tölli
abstract: We present a new analytical framework for the uplink data detection in massive multiple-input multiple-output systems with 1-bit analog-to-digital converters (ADCs). We first characterize the expected values of the soft-estimated symbols (after the linear receiver and prior to the data detection), which are affected by 1-bit quantization during both the channel estimation and the uplink data transmission. In our analysis, we consider conventional receivers such as maximum ratio combining (MRC), zero forcing, and minimum mean squared error (MMSE), with multiple user equipments (UEs) and correlated Rayleigh fading. Additionally, we design a linear minimum mean dispersion (LMMD) receiver tailored for data detection with 1-bit ADCs, which exploits the expected values of the soft-estimated symbols previously derived. Then, we propose a joint data detection (JD) strategy that exploits the interdependence among the soft-estimated symbols of the interfering UEs, analyzing its symbol error rate (SER), and subsequently introduce a low-complexity variant. These strategies are compared with robust maximum likelihood data detection with 1-bit ADCs. Numerical results examining the SER show that MMSE exhibits considerable performance gains over MRC, whereas the proposed LMMD receiver significantly outperforms the conventional receivers. Lastly, the proposed JD and its low-complexity variant provide a significant boost in comparison with the UE-specific data detection.

title: Quench Propagation Characteristic of REBCO Stacks Under Conduction-Cooled Conditions
authors: Qing Pang, Hui Yu, Yafei Tao, Bowen Xie, Bohan Tang, Shili Jiang, Xiangyang Wu, Donghui Jiang, Guangli Kuang
abstract: Due to the limited current-carrying capacity of a single high-temperature superconducting (HTS) tape, multiple tapes are often connected in parallel for high-field applications. Therefore, investigating the quench propagation characteristics of HTS stacks is crucial for ensuring the safe operation of HTS devices. Compared with liquid nitrogen immersion cooling, conduction-cooled quench tests are performed in a quasi adiabatic environment, minimizing environmental effects and allowing the results to more accurately reflect the intrinsic quench characteristics of the HTS stack. In this study, we investigate the quench propagation characteristics of REBCO stacks under conduction-cooled conditions. The stacks were fabricated using 4 mm-wide REBCO tapes, and a conduction cooled quench test platform was built based on a GM cryocooler to perform measurements under near-adiabatic conditions. Potential taps were placed continuously along the length of the sample to monitor quench propagation, while thermocouples were attached to measure the temperature distribution. The minimum quench energy (MQE) and normal zone propagation velocity (NZPV) of the REBCO stacks were measured at ambient temperatures ranging from 60 K to 77 K. The experimental results are compared with those of a single tape sample. To verify the thermal behavior of the REBCO stack, a numerical analysis of the temperature trace was performed using the Finite Element Method (FEM). The numerical simulation results were compared with the experimental data to verify the validity of the experimental data. The results indicate that differences in the coefficient of thermal expansion between the solder and the tapes during the cooling process led to a reduction in the critical current of the REBCO stack. As the temperature increases, both types of samples exhibit a decrease in MQE and a slowdown in NZPV. The heat absorption of the solder during quench increases the MQE of the stacked HTS tapes. Meanwhile, it also enhances the Joule heating power within the normal zone, and accelerates the NZPV.

title: Critical Evaluation of Time Parameters for Lightning Impulse Voltages Using Pareto Front
authors: Shanmukha Reddy Vippala, Gururaj S. Punekar, Krishnan Chemmangat
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Double exponential waves are commonly used test voltages in lightning tests of electrical power system components and electromagnetic interference (EMI) studies. The conventional mathematical model employed to represent lightning impulse voltage waveforms, characterised by the time features front time (Tf) and tail time (Tt), is the double exponential equation, which incorporates the parameters α and β. The simulated non-standard lightning impulse (N-SLI) voltage waveforms with varying Tf and Tt can be analysed to estimate breakdown voltages (BDV) and breakdown times of high-voltage (HV) equipment insulation. Notably, there are no direct analytical formulations linking the parameters α and β to the specific time characteristics Tf and Tt. The present study utilises a Pareto front-based methodology, facilitated by a genetic algorithm (GA) optimisation tool, to critically evaluate the time features Tf and Tt of lightning impulse voltages. The findings indicate that when the ratio Tt/Tf is equal to or exceeds 2.68, the optimisation yields a unique uncompromised solution while estimating the parameters α and β that correspond to the desired values of Tf and Tt. Conversely, when the ratio Tt/Tf falls below 2.68, the optimisation yields a Pareto optimal set of (α, β) pairs, each representing a distinct trade-off among the competing objectives. Contour plots further validate these results.

title: A Survey on the Coding for DNA Storage: Challenges, Algorithms, and Future Directions
authors: Liwei Mu, Ling Liu, Lulu Ding, Zexuan Zhu
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: DNA-based data storage has developed rapidly in recent years and is increasingly viewed as a promising solution for long-term, ultra-dense archival storage. As research moves beyond early demonstrations, encoding has become a central technical challenge that directly affects system reliability, scalability, and practical deployment. This survey presents a structured overview of coding techniques for DNA data storage. We first outline the biochemical and structural constraints of DNA storage, including synthesis and sequencing errors, molecular instability, and context-dependent biases, which strongly influence encoding design. We then review how classical error-correcting codes—such as Reed–Solomon, BCH, convolutional, fountain, and LDPC codes—have been adapted for use in molecular storage systems. Next, we introduce emerging coding approaches designed specifically for DNA storage, including ALD and Damerau distance codes, set-based coding models, and biochemical rule–guided methods such as Yin–Yang coding. Beyond error correction, the survey also examines coding strategies aimed at improving efficiency, with attention to storage rate, biochemical feasibility, and structural robustness. By comparing representative encoding schemes across different channel conditions and application scenarios, this survey summarizes existing theoretical analyses and experimental results and provides practical guidance for selecting suitable codes. Finally, we discuss open challenges and future research directions, highlighting the potential of adaptive, secure, and machine learning–assisted encoding techniques for large-scale DNA data storage systems.

title: Identifying prognosis-associated spatial patterns by integrating bulk RNA-seq and spatial transcriptomic data
authors: Yuxin Miao, Yanhong Wu, Xinqi Li, Wenbo Guo, Cheng Chen, Jin Gu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Biology and Bioinformatics
abstract: The latest spatial transcriptomics (ST) technology can characterize spatial-resolved intra-tumor heterogeneities, and while large-scale traditional bulk transcriptomic datasets possess valuable clinical phenotype information. To link spatial features with survival information, we present stSurvTrans, a deep transfer learning framework for prognosis-associated spatial patterns identification. stSurvTrans harmonizes bulk RNA sequencing (RNA-seq) and ST data based on conditional variational autoencoder (CVAE), and utilizes a Weibull module to transfer clinical survival information from bulk samples to ST data. We benchmarked stSurvTrans on three simulated datasets, demonstrating its accuracy and superiority. Furthermore, in ST data from hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), stSurvTrans identified the bile duct tumor thrombus, a spatial structure associated with worse prognosis.

title: Dual-Gate Architecture-Boosted 800-ppi IGZO ISTFT Array With Super-Nernstian Sensitivity for On-Chip Ion Imaging and Parallel DNA Detection
authors: Zikang Mei, Wei Tang, Jun Li, Kaiyutai Zhou, Ziwen Chen, Xiao Peng, Wei Jiang, Xiaojun Guo
abstract: Ion-sensitive thin-film transistors (ISTFTs) face a fundamental tradeoff between pH sensitivity and pixel resolution. To overcome this limitation, this work presents a novel ISTFT with super-Nernstian sensitivity and ultrahigh resolution using a dual-gate (DG) architecture. By synergizing capacitive coupling amplification with oxygen plasma-enhanced surface-chemistry engineering, the DG ISTFT array achieves a sensitivity of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$110~\pm ~20$</tex-math> </inline-formula> mV/pH. Both TCAD simulations and experimental characterization demonstrate that this amplification is dimension-independent, ensuring scalability for high-density integration. Consequently, a fully functional 800-ppi <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$256\times 256$</tex-math> </inline-formula> active-matrix ISTFT array with integrated gate-driver circuits is realized, enabling real-time pH imaging. Furthermore, the platform serves as a parallel DNA biochip for loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) to realize label-free detection of multiple foodborne pathogens. This work provides a foundry-compatible path toward a high-throughput multiplexed ISTFT array, bridging advanced sensor design with practical on-chip bioanalytical applications.

title: Improved Constructions of Linear Codes for Insertions and Deletions
authors: Roee Gross, Roni Con, Eitan Yaakobi
abstract: In this work, we study linear error-correcting codes against adversarial insertion-deletion (indel) errors. While most constructions for the indel model are nonlinear, linear codes offer compact representations, efficient encoding, and decoding algorithms, making them highly desirable. A key challenge in this area is achieving rates close to the half-Singleton bound for efficient linear codes over finite fields. We improve upon previous results by constructing explicit codes over Fq2 , linear over Fq, with rate 1/2 − δ − ε that can efficiently correct a δ-fraction of indel errors, where q = O(ε−4). Additionally, we construct fully linear codes over Fq with rate 1/2 − 2 √ δ − ε that can also efficiently correct δ-fraction of indels. These results significantly advance the study of linear codes for the indel model, bringing them closer to the theoretical half-Singleton bound. We also generalize the half-Singleton bound, for every code C ⊆ Fn linear over E ⊂ F a subfield of F, such that C has the ability to correct δ-fraction of indels, the rate is bounded by (1 − δ)/2.

title: Micron-level Precision Cavity Regulation and Active Noise Suppression Method for VC-OA-ICOS System Based on Re-entrant Order Recursion Theory
authors: Chen Chen, Yongyang Li, Youtong Li, Jingnan Cai, Yanguo Guo, Haozhi Xu, Wei Huang, Chunguang Li, Frank K. Tittel
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: In the off-axis integrated cavity output spectroscopy (OA-ICOS) technology, the suppression of cavity mode noise remains the primary challenge in enhancing the sensitivity of trace gas detection. The paper addressed two critical limitations in the existing OA-ICOS system: the absence of specific theoretical guidance for cavity parameter design and the reliance on empirical adjusting to suppress cavity mode noise. To overcome these limitations, we proposed a micron-level precision cavity regulation and active noise suppression based on reentrant order recursion theory method for OA-ICOS system. For the first time, the study elucidated the regulatory mechanism of the cavity length (CL) parameter on the number of re-entrant light spots and cavity mode density under constant curvature radius. The combined error between the theoretical CL obtained by the method and the actual optimal cavity length (AOCL) was less than 25 ± 10 μm, which was at least 40 times higher than the traditional millimeter level blind adjustment accuracy. Experimental results indicated that compared to the conventional full circle re-entrant method, the method reduced the standard deviation of raw signal noise approximately 10 times, thereby significantly improving the detection performance of system. The CH4 sensing system based on the method achieved a minimum detection limit (MDL) of 0.34 ppmv with an integration time of 69 s, while the vibration cavity (VC) OA-ICOS sensing system attained a MDL of 0.18 ppmv with an integration time of 59 s. The research not only addressed the theoretical gap regarding the influence of the re-entrant order on cavity mode noise, but also provides specific methodologies for the precise design and fine adjustment of cavity parameters in the OA-ICOS system.

title: Optical-flow-based Hybrid Neural Operator for Unsupervised Flow Estimation in Particle Image Velocimetry
authors: Yiye Zou, Shusheng Gu, Yu Zhang, Jingyu Wang, Xiaogang Deng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Particle image velocimetry is a widely-used experimental technique for measuring fluid flow, but accurate flow field estimation from particle images remains challenging. Traditional methods, such as cross-correlation and optical flow, are either resolution-limited or computationally expensive, and deep learning approaches often suffer from discretization errors, spectral bias, or a heavy reliance on labeled data. In this study, we propose the first framework that integrates operator learning with unsupervised optical-flow training by incorporating an optical-flow constraint. This formulation treats flow estimation as learning a solution operator from particle image pairs to continuous velocity fields. Comprehensive evaluations demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing approaches on publicly available synthetic data and achieves accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art optical-flow algorithms on real-world experimental data. Furthermore, the ablation studies demonstrate the effectiveness of our hybrid neural operator architecture. Finally, we demonstrate the zero-shot super-resolution capability, highlighting the potential of our approach to enhance PIV measurements by producing continuous flow representations across scales.

title: Generalized Multimodal BrainComputer Interfaces: From Neural Signal Sensing to Scenario-Adaptive Decoding
authors: Shiqiang Ma, Tengfei Xiao, Yanyan Shen, Yang Zhan, Baiying Lei, Michael Kwok-Po Ng, Shuqiang Wang
abstract: Fueled by concurrent advances in sensing and computing, braincomputer interfaces are evolving from single-electroencephalography modalities toward generalized fusion systems that synergistically integrate electrical, hemodynamic, optical, magnetic, and peripheral physiological signals. Flexible dry-electrode arrays and optically pumped magnetometers now deliver high spatio-temporal resolution within everyday wearable form factors; cross-modal attention and graph-network approaches unify millisecond-scale electrophysiology with second-scale hemodynamics at the feature level; decision-level and deeplearning frameworks further enable adaptive modality weighting and robust transfer. Building on these advances, we propose the concept of generalized fusion, emphasizing dynamic synergy among heterogeneous signals across the entire encodingdecodingfeedback loop, and discuss the emerging role of generative AI in completing missing modalities, balancing signal-to-noise trade-offs, and expanding demographic coverage. Finally, we summarize the distinct demands of medical rehabilitation, humancomputer interaction, and intelligent control scenarios on fusion accuracy and user load, highlighting that hardware comfort, algorithmic real-time capability, and multidimensional evaluation must be continually refined to translate multimodal braincomputer interfaces from laboratory validation into real-world deployment.

title: ThermoAct: Thermal-Aware Vision-Language-Action Models for Robotic Perception and Decision-Making
authors: Young-Chae Son, Dae-Kwan Ko, Yoon-Ji Choi, Soo-Chul Lim
abstract: In recent human-robot collaboration environments, there is a growing focus on integrating diverse sensor data beyond visual information to enable safer and more intelligent task execution. Although thermal data can be crucial for enhancing robot safety and operational efficiency, its integration has been relatively overlooked in prior research. This paper proposes a novel Vision-Language-Action (VLA) framework that incorporates thermal information for robot task execution. The proposed system leverages a Vision-Language Model (VLM) as a high-level planner to interpret complex natural language commands and decompose them into simpler sub-tasks. This approach facilitates efficient data collection and robust reasoning for complex operations. Unlike conventional methods that rely solely on visual data, our approach integrates thermal information, enabling the robot to perceive physical properties and proactively ensure environmental safety. Experimental results from real-world task scenarios validate the feasibility of our proposed framework, suggesting its potential to enhance task success rates and safety compared to existing vision-based systems.

title: A Prototype of 20GHz-Low-Complexity Digital Beamforming Transmitter Antenna Module For FR3 Band Using Direct Digital RF Technology in DSoF Configuration
authors: Junhao Zhang, Koki Furuuchi, Noriharu Suematsu
abstract: To miniaturize the digital beamforming (DBF) transmitter module, 1-bit bandpass delta-sigma (BP-DS) direct digital RF transmitter using delta-sigma-over-fiber (DSoF) link by 10GE SFP (Small Form Factor Pluggable) optical module has been studied. However, because transmitter module and antenna module are individual and SFP optical module can only transmit one channel’s signal, it is difficult to scale it up massive multiple-input-multiple-output (M-MIMO) application. In this paper, by using array-antenna-integrated transmitter module and 100GE QSFP28 (Quad Small Form-factor Pluggable) optical module, we propose a 4-element 20GHz-band low-complexity DBF transmitter antenna module using DSoF for scalable fully digital M-MIMO application in FR3 band, which is at high super high frequency (SHF) band. The original RF signal whose center frequency is 6.45 GHz, about quarter of sampling frequency (25.78 Gbps), is modulated to 1-bit signal by 1-bit BP-DS modulator in Matlab. The 1-bit signal is generated by pulse pattern generator (PPG). Then, the 1-bit electrical signal is converted to optical signal by a standardized QSFP28 module and transmitted by fiber to remote radio heads (RRH). The RRH consists of a QSFP28 module, balun, bandpass filter, power amplifier and integrated array antenna. In RRH, after optical signal is converted to 1-bit electrical signal by QSFP28 module, its first image component whose center frequency is 19.33 GHz is extracted, amplified, and then fed to array antenna. The DBF transmitter performance is evaluated by over-the-air (OTA) measurement across 1 m wireless distance. In single beam measurement for multiple-input-single-output (MISO) communication, the measured result shows an EVM of 3.4% in worst case at 200 Msym/s 64 quadrature amplitude modulation (200M-64QAM) scheme. In dual beam measurement for multi-user MIMO (MU-MIMO) communication, there are two beams generated simultaneously and pointed to different directions. The measured result shows an EVM of 4.5% for both two users in the same 200M-64QAM scheme.

title: Joint Phase Noise and Channel Estimation Technique for DFT-s-OFDM in Sub-THz band
authors: Geunho Kim, Donghwan Jang, Kae Won Choi
abstract: In the 6G era, communication systems must accommodate extremely high data rates, steering attention toward the sub-terahertz (sub-THz) band, which can offer substantially wider bandwidth than lower-frequency bands. However, key challenges in sub-THz communications include high path loss, limited RF efficiency, and especially phase noise from local oscillator (LO) imperfections. Although DFT-s-OFDM effectively mitigate the high peak-to-average power ratio (PAPR) of CP-OFDM, research on phase noise compensation for this waveform is limited. Specifically, to the best of our knowledge, while joint channel and phase noise estimation has been investigated for CP-OFDM, it remains unexplored in the context of DFT-s-OFDM. This paper proposes the first iterative joint estimation framework for DFT-s-OFDM that exploits both frequency-domain and time-domain reference signals. The proposed method alternately refines channel and phase noise estimates, creating a virtuous cycle where accurate channel estimation improves phase noise tracking and vice versa. We propose least squares (LS) and linear minimum mean squared error (LMMSE) methods to reduce computational complexity without sacrificing performance. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed LS and LMMSE methods achieve normalized mean squared error (NMSE) improvements of 12.44 dB and 12.17 dB in channel estimation, and 15.15 dB and 14.24 dB in phase noise estimation, respectively. Consequently, these gains translate to bit-error-rate (BER) reductions from 1.03 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−2</sup> to 4.13 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−3</sup> for the LS scheme and 4.48 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−3</sup> for the LMMSE scheme at an signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of 40 dB. Additionally, a 140 GHz sub-THz prototype system validates these gains under realistic channel and phase noise conditions, demonstrating the practical viability of the proposed algorithms.
